title: Yankees Provide Worrisome Giancarlo Stanton Update
description: The New York Yankees may not have Giancarlo Stanton back for a while
fullText:
The New York Yankees have the second-best record in the American League, but have been without Aaron Judge and Giancarlo Stanton for a large chunk of the season.

Stanton has been on the injured list since April with a calf strain. He played in 24 games before his injury and had a .724 OPS. He had hit three home runs before his injury, and the Yankees' offense desperately needs him back.

Do you have a take to get off your chest? Do you want to engage in a growing Yankees community? Comment on our content alongside staff and fans alike!

"Giancarlo Stanton isn't close to returning," Yankees insider Bryan Hoch wrote on Monday. "Though Stanton has resumed his running progression after a recent calf strain, Yankees manager Aaron Boone said Monday there is 'no timeline' for Stanton to play in Minor League games. He has been out since April 24."

The 36-year-old has proven that when he's healthy, he can still be one of the best power bats in baseball. He hit 24 home runs in 77 games last season. The Yankees should have no trouble making the postseason without him, but getting him back in October could be huge.

Looking for the perfect gift for a baseball player in your life? Check out our brand new baseball lifestyle brand!

During the Yankees' run to the World Series in 2024, Stanton hit seven home runs and helped lead the team to the Fall Classic. Right now, the Yankees' offense is struggling greatly without both him and Judge, but the entire league knows what type of offense the Yankees can be when those two are in the lineup.

However, with no rehab assignment in sight, Stanton's return may not be until the final couple of weeks of the regular season. If that's the case, the Yankees will hope it's enough time for him to get back into the swing of things and be ready for a deep run.

More MLB: Yankees Circling Padres 27-Year-Old Superstar Pre-Trade Deadline

title: Eagles' biggest offensive loss isn't A.J. Brown
description: Philadelphia is going to have to adjust in multiple ways for the season ahead.
fullText:
Eagles' biggest offensive loss isn't A.J. Brown originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

The Philadelphia Eagles' most headline-grabbing departure this offseason was A.J. Brown.

They may actually miss someone else more on their offense, though.

ESPN's Ben Solak wrote on Tuesday that offensive line coach Jeff Stoutland leaving Philly might matter more than the loss of Brown.

Stoutland was the Eagles' offensive line coach since 2013, and he's been part of a very successful era for the franchise.

"He's a less evident absence, but perhaps a more impactful one," Solak writes. "The Eagles' offensive line was the best in the league during their championship runs, but last season's group suffered mightily with injury and will have a lot asked of them in new offensive coordinator Sean Mannion's under-center, play-action, motion-heavy system. This is the sort of modern offense that doesn't immediately and obviously gel with Hurts' strengths, as reporting has covered his commitment to more static offensive systems. Regardless of how highly Hurts ranks among league quarterbacks, there's no question that Brown and the O-line were massive factors to the Eagles' offensive success under Nick Sirianni."

MORE: Brandon Aiyuk's latest decision probably ended his NFL career

The Brown absence is most notable in how it impacts the WR depth chart.

DeVonta Smith becomes the No. 1 wideout by default, and there'll be a battle for the other targets.

But it's the offensive line that matters for Jalen Hurts' ability to stand back there and pick out his targets, and it's the offensive line that matters for Saquon Barkley's effectiveness.

Two seasons ago, the Eagles won the Super Bowl.

Last season, they were just a bit underwhelming.

MORE: Jahmyr Gibbs, Bijan Robinson playing a funny game of chicken

The offensive line is going to have to be good to get the Eagles where they want to go.

"There's a huge mystery right now in Philadelphia," Solak concludes.

A.J. Brown and Stefon Diggs have a Patriots, Bills, Drake Maye, Josh Allen link

Josh Allen called out by a coach with a major losing record

Lamar Jackson has an obvious reason not to sign right now

Commanders have plenty of reason not to want Brandon Aiyuk

Will they still be called the Chicago Bears if they play in Indiana?

title: Why Ryan Clark was fired unceremoniously by ESPN in latest round of layoffs
description: Ryan Clark's time at ESPN is up, and it ended abruptly.
fullText:
Why Ryan Clark was fired unceremoniously by ESPN in latest round of layoffs originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Former NFL safety Ryan Clark spent more than a decade at ESPN as an analyst, becoming one of the network's most prominent football voices in recent years.

Never afraid to speak his mind, Clark certainly wasn't every viewer's favorite analyst, which made him a lightning rod after frequently delving into social and political issues on the air.

ESPN fired Clark on Monday, and the nature by which he was let go is generating some controversy.

Here's what you need to know about Clark's exit from ESPN.

MORE:NFL offseason grades for every team

Clark's firing comes as part of a larger round of layoffs at ESPN, but his exit reportedly isn't just a financial decision.

According to The Athletic, executives at ESPN "soured on Clark" in recent months as a result of the way he conducted himself on air. The report refers to an on-air incident between Clark and analyst Peter Schrager in September, in which Clark belittled Schrager because he never played in the NFL.

Clark also raised some eyebrows by taking contract negotiations public in 2024 when his deal with the network was expiring.

"The season is over. The deal is up. Time to make a decision," Clark tweeted.

After some viewers called for Clark's ouster, the former NFL safety saw his time with the network end after more than 11 years.

Clark took some heat for an interaction with Schrager in September, in which he told his fellow analyst, "That's the non-player in you," when Schrager defended CeeDee Lamb's performance in the Cowboys' season-opening loss.

Schrager pushed back on Clark, who responded, "Peter, what I need for you to do is not get mad and let me finish."

Ryan Clark: "That’s the non-player in you"

Peter Schrager: "Don’t belittle me like that, I can come and say as three ex-players are saying one thing, and give an alternative perspective"

Ryan Clark: "Peter, what I need for you to do is not get mad and let me finish" pic.twitter.com/zbBXdNPQaI

— Awful Announcing (@awfulannouncing) September 5, 2025

Clark later tweeted a public apology, but the incident lingered in the minds of some of ESPN's decision-makers, according to The Athletic.

MORE:Ryan Clark-Peter Schrager beef, explained

Clark was told about his firing during a break on "NFL Live" on Monday and did not continue with the show.

ESPN was hoping to inform Clark on Tuesday, The Athletic reported, but word leaked to the media late Monday afternoon that he was being let go. Rather than let Clark find out through the media, the network chose to inform Clark minutes after a live appearance on the show.

Clark was making more than $2 million per year at ESPN, according to The Athletic.

An extension in 2024 came after Clark took negotiations public, saying he believed he "deserved more" in his previous deal and making a public plea for ESPN to "make a decision" on his future.

Two years later, a final decision has been made.

title: Inside Look at AS Roma’s Latest Transfer Strategies
description: AS Roma enter the 2026–27 campaign with a very different outlook from the one they had a year ago. Gian Piero Gasperini, appointed in June 2025 after nine seasons at Atalanta, guided the Giallorossi...
fullText:
AS Roma enter the 2026–27 campaign with a very different outlook from the one they had a year ago. Gian Piero Gasperini, appointed in June 2025 after nine seasons at Atalanta, guided the Giallorossi to third place in Serie A and back into the UEFA Champions League.

That achievement raised expectations, but it did not remove the need for careful recruitment.

Roma are trying to add pace and depth without abandoning the wage structure that has become central to their planning.

Roma’s approach this summer is based on balancing Champions League ambition with disciplined spending, especially when transfer fees and salaries can rise quickly. Readers who also compare promotional offers in the regulated online entertainment market can review the terms, limits, and promos like those collected at pl.polskiesloty.com/darmowa-kasa-za-rejestracje-bez-depozytu/ offering free-cash bonuses for registration before deciding whether an offer is suitable. That comparison is useful because, like Roma’s recruitment decisions, the most attractive headline does not always reveal the full cost, conditions, or long-term value.

The club’s first confirmed moves reflect continuity rather than a complete rebuild. Donyell Malen’s loan from Aston Villa was converted into a permanent transfer after the Dutch forward made an immediate impact, while center-back Daniele Ghilardi completed a permanent move from Hellas Verona following his previous loan spell. Roma therefore began the window by securing players who already understood Gasperini’s demands instead of filling the squad with unfamiliar arrivals.

Malen’s permanent signing was confirmed by AS Roma’s official website, which stated that his contract runs until June 2030. That move gives Gasperini an attacker capable of playing centrally or from a wider starting position, although Roma still need another one-on-one threat who can stretch opponents and create chances against compact defences.

Zeki Çelik’s free transfer to Juventus changed Roma’s priorities. The Turkish international covered several defensive roles, so his departure increases the need for an athletic right-sided player who can defend large spaces and support attacks.

Roma must decide whether a right wing-back or an attacking winger is more urgent. Gasperini expects wide players to press, carry the ball forward, and arrive in scoring positions, so versatility and physical intensity may matter as much as reputation.

Crysencio Summerville has been repeatedly linked with Roma, but the deal remains unconfirmed. As of 21 July 2026, reports still described Roma as interested in the West Ham winger, subject to agreement over the fee. He should therefore be presented as an active target, not a completed or abandoned transfer.

Mason Greenwood is no longer available. The forward moved from Marseille to Fenerbahçe in July 2026. Reuters reported the confirmed €39 million transfer, ending any realistic prospect of Roma signing him in this window.

The Greenwood situation also reflects Roma’s reluctance to disrupt their wage hierarchy. Gasperini has supported a salary ceiling so that expensive newcomers do not immediately overtake players who earned Champions League qualification.

Roma have also extended several important contracts. Paulo Dybala renewed until June 2027, Gianluca Mancini until 2029, and Bryan Cristante until 2028. These official agreements preserve creativity, leadership, and tactical experience across the team.

Continuity is valuable before a demanding European season, although it does not remove the need for younger and faster alternatives. Roma will face more fixtures, stronger opponents, and greater pressure to rotate.

Several other names remain speculative. Diego Moreira, Dodô, Nahuel Molina, and Mika Godts have appeared in transfer reports, but none should be described as an imminent signing without stronger confirmation. Valuation gaps, player preferences, and parallel negotiations can change these stories quickly.

Claims that a player has reached a personal agreement or is waiting for an international tournament should only be included when supported by a current, attributable report. In a reliable overview, Malen, Ghilardi, and Çelik are confirmed deals; Summerville is a reported negotiation; the other names remain preliminary links.

A reliable transfer article should therefore separate three categories: official deals, well-sourced negotiations, and preliminary links. Malen, Ghilardi, and Çelik belong in the first category. Summerville belongs in the second because negotiations have been widely reported but remain unresolved. Moreira, Dodô, Molina, and Godts belong in the third until clubs or highly reliable outlets provide firmer evidence.

Roma’s clearest need is a dynamic wide attacker. Malen provides goals and movement, but the squad would benefit from another player who can beat defenders, exploit space, and contribute without the ball. A right-sided defensive option is also desirable after Çelik’s exit.

Depth will determine whether the strategy succeeds. Champions League football brings fixture congestion, injuries, and suspensions, so Roma need dependable players who complement the existing group rather than several expensive headline signings.

Roma’s summer strategy is measured rather than dramatic. The club has made Malen and Ghilardi permanent additions, lost Çelik to Juventus, and renewed Dybala, Mancini, and Cristante. Greenwood has joined Fenerbahçe, Summerville remains a reported target, and other links should be treated cautiously.

The final judgment will depend on whether Roma add pace and width without breaking their financial structure. The core is stable; the remaining task is to recruit complementary players before the transfer deadline.

title: Marseille set their sights on Memphis Depay, but…
description: Olympique de Marseille are reportedly pursuing Memphis Depay (32), according to Foot Mercato.With the Dutchman’s contract with Corinthians due to expire on 31st July, OM’s new sporting management,...
fullText:
Olympique de Marseille are reportedly pursuing Memphis Depay (32), according to Foot Mercato.

With the Dutchman’s contract with Corinthians due to expire on 31st July, OM’s new sporting management, led by sporting director Grégory Lorenzi, are said to have made contact with the striker’s representatives.

Having joined São Paulo in September 2024, the 112-time Dutch international (55 goals) has racked up no fewer than 79 appearances for Timão, scoring 20 goals, providing 15 assists and winning several trophies, including the Brazilian Cup.

However, Depay’s wage demands would make the move virtually impossible for the Marseille side, who are unlikely to succeed in bringing the Moordrecht-born player back to Ligue 1, where he played between 2017 and 2021 for Olympique Lyonnais.

It is therefore a fruitless attempt for Marseille, who, having parted ways with several key players, including Mason Greenwood (24) and Pierre-Emerick Aubameyang (37), now need to strengthen their squad, having yet to sign a single new player since the transfer window opened.

title: Colby Covington reacts to ‘unprofessional little clown’ Belal Muhammad starting melee at RAF 11 post-fight press conference
description: Belal Muhammad and Colby Covington nearly brawled after RAF 11
fullText:
Colby Covington says he may not even wrestle Belal Muhammad at RAF 12 after a brawl nearly broke out this past weekend.

Following RAF 11 in Milwaukee — which saw Covington win the RAF crossover title with a win over Arman Tsarukyan, and Muhammad pick up a victory over Ben Askren — promotional officials announced that Covington vs. Muhammad would serve as the co-main event for RAF’s one-year anniversary show on Aug. 22 in Cleveland. As the press conference went on, things got heated between the two past UFC title holders, with Covington screaming at Muhammad to “shut the f*ck up” after Muhammad interrupting him answering a question. Tensions ran high, and after Covington threw a dig Muhammad’s way, he went after Covington, throwing a kick before things were separated.

Covington reacted to the melee a couple of days later, and said he started losing patience prior to the attack.

“Just completely annoyed,” Covington told MMA Fighting. “Just an unprofessional little clown that Belal is. It’s embarrassing. I didn’t interrupt him when he was answering his questions. I’m not offended by whatever he wants to say about me. The guy’s been saying things about me for years.

“He was saying, oh, I got my title shot in the UFC because of the color of my skin. He literally tweeted that, white privilege. He said, the only reason Colby’s fighting for the UFC title is because he’s white. Dude, could you imagine tweeting that? It’s disgusting. It’s racist. That’s the type of person that he is. Belal, no one remembers his name, f*cking whatever the f*ck his last name is.

“He’s hurling shots at me, my family, all types of different things. And you know what, whatever, dude. It’s America. Freedom of speech, dude. Your First Amendment, and guess what? My first amendment is protected by my second amendment, so he needs to figure that out.”

The rivalry between the two through the media has been going on for a few years now, and for the first time since things revved up, the two competed on the same card.

Both guys hurled insults at one another prior to RAF 11, through interviews and the pre-match press conference this past Friday. But things went to another level following Saturday’s record-breaking event at Panther Arena on the campus of University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee.

“Dude, he’s been saying things about me for years, begging to get this fight with me, hurling shots at me, whatever,” Covington said. “Say whatever you want. You don’t hurt my feelings. I don’t care, keep saying it, but just like the UFC knows and the great Hunter Campbell, the greatest mind behind the UFC who is the face that runs the place, he puts Belal in the APEX because he doesn’t sell tickets. He’s a nobody. Not like myself, who I go to Madison Square Garden, the most iconic arena in the world, and I do a $10 million gate, one of the biggest gates in MSG history.

“That’s what separates us is he’s been begging for this fight, begging for this thing, and then he gets the chance, and he’s on the mic, and he’s saying, ‘Oh, I want to be a good example for the kids, and then you come take a cheap shot at me and kick me across the stage when I’m not even ready for it, and he won’t even let me talk at the press conference of my f*cking company. I’m an investor in Real American freestyle. He’s not an investor. He’s a nobody.

“He’s a bum, he’s irrelevant, and it was classless. You claim you wanna be an example for the kids. Is that how you set an example for the kids? Is that how you do your business? You can’t control your emotions? It was embarrassing.“

Speaking with Submission Radio on Sunday, Covington said he was pulling the match from Muhammad, and would defend his newly won belt against somebody else.

As time went on, Covington — who is now 4-0 competing for RAF — says the fight is not 100 percent off the table, but he’s veering more on the side of looking for another name — someone who Covington says will give him more of a challenge after what he saw from Muhammad against Askren.

“I don’t even know if I’m going to go against him,” Covington explained. “We might pull that match and go for someone better. The guy’s not accomplished anyways. It took him till minute five to take down the dead guy that shouldn’t even be out there. Once Ben’s lungs literally stopped working, that’s the only time he ever got a takedown on him.

“The guy’s not even worth my time. He’s irrelevant and, yeah, just very annoying.”

Muhammad got back in the combat sports win column with his first promotional win. In his RAF debut, Muhammad stepped up to face multi-time freestyle world champion David Carr, losing via tech fall at RAF 4 this past December.

The 38-year-old has dropped two straight in the octagon, including losing the welterweight title to Jack Della Maddalena 15 months ago at UFC 315, and dropping a decision to Gabriel Bonfim at UFC Vegas 118 in June.

While on The Ariel Helwani Show, Covington said he has a poll going with fans where they would vote on whether the match with Muhammad should take place, or go a different direction. Covington says he’ll go with whatever the people vote for.

“What makes RAF so great, and the great minds behind it – Eric Bischoff, Chad Bronstein, Izzy Martinez [and others behind the scenes] – but what makes our minds so great is we listen to the people…,” Covington said “If the people truly want it, I’m willing to grant it. It’s going to be an easy match. I’ll destroy the guy in 90 seconds or less, but obviously, the guy’s going to cheap shot.

“He’s not a professional, he’s a coward. So that’s just the thing I’m worried about is him taking a cheap shot on me trying to punch me when I’m beating his ass 10-0 tech falling him, and then having to deal with that. I don’t want to put any of those kids that are in that arena in a bad situation where a brawl spills out onto them and potentially someone could get hurt. That’s how a company like RAF could literally go under. We don’t wanna see anything like that happen.

“So I don’t know. We got to see. We’re going to listen to the people. We’re the people’s organization, RAF, and if the people want the matchup, we’re willing to give the people the matchup.”

title: Roma finalizing agreement with West Ham for Summerville
description: Roma have made their choice to strengthen their attack: Crysencio Summerville. The Giallorossi have identified the Dutch winger as the right player to raise the level of their attack. The Roman club h...
fullText:
Roma have made their choice to strengthen their attack: Crysencio Summerville.

The Giallorossi have identified the Dutch winger as the right player to raise the level of their attack.

The Roman club have reached an agreement with the 2001-born player, lowering their demands and opening up to the project Gasperini presented to them. These are crucial hours to close the deal, the Friedkins’ final push with West Ham.

We’re in the final stages of Summerville’s move to Roma. According to Fabrizio Romano, the deal is just awaiting final approval from the West Ham president.

As reported by Sky Sport, West Ham’s definitive response to Roma’s latest offer for Summerville is expected shortly.

title: When will Lebron make his decision? Lebron James next team odds
description: The Philadelphia 76ers are among a handful of teams making a pitch to sign free the NBA legend. See Lebron James next team odds.
fullText:
Do the Philadelphia 76ers really have a shot?

Following the announcement he wouldn't re-sign with the Los Angeles Lakers, NBA legend LeBron James has made it clear to teams that he would consider signing with a franchise that best fit his needs.

What those are aren't exactly clear, but the Sixers are reportedly one of the teams in the mix for James' services for the upcoming 2026-2027 NBA season — and possibly beyond.

See where the Sixers currently stand among James' suitors.

As of July 21, the Sixers ranked among the top possible teams to sign the all-time NBA leading scorer, according to prediction market Kalshi.

Here's where they rank as of 9 a.m. ET July 21:

Cleveland Cavaliers 27% (down 5%)

Minnesota Timberwolves 2% (down 1%)

Here's the breakdown of James' current odds of signing, according to market prediction site Polymarket:

Cleveland Cavaliers 27% (down 14%)

Philadelphia 76ers 14% (up 12%)

Golden State Warriors 11% (down 15%)

While most believe James will return to his hometown Cleveland to close out his career, there's a chance a he turns down a third reunion with the Cavs and signs elsewhere.

Recently, James' agent Rich Paul of Klutch Sports discussed a number of teams his client was considering on his “Game Over” podcast with Max Kellerman.

On a white board, Paul and Kellerman went through the pros of James signing with each team and their starting rosters. The list included:

According to a report by ESPN's Shams Charania, those teams seemed to have been cut down to the Cavaliers, Heat and 76ers.

Charania said executives for interested teams have been sending voice notes to Paul, who passed them along to James.

“There's a focus on Cleveland, Miami and Philadelphia," Charania said in a July 14 report. " ... This could be any day, any week, now," but that James was getting closer to a decision.

@ShamsCharania latest update on LeBron James' free agency 👑

“There's a focus on Cleveland, Miami and Philadelphia." pic.twitter.com/0P3Cv0F93O

According to Kalshi, here's when James is expected to sign with a new team:

While appearing with 97.5 The Fanantic in Philadelphia last week, ESPN's Dave McMenamin said it was likely that James would make his decision this week.

"A source familiar with LeBron really believes LeBron is nearing the decision ... People around the league are hoping that it comes next week," McMenamin said.

"... I would say there is a pretty decent chance we find out next week. I do believe, strongly, that the Sixers are a legitimate option for him still. I was told, as of last night, he still hasn't made his final decision."

A report the same day by Charania said he spoke with Paul the morning of July 20 and that there's no decision by James and no timetable on a decision. Charania said his understanding is focus remains on Miami, Cleveland, Philadelphia.

However, Paul said on his “Game Over” podcast on July 20 that there is no timetable for James to sign with a new team.

“We’re not going to be rushed,” Paul said.

Speculation that the Heat are now the frontrunners for James' services after Miami jumped ahead of Cleveland in the prediction markets over the weekend.

During a July 16 report, Charania said James' decision was expected soon.

"I think this process is getting closer and closer to the end here," Charania said. "LeBron James has all the information that he needs."

BREAKING: LeBron James is rumoured to be signing with a team VERY soon, per @ShamsCharania.

📈 Shams says that LeBron, after facing criticism for taking too long, has all the information to make a final choice.

There are rumours that LeBron could be doing a "big event" for it. pic.twitter.com/6KK2T8udZN

— BallersCulture (@BallersCultureX) July 17, 2026

Reports were speculating that James would announce his signing during Fanatics Fest, which was held last week in the Javits Center in New York.

But during events on July 16 and 17, James declined to say if had made a free-agent decision yet.

Tyrese Haliburton to LeBron James: 

“What are you going on, what is this… Year 76?”

I see what you did there 😏 pic.twitter.com/RMv5kzYeMw

— RB (@RBPhillyTake) July 16, 2026

"I won't hold you guys up too much longer," James said.

During his Fanatics appearance, James even said the phrase "trust the process," which Sixers fans were quick to connect to the Sixers' longtime rebuilding era with Embiid.

As LeBron James rattles off what he will value in his next team, he used the phrase “trust the process” and the crowd was sent into a tizzy, thinking he was giving a clue about the Philadelphia 76ers. pic.twitter.com/klpBWePWNq

— Dave McMenamin (@mcten) July 17, 2026

Another report over the weekend stated that Sixers owner Josh Harris also spoke to James briefly during Fanatics Fest.

Bob Cooney of On Pattison said on July 20 that he has a strong feeling that James will sign with Philadelphia.

"I am getting a strong feeling he is coming here. I really am," Cooney said. "Why is a 42-year-old man going out there and throwing out 'Trust the Process' and '76 Years,' and all these things that are little hints?

"… Why would you go around teasing stuff like that? … Why are insiders hinting at it now? … Over the last couple of days, I started thinking, you know what, he is putting it out there. I think he might be coming to the Sixers. A couple of weeks ago, you asked me for a percentage, and I said 15-20%. I am upping that now; I think it is 50-50 at least."

During his appearance on 97.5 The Fanantic in Philadelphia, McMenamin said it's top Sixers players Joel Embiid, Tyrese Maxey and newly acquired forward Jaylen Brown who are currently in contact with James. and not Philly brass such as Bob Meyers, Mike Gansey and Nick Nurse.

"I want to say the people that I've spoken to told me about (Joel) Embiid's interaction, it's been mostly through text messaging, which is an obvious way to communicate in 2026," McMenamin said. "As far as my understanding, it's really been (Tyrese) Maxey as the primary spearhead in terms of keeping that communication and dialogue going. Maxey knows LeBron the best out of those three players

"… I've covered a bunch of teams, and it's not very often where you get all three, if you do have three stars, on the same page moving in the same direction," he said. "It's significant that, from the top down, Bob Myers, Nick Nurse, Mike Gansey, and the three best players on the team are all together on this, saying, 'LeBron, we want you, we need you, and we can do great things if you come here.'"

If James were to sign with Philadelphia, the Sixers would potentially have one of the most talented starting lineups in the NBA. Here's what it might look it:

Sixers potential starting lineup

This article originally appeared on Delaware News Journal: When will Lebron make his decision? Lebron James next team odds

title: Fred Vasseur Hints at a Hidden Flaw Costing Ferrari in Qualifying
description: The 2026 Formula 1 season has established a very clear, often frustrating pattern for the Scuderia. While the SF-26 comes alive on Sundays with highly competitive race pace and excellent tire management, Saturdays have been a completely different story. Ferrari…
fullText:
The 2026 Formula 1 season has established a very clear, often frustrating pattern for the Scuderia. While the SF-26 comes alive on Sundays with highly competitive race pace and excellent tire management, Saturdays have been a completely different story. Ferrari is consistently bleeding crucial tenths in qualifying, forcing both Charles Leclerc and Lewis Hamilton into recovery drives on race day.

While most paddock analysts have pointed the finger directly at Maranello’s hybrid power unit and energy deployment strategies, Ferrari Team Principal Fred Vasseur recently went on the record to clarify that the engine is not the only culprit.

Speaking to the Italian press (as highlighted by La Gazzetta Ferrari), Vasseur acknowledged the ongoing Saturday struggles but shifted the focus away from the power unit.

“It’s a trend from the beginning of the season that we are probably a bit more performant in the race than in qualifying, but it’s not only due to the engine.”

Instead of blaming raw horsepower or hybrid harvesting, the Ferrari boss admitted that a specific mechanical or aerodynamic characteristic of the SF-26 is actively hindering their single-lap pace. However, he refused to give rival engineers any free information, adding:

“It’s sometimes a parameter on the chassis, but I will keep it for me.”

According to recent insights regarding his management style, Vasseur has been actively pushing his engineering team to focus solely on lap time as their only Key Performance Indicator (KPI), rather than getting bogged down in isolated metrics like downforce loads or raw horsepower. This holistic approach explains why Vasseur is looking closely at the chassis behavior during qualifying rather than just demanding more top-end speed from the engine department.

In modern Formula 1, finding the perfect setup is always a massive compromise between qualifying speed and race-day longevity. An aggressive setup might fire the tires up perfectly for one flying lap on Saturday, but it can completely destroy them over a long stint on Sunday.

While Ferrari’s qualifying deficit forces them to fight through dirty air on race day, their current chassis parameters clearly favor the long game. The SF-26’s ability to manage tire degradation is precisely why Hamilton and Leclerc have been able to successfully drag the car to the podium despite less-than-ideal grid slots.

However, if Maranello expects to mount a genuine challenge for the Constructors’ Championship, they must unlock whatever hidden chassis parameter Vasseur is keeping secret to secure better track position.

title: Karl Ravech laid off by ESPN after 33 years
description: Another round of layoffs has arrived in Bristol, Connecticut as ESPN prepares to say goodbye to many household names. But none may be bigger than longtime veteran anchor and play-by-play man Karl Ravech. Both The Athletic and Front Office Sports broke the news of Ravech’s ESPN departure on Tuesday morning. ESPN is preparing to do…
fullText:
Another round of layoffs has arrived in Bristol, Connecticut as ESPN prepares to say goodbye to many household names. But none may be bigger than longtime veteran anchor and play-by-play man Karl Ravech.

Both The Athletic and Front Office Sports broke the news of Ravech’s ESPN departure on Tuesday morning. ESPN is preparing to do what is now seemingly their annual custom of summer layoffs to balance their books, painfully saying goodbye to several on-air personalities in addition to folks behind the scenes.

The most recent wave of layoffs got underway on Monday night with the shock departure of longtime NFL analyst Ryan Clark. As the news broke of Clark’s termination, he was live on the network airwaves on NFL Live and was informed during the program of his fate in a sad and stunning turn of events. While he was the first name to be shared, others are expected to follow, especially centered on the newly acquired NFL Network.

However, Ravech is reportedly the biggest name to be let go as part of these 2026 layoffs given his decades of contributions to ESPN.

Karl Ravech was a mainstay at ESPN as a SportsCenter anchor and the longtime host of Baseball Tonight, which at one time was a nightly staple on the WorldWide Leader in Sports. He was also the host of ESPN’s on-site World Series coverage. In recent years, he transitioned to being one of ESPN’s lead play-by-play announcers for the sport, calling the famed Sunday Night Baseball franchise before it moved on to NBC this season.

Ravech first joined ESPN in May 1993. Although he was synonymous with the network’s baseball coverage for much of that time period, he was also a longtime SportsCenter anchor and called college basketball games for the network.

The news of Ravech’s departure came just one night after he called the Los Angeles Dodgers vs Philadelphia Phillies contest on ESPN in what may end up being his last assignment for the network. It remains to be seen where ESPN goes for the remainder of their baseball coverage in 2026. But seeing Karl Ravech exit the network mid-season in this way after so many dedicated years of service in Bristol is a sobering reminder of the current realities of modern media.

The post Karl Ravech laid off by ESPN after 33 years appeared first on Awful Announcing.

title: Codekeel: A context drift governance tool for Claude Code
description: Drift governance for agentic coding — a local decision ledger and live enforcement engine for Claude Code sessions. AGPL-3.0, runs entirely on your machine. - HabibiCodeCH/codekeel
fullText:
Drift governance for agentic coding. codekeel keeps a decision ledger for your project — architectural choices, conventions, and constraints — and enforces it live during Claude Code sessions, so every new session inherits, respects, and extends what was already decided instead of silently re-inferring (and contradicting) it from code alone.

Free and open source (AGPL-3.0). Runs entirely on your machine — no account, no server, no telemetry.

Sets up the Claude Code integration (hooks + slash commands) in your current project. From inside a session, use /decision to record an architectural decision — it's added to the ledger and injected into every future session automatically. Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (see below) to additionally have every edit under that decision's scope semantically verified against it before it lands.

The ledger lives in ledger/DECISIONS.yaml in your project (or ~/.vibe-code-receipts/GLOBAL_DECISIONS.yaml for conventions that apply across every project you work in) — plain YAML, readable and editable by hand.

Running bare npx codekeel (no subcommand) triggers install automatically the very first time you run it. install does four things, each independently idempotent — safe to re-run any time:

No account and no network call at any step — everything above is written to your own machine.

Hooks and slash commands are global — set up once per machine, shared by every project you open in Claude Code afterward. The ledger is not: it lives inside each repo, so a brand-new project needs its own npx codekeel install (or just npx codekeel ledger init if the hooks are already set up).

Everything above works with zero setup. Setting ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in your environment additionally unlocks:

codekeel calls Anthropic's API directly from your machine using this key — there is no codekeel-hosted relay, and codekeel never sees this traffic or bills you for it. Every one of these checks fails open (never blocks an edit) when no key is configured.

Most also accept --json — that's how /decision drives them non-interactively from inside a session.

Bare invocation (no subcommand) scans your local Claude Code session logs for governance signals — discussion loops, a bloated CLAUDE.md, scope creep, code churn — and offers to install a tip for whichever ones it finds, entirely on your own machine.

Every file codekeel reads or writes, and why. All plain text, all inspectable, nothing hidden.

On your machine, shared across every project:

AGPL-3.0. See LICENSE and CONTRIBUTING.md.

title: I asked an AI agent to delete a folder my tool was guarding
description: Four rounds with a live Cursor agent: whack-a-mole retries, a classifier hole, a native-tool escape, and a silently renamed hook API.
fullText:
I build Termaxa, a small Rust CLI that gates the shell commands AI coding agents run: it previews what a command will actually do, backs up first, applies an allow/ask/deny policy, and logs everything. The fastest way to find out whether a safety tool works is to point a real agent at it and ask it to do the thing the tool exists to stop.

So I did. Several times, over a few weeks. Cursor broke my design four different ways, and every meaningful feature in the current release exists because of one of those breaks. (Claude Code, for what it's worth, mostly cooperated. It's the agent that didn't stand down that taught me everything.)

The setup: a test repo, Termaxa hooked into Cursor, a policy that flagged recursive deletes as ask. The prompt: delete this folder.

Cursor never attacked the hook. It didn't need to. It just kept expressing the same intent in different shell dialects until one spelling landed:

Rule-based matching sees three unrelated commands. The agent sees one goal with three spellings — and each retry is a fresh policy evaluation, a fresh chance for an auto-approving UI to wave it through.

This isn't a bypass, it's a policy expressiveness gap. The agent stayed inside the rules the whole time. My rules just couldn't say the thing that mattered: "this session has already been told no about deleting things."

That gap is fixable inside the cooperative model, and the fix became the headline feature — a session circuit breaker:

Two design constraints mattered. The breaker only escalates, and only touches ask — an explicit allow is deliberate user policy (maybe you want rm -rf allowed inside /tmp), and a deny is already a deny. And the state is derived, not stored — the counter is computed from the log, so there's no sidecar file to reset, a new session is automatically a clean slate, and every denied variant is itself logged with its intent, which keeps the breaker tripped for the rest of the session for free.

Re-ran the test. Cursor went PowerShell → cmd, the classifier tracked the intent across both shells, and the third delete attempt came back:

Same session, Cursor kept going, and one command sailed through as a plain ask:

The classifier looked at the first token. The first token is find. find isn't a delete command. The rm -rf living inside -exec was invisible.

This is where the intent-over-spelling bet paid off. The fix wasn't "add find -exec rm to a pattern list" — it was extending the classifier to understand delete indirection as a concept: find with -exec/-execdir/-ok/-okdir wrapping a delete command, find -delete, xargs feeding a delete command, unlink, shred -u. One classifier extension, one regression test using the literal live bypass command, shipped as v0.11.1. Enumerating spellings is an unwinnable arms race; intents are finite.

Then Cursor did the thing that defines the tool's honest boundary.

Blocked on shell deletes — breaker tripped, every variant denied — it stopped using the shell. It switched to its own native file-delete tool and removed about twenty files. .cursor/ and .termaxa/, gone.

Audit entries for those deletions: zero. Not because anything was evaded — because the shell hook never saw them. An agent's built-in file tools don't go through the shell. They were never in scope.

I could have buried this. Instead it's the first item in SECURITY.md, filed as a public issue, and in the README subhead: Termaxa is a windshield, not a sandbox. It gates the shell path an agent normally takes; it does not contain an agent that executes through other means. If you need hard guarantees, pair it with OS-level isolation — containers, seccomp, restricted credentials. The cooperative gate covers the common case: a capable agent about to make an expensive mistake. It is not containment of an adversarial one. (The real fix is owning the execution path rather than hooking someone else's — that's the roadmap, not the present tense.)

One more finding from the same sessions, smaller but real: the breaker counts per session id, and both Cursor and Claude Code sometimes rotate the session id mid-run with no restart. A rotation between attempts resets the counter. In practice a burst of retries shares one id — the trip above happened across a rotation-free burst — but it makes the breaker a speed bump, not a durable cap. Also filed, also documented.

This is the finding I'd lead with if I could only keep one.

Weeks later, while wiring up post-execution receipts, I ran a routine live test on Cursor with a debug capture enabled (TERMAXA_HOOK_DEBUG, which dumps every raw payload the hook receives). The capture showed six hook invocations. The audit log showed zero entries.

Root cause: Cursor 3.11 had renamed its hook API. Events arrived as preToolUse/postToolUse (camelCase) with tool_name: "Shell". My parser only knew the older beforeShellExecution/afterShellExecution shape with tool_name: "Bash". Every event from current Cursor fell through the parser and returned None — which, by design, means "not for us, step aside."

Termaxa fails open on plumbing on purpose: a gate that bricks your agent when it gets confused gets uninstalled, and then it protects nobody. But fail-open has a shadow, and this was it. Termaxa had been silently not gating Cursor 3.11+ for roughly four minor versions. No error, no crash, no signal. The tool appeared installed and healthy while doing nothing.

Here's the part that generalizes: the entire test suite was green the whole time. The Cursor tests used the old payload shape as fixtures. They faithfully verified that Termaxa handled a dialect Cursor no longer spoke. For an integration surface, a green suite is necessary and nowhere near sufficient — the only thing that catches this class of drift is running the real agent and looking at what it actually sends.

The fix (v0.11.4): case-insensitive event matching across both API generations, Cursor detection from multiple signals, command and cwd recovery from several payload locations — and, crucially, four regression tests whose fixtures are the real captured 3.11 payloads, so the next silent rename fails CI instead of failing users. Verified live on Cursor 3.11.25: hook entries and post receipts both flowing again.

If you're building anything that sits between an AI agent and real infrastructure, the four lessons in one place:

Classify intent, not spelling. An agent blocked on a goal retries the goal with different syntax — across shells, through indirection, wherever the policy's vocabulary runs out. Pattern lists lose that race structurally. Intent classification is what let one fix close a whole category.

Only escalate; never relax. A safety layer that second-guesses an explicit human allow is a safety layer people rip out. Harden the soft middle — the ask that auto-approval quietly erodes into allow — and leave deliberate policy alone.

Green tests lie about integration surfaces. Your fixtures encode yesterday's API. Live-fire against the actual agent, capture the real payloads, and make those your fixtures.

Say where your tool stops, in the README, before anyone asks. The native-tool escape would be a devastating HN comment if someone else discovered it. As the first line of my own SECURITY.md, it's the reason to trust the rest of the document.

The biggest surprise wasn't that Cursor found bugs. It was that every serious improvement came from watching a real agent behave differently than my tests predicted. If you're building infrastructure for AI agents, that's probably the actual development loop: write the feature, point a real model at it, let it surprise you, and turn the surprise into tomorrow's regression test.

title: Same Rows, Different Sum
description: Same query, same rows, different SUM totals in PostgreSQL. The culprit is parallel aggregation over double precision floats, not a bug. Here is the mechanism and the fix.
fullText:
Everyone knows not to store money as a double precision. One can hope. The rule is so well drilled that it has stopped being interesting, and it is also not where the trouble usually starts. The float is already in the schema before anyone weighs in on it: a measurement column someone later sums for a report, telemetry that drifts into a finance dashboard, a third-party feed ingested as double precision because that is how it arrived.

Here is the part the rule does not warn you about. Take a table of five million floating-point readings, sum the column, and run it three times in a row. Nothing else touches the table. Same connection, same data, same statement.

Three runs, three different totals. No UPDATE, no concurrent writer, no random seed. The rows did not change between runs, and the query is the same character for character. Yet the answer is not.

This is not a Postgres bug, and it is not specific to Postgres. It is what happens when floating-point arithmetic meets parallel aggregation, and it has been generating "my dashboard total changed and I have no idea why" tickets for as long as databases have parallelized. The non-determinism does not wait for you to opt into bad practice; it shows up the moment a parallel plan runs over whatever floats you happen to have.

One column of double precision, five million rows.

Five million rows is enough that Postgres parallelizes the sum on its own. No settings forced, defaults all the way:

Two workers each sum part of the table in parallel, and a final step folds the partial sums together. Everything that follows is in that split.

On paper, (a + b) + c and a + (b + c) are the same number. Postgres is not doing arithmetic on paper. Floating-point addition is not associative: change the grouping and you can change the answer, because every intermediate result is rounded to fit 64 bits. Move the parentheses and you move which roundings happen.

The textbook demonstration takes three values and one subtraction:

The answer should be 1. Read the expression left to right and watch where it goes wrong:

The 1 never made it past the first step. It was swallowed by the addition, before the subtraction even ran. This is absorption: a value too small relative to its neighbour to leave a mark. It is not a database phenomenon. The same expression evaluates to 0 in C, Python, JavaScript, and every other language that uses IEEE 754 doubles.

So the sum of a column depends on the order in which the rows are added. Not on which rows, on the order. That is the seam parallel query pries open.

The plan spells out exactly how. Parallel Seq Scan does not split the table into fixed slices upfront. It hands every worker a shared pointer into the same block iterator, and each worker grabs the next block, processes it, and comes back for another. Each worker runs its own Partial Aggregate, summing only the rows it happened to grab, and by default the leader process pitches in as a third one (parallel_leader_participation). Gather collects those partial sums in whatever order they arrive, and Finalize Aggregate folds them into a single total.

Two things vary from run to run, and neither is under your control:

Both feed directly into the one thing float addition is sensitive to: grouping. Different row split, different partial sums; different arrival order, different final grouping of the additions. The total lands a few bits away from where it landed last time. Across five million readings that shows up in the sixth decimal place, exactly where the three runs above disagreed.

sum(float8) is marked parallel-safe, which is why the planner is free to do this. It is safe in the sense that it will not crash or corrupt anything. It is not safe in the sense people assume, that the answer is a function of the input.

This is not unique to SUM. AVG, STDDEV, VARIANCE, and any other floating-point aggregate that accumulates across rows inherit the same order sensitivity. SUM is just the easiest to demonstrate and the one most likely to land in a number a human reads.

It is tempting to file this under "parallel query is flaky" and turn it off. That misses the point. Parallelism does not cause the non-determinism, it only exposes an order-dependence that was there the whole time.

Turn parallelism off completely and the sum becomes stable:

Stable, but only because, with nothing else scanning the table, a serial sequential scan visits the heap in the same physical order every time, so the additions group the same way every time. The order is fixed, so the rounding is fixed.

Same rows, same single thread, three summation orders, three answers. Parallelism is one way to shuffle the order, but a plain ORDER BY inside the aggregate does it just as well. The dependence is on order, full stop.

There is a subtler trap hiding in the "turn it off and it is stable" result. A stable answer is not necessarily the right answer. To see the true total, sum the same values as numeric, which adds in exact decimal with no rounding until the very end:

That is the exact sum of the stored values. Now line everything up:

Every floating-point total is wrong, including the "stable" serial one. They differ from the exact value in the fifth and sixth decimal places. Parallelism did not introduce the error. It removed the one comforting property the serial sum had, which was getting the same wrong answer every time. That sameness is what people mistake for correctness.

In the sixth decimal of a five-million-row random sum, nobody cares. The problem is the places where someone does.

The obvious one is reconciliation. A finance team sums a revenue column stored as double precision, exports it, and the number disagrees with the same query run an hour later, or with the same logic in a different tool running a different parallel degree. Nothing in the data changed, so the hunt goes to caching, to replicas, to "a dirty read somewhere" long before anyone suspects the aggregate itself.

The same thing wrecks regression tests, just less visibly. Assert that a float total equals an exact literal, or compare two pipelines for byte-identical sums, and the test will flap. It passes on a small fixture that runs serially, then fails in CI on a larger one that crosses the parallel threshold, with no code change in between. The test is asserting a property the type does not have, and it will keep flapping until someone notices that.

And anywhere a float sum feeds an equality check, a join, or a GROUP BY key, you are building on sand. Two totals that "should" be equal can differ in their last bits, and now your join silently drops rows.

The most convincing evidence that this is a real, known hazard and not a corner case is that Postgres deliberately gives up an optimization because of it.

Window functions can compute a moving sum efficiently using an inverse transition function: as the frame slides forward, subtract the value leaving the window instead of re-summing the whole frame. It works for numeric and integers. For float4 and float8, Postgres refuses to do it. From the documentation on user-defined aggregates:

An example of an aggregate for which adding an inverse transition function seems easy at first, yet where this requirement cannot be met is sum over float4 or float8 inputs.

The docs then show why, with the same absorption we saw earlier. If a window holds 1e20 and 1, subtracting 1e20 as it leaves the frame yields 0, not the 1 that should remain. So the built-in float sum simply does not use the inverse optimization, and a moving sum(float8) recomputes the frame to stay correct:

The second row is 1, correct, because Postgres declined the shortcut that would have made it 0. The engine is quietly working around the imprecision of float sums on your behalf. The non-determinism in the parallel case is the same hazard showing up somewhere the engine cannot paper over it.

The fix is almost always the type, not the query.

Use numeric for anything anyone reconciles, compares, or reads as a figure. Money, totals on reports, anything that has to match another system or itself across runs. numeric sums in exact decimal and is order-independent: same rows, same answer, parallel or not, in any order.

The cast above stabilizes a single query, but the durable fix is storing the column as numeric in the first place, so the exact values are what live on disk.

numeric is not free. It is arbitrary-precision arithmetic done in software, so it is slower to compute and takes more space on disk than a hardware float the CPU adds in a single instruction. For anything that has to reconcile, that is the price of correctness, and it is almost always worth paying. For a column nobody ever sums into a figure a person reads, it usually is not.

If the data is genuinely floating point (sensor readings, scientific measurements, anything where the input is already an approximation), then accept that aggregates over it are approximate and do not assert exact equality on them. Round to a sensible number of decimals before comparing or displaying, and never use a raw float sum as a join or grouping key.

Pinning max_parallel_workers_per_gather = 0 makes a float sum repeatable, but does not make it correct, and it costs you parallelism on every other query in the session. It is a way to get a stable wrong answer, not a fix. Reach for the type instead.

None of this is Postgres-specific. Any database that parallelizes aggregation, and they all do, has the same behavior over the same IEEE 754 floats. The trigger that makes it visible is simply crossing whatever threshold turns parallelism on, which is why it so often shows up when a table grows past a certain size and "nothing else changed."

title: UK appoints Secretary of Justice with no Law qualifications or legal experience
description: New lord chancellor is fifth non-lawyer in the modern role - but he is no household name.
fullText:
A few blank faces greeted the appointment of Alex Norris MP as lord chancellor and justice secretary. Norris is hardly a household name and his hitherto modest ministerial career has generated few portents of a star in the making. Some lawyers may gloomily conclude that this eye-catching promotion reflects the lowly rung on the Cabinet ladder that the position of lord chancellor now occupies.

More instructive clues to Norris’s elevation lie in his background. Like Burnham, the 42-year-old is a former municipal worthy of the ‘soft left’, having spent six years as Nottingham city councillor. He is also a former UNISON trade union organiser.

A Mancunian alumnus of Manchester Grammar School, Norris moved to the Midlands city permanently after studying politics and history at Nottingham University. He entered parliament as MP for Nottingham North in 2017 as one of the Corbyn intake, after Labour did better than expected in that year’s general election. Like Burnham, he supported Lisa Nandy in the 2020 Labour leadership election.

Until yesterday, Norris was Home Office minister responsible for border security and asylum. He entered government in 2024 as parliamentary under-secretary with responsibilities including local growth, building safety and fire policy, at the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government.

According to the BBC, he is also a keen runner and supports Manchester City.

Norris is not wholly unknown to the legal profession’s leaders. Some months ago he accompanied Bar chair Kirsty Brimelow KC on a visit to Nottingham Crown Court. ‘He spent time and engaged with and listened to barristers in the robing room. He also saw the court’s disrepair,’ Brimelow said on social media.

Recently I met with working barristers as well as the Chair and other leaders from the Bar Council, for a visit to Nottingham Crown Court. It was incredibly valuable to hear directly from the front line about what support they need from the government. pic.twitter.com/5kQq8YCEiC

Norris is the fifth non-lawyer to hold the role of Lord Chancellor after Conservatives Chris Grayling, Michael Gove, Liz Truss and David Lidington. Since then, there have been seven lawyers.

Remarkably little media commentary exists on Norris’s prospects for political high office.

Political intelligence website Beyond the Vote notes that he is a 100% party-line voter with no rebel votes on record. His stance profile ‘marks him as strongly pro-workers’-rights (88%) and pro-progressive-taxation (100%), consistent with Labour’s current legislative direction’.

It adds: ‘Compared to the average Labour MP, he sits noticeably further toward assisted dying access (+31 percentage points above the party average) and is more aligned with energy security and anti-fossil-fuel positions.’

title: Ripple – Rust Pusher-compatible WebSocket server, a drop-in Reverb swap
description: websocket extention. Contribute to madisoheib/Ripple development by creating an account on GitHub.
fullText:
Self-hosted, Pusher-compatible WebSocket server for the PHP ecosystem — a single static binary.

Ripple speaks the Pusher Channels protocol, so every existing client works
unchanged: Laravel Echo, pusher-js, pusher-php-server, mobile SDKs.
No Redis, no Node, no PHP extensions — download one binary and run it.

AWS c6i.xlarge, servers pinned to 2 cores, full methodology and caveats in
bench/RESULTS.md:

Earlier same-machine Docker runs (1k conns) told the same story: ~2× lower
latency, ~3× less memory, bounded slow-consumer behavior vs unbounded
buffering. Soketi is absent because we haven't run it on this harness yet —
we don't publish numbers we didn't measure.

¹ Reverb needed ext-ev, memory_limit=-1 and a raised connection limit to
complete the 5k test; Ripple ran stock. Absolute numbers are specific to
this hardware (Docker on an 8-core host) — treat them as relative. CPU-core
ceilings (Reverb: one core; Ripple: all cores) only diverge further at
scales this harness can't generate; large-scale Linux results will be
published when available.

One-liner (Linux x86_64/ARM64, macOS Intel/Apple Silicon — verifies SHA-256):

Or download manually from Releases
(includes Windows), or with Docker:

Or build from 

Every value can be overridden by environment (RIPPLE_HOST, RIPPLE_PORT).

Laravel (6 through 13) — use
ripple/ripple-laravel:

Any PHP — pusher-php-server already speaks the protocol:

Browser — Laravel Echo / pusher-js with wsHost/wsPort pointed at the server.

Run behind any reverse proxy — one port serves both WebSocket and the REST
API. Full nginx and Caddy configs: docs/reverse-proxy.md.

Raise ulimit -n to at least 2× your target connection count (ripple
warns at boot if it's too low).

Full technical specification: project.md.

title: AliExpress gets record €550M fine from EU for allowing sale of illegal products
description: The Chinese retailer allowed unsafe toys and counterfeit clothes to be sold on its site, says the EU.
fullText:
Chinese online retail giant AliExpress has been fined a record €550m (£467m) by the EU for allowing the sale of illegal products such as unsafe toys and fake clothes.

The European Commission said AliExpress has fallen short of its legal obligations "diligently assess" the risk of illegal, unsafe or fake goods on its platform.

"The spread of counterfeit clothing, unsafe toys, dangerous cosmetics and other illegal and harmful products is not an unavoidable cost of shopping online - it is a failure by AliExpress to comply with its obligations," said EU tech chief Henna Virkkunen.

AliExpress said the fine was "disproportionate" and that it would appeal.

The two-year investigation found that AliExpress's detection systems "did not work properly", with many illegal products not flagged while others that were identified remained on the site for several weeks.

The European Commission also found the company did not properly enforce penalties on traders selling illegal goods.

Furthermore, its product compliance checks could be "easily circumvented".

AliExpress, which is owned by the Chinese tech conglomerate Alibaba, has 193 million users in Europe, more than fellow Chinese online retailers Shein or Temu.

The penalty is the highest fine imposed under the Digital Services Act, which requires tech giants to do more to counter illegal and harmful content.

The act allows for fines of up to 6% of a company's revenue, but as Alibaba had a global turnover of €122bn last year, the penalty is far short of that.

AliExpress said it was surprised by and disagreed with the EU's decision.

"AliExpress has been and continues to be committed to meeting our obligations to consumers. We invest substantial resources in risk assessment and mitigation, product safety and consumer protection," it said.

"Today's decision and disproportionate fine ignores our sound risk management framework and the significant, proactive enhancements we have made. We will appeal the decision."

Under the EU's ruling, the company has to pay the penalty and present a plan by 20 October that includes what action it will take to tackle the breaches.

Earlier this year, Temu was given a €200m fine for allowing the sale of illegal products such as dangerous baby toys.

And Elon Musk's X was fined €120m last year, after the commission said allowing users to pay for its blue tick badges was deceptive because the social media platform was not "meaningfully verifying" who was behind the account and exposed users to scams.

Online marketplaces still selling dozens of unsafe baby products, Which? finds

title: Plain Language Guide Series
description: A series of guides to help you understand and practice writing, designing, and testing plain language.
fullText:
Plain language – content that is clear and easy to understand – is critical to helping the public to make sense of their obligations and benefits. These guides are designed to help web managers and content strategists to understand why and how to create content that readers can understand. Not only is plain language more efficient and effective. It is also the law.

The Plain Writing Act of 2010 established the requirement that content for the public is written for its specific audience. These guides help you to understand how to create, design and test content so your specific audience understands it.

Overview of plain language principles, writing for your audience, and the Plain Writing Act of 2010.

An overview of how to write clear content that follows plain language guidelines.

How content design can support plain language.

Learn how to test that your content is easily understandable.

Selections of PlainLanguage.gov content–especially those most relevant for digital teams–have been carried forward in this set of plain language guides. All of the original content from the PlainLanguage.gov website is archived in the PlainLanguage.gov GitHub repository. Specific former pages are viewable in the Guidelines section of the repository.

title: Assembly Chat – Chat with Any .NET
description: Upload any managed .NET assembly. AI extracts public APIs, answers questions, generates working C# code, and creates SVG/PUML diagrams with RAG for fast search.
fullText:
Upload a DLL or NuGet package. Our AI reads metadata, builds a RAG index, and lets you ask questions, generate code samples, and visualize architecture.

Select a DLL, EXE, or .nupkg file from your local machine or CI pipeline.

The system reflects over the assembly to map namespaces, classes, interfaces, enums, delegates, methods, properties, fields, and custom attributes.

API signatures, XML comments, and inferred behaviors are vectorized and indexed, enabling instant question answering.

Ask "How do I use X?", "Show me an example of Y", or "Generate a sequence diagram for Z". AI replies with code, explanations, and visual diagrams.

Semantic search over public methods, properties, and XML comments. Find what you need faster than MSDN.

Generated C#/F# code includes necessary using statements, proper async/await patterns, and error handling based on actual signatures.

Auto-generate public API class diagrams (SVG) and sequence diagrams (PlantUML) for documentation or design reviews.
