title: Rand Paul releases docs that reveal FBI halted border inspection of virus researcher over Wuhan lab links
description: CBP flagged Peter Daszak as a high person of interest over his gain-of-function research ties to the Wuhan Institute of Virology before the FBI intervened.
fullText:
Agents with U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) were preparing to question a British zoologist and disease ecology expert upon his return to the United States from the World Health Organization's investigation into the origins of COVID-19, before the FBI intervened and asked agents to stand down, according to files released Monday by Sen. Rand Paul, R-Ky.

"They told the public a COVID lab leak 'wasn't plausible,'" Rand wrote on X. "Their own private messages say otherwise. Today I'm launching The Reading Room where the public can review records related to my ongoing investigation – not just conclusions but the actual documents."

The documents show CBP's National Targeting Center flagged Peter Daszak as "a high person of interest" ahead of his Feb. 5, 2021, arrival at John F. Kennedy International Airport.

Daszak, the former president of EcoHealth Alliance, was flagged because of more than 15 years of collaboration with the Wuhan Institute of Virology (WIV) and his longstanding relationship with its lead researcher, Zhengli Shi, as well as his role as principal investigator on National Institutes of Health (NIH) grants funding bat coronavirus research in China.

Rand Paul Brings Cia Whistleblower To Senate Hearing Alleging 'Deep State' Covid-19 Conspiracy

Much of the funding Daszak received was put toward gain-of-function research, the documents state.

The documents highlight that the NIH had asked Daszak to retrieve the original type-specimen used by WIV for genetic analysis and return it to the U.S., as well as to explain several aspects of his collaboration with the lab, including providing related cell phone data.

CBP officials planned to question Daszak on his activities in China, his contacts at the WIV, and whether he illegally collected or carried biological samples.

Top 4 Explosive Moments From Cia Whistleblower's Testimony On Alleged Covid-19 Lab Leak Cover-up

Internal communications highlighted concerns regarding Daszak's involvement in gain-of-function research and potential conflicts of interest involving federal grants.

However, three days before his arrival in New York, CBP received a request from the FBI's New York Field Office to halt the stop, and the inspection never happened.

"FBI New York requested we do not stop the subject below," the request states, according to the documents.

Missing Scientist Scrutiny Reaches Fbi As Patel's Probe Of Mystery Disappearances Set For 'Final Report'

The records include detailed flight itineraries, proposed interrogation questions, and internal emails documenting the interagency disagreement over the search.

"This isn't the whole story. It's the start of one," Paul wrote in a lengthy thread on X. "The Reading Room will be updated on a rolling basis as the investigation continues."

At the time of the planned interception, Daszak headed the now-defunct EcoHealth Alliance, an American-based nonprofit that conducted research and outreach on the causes of emerging pandemic diseases, such as those originating in bats.

He was a member of the 10-person WHO team that traveled to China to investigate the origins of SARS-CoV-2, the virus that causes COVID-19.

Daszak had received public criticism regarding a potential conflict of interest for being included on the WHO investigative team despite his financial and professional ties to WIV.

Daszak now heads Nature Health Global, a non-profit scientific organization focused on ecological research, public health, and pandemic prevention.

Fox News Digital has reached out to the FBI and Nature Health Global.

title: Boy, 14, charged over city centre stabbing
description: A teenager has been charged over the Angel Row stabbing and an assault in Upper Parliament Street.
fullText:
A teenager has been charged following a stabbing in Nottingham city centre.

Nottinghamshire Police said a 25-year-old man was attacked in Angel Row at about 15:15 BST on Friday and taken to hospital.

A 14-year-old, who cannot be named for legal reasons, was charged over the incident as well as punching a man in an attempted robbery of an e-bike in Upper Parliament Street earlier the same day, police said.

The boy appeared at Nottingham Magistrates' Court on Monday and was remanded in custody until his next hearing on 27 July, said the force.

He has been charged with committing grievous bodily harm with intent and possession of a knife in public in relation to the Angel Row attack, as well as assault, two counts of assault on a police officer, and criminal damage at Queen's Medical Centre following his arrest.

Det Ch Insp Claire Gould said the stabbing victim's injuries were not as serious as "first feared and we wish him a speedy recovery".

title: House Democrats launch investigation into whether Jeffrey Epstein acted as a foreign agent
description: Democratic Rep. Jamie Raskin is launching an investigation into whether Jeffrey Epstein acted as an unregistered foreign agent for multiple countries, requesting records from the Trump administration ...
fullText:
Democratic Rep. Jamie Raskin is launching an investigation into whether Jeffrey Epstein acted as an unregistered foreign agent for multiple countries, requesting records from the Trump administration on the scope of the late convicted sex offender's foreign ties.

In the Republican-controlled House, Raskin's demands to the Department of Justice, Department of State and intelligence community are likely to go unanswered, but his push as the top Democrat on the House Judiciary Committee serves as a preview for how Democrats will pursue investigations if they reclaim the House majority in the next Congress, which would give them control of committees with subpoena power.

In a new letter, Raskin points to documents released by the Department of Justice and the House Oversight Committee and says that he has found "substantial evidence" that Epstein "acted to advance the interests" of the Saudi Arabian and Kuwaiti governments, advised the Russian government and worked as a consultant for a former Israeli prime minister. Raskin also raises concerns about the potential security risks Epstein's foreign ties posed, given he was never registered as a foreign agent.

"Our examination of available evidence has already revealed damning indications of Mr. Epstein's activities on behalf of foreign states," Raskin wrote.

CNN has reached out for comment to the Justice Department, the State Department and the Office of the Director of National Intelligence.

But Raskin believes there could be even more information about Epstein's foreign ties within the approximately 2.5 million documents that the Justice Department has not released related to Epstein. The Democratic lawmaker is requesting any records related to Epstein's interactions with foreign officials. In addition, Raskin is also calling on DOJ to provide a written response to address if there has ever been an investigation into whether Epstein acted as a foreign agent.

"Despite having such extensive, well-documented personal and professional contacts with officials of foreign governments, Mr. Epstein never registered as a foreign agent," Raskin wrote.

title: Walker to take over from Farbrace as Sussex coach
description: Sussex announce former Kent boss Matt Walker will replace Paul Farbrace as their head coach for the 2027 season.
fullText:
Sussex have announced that former Kent boss Matt Walker will take over as their new head coach for the 2027 season.

The 52-year-old will take charge on 1 November, succeeding Paul Farbrace, who is switching roles to become Sussex's new Director of Cricket.

Walker spent eight seasons as head coach at Kent, guiding them to promotion to Division One in 2018, T20 Blast success in 2021 and the One-Day Cup in 2022.

He has also coached in the England men's set-up and is currently batting coach at Warwickshire and assistant coach with MI London, formerly known as Oval Invincibles, in The Hundred.

Farbrace to take up director role at Sussex

Sussex bowler Crocombe to make Surrey move

Sussex appoint ex-athletics chief Warner as chair

Walker said: "Sussex is a club I have always admired and one with a proud history. The opportunity to work with this group of players and staff, alongside Paul [Farbrace], was one that really excited me.

"There is already a strong foundation in place and I'm looking forward to building on that as we strive to help Sussex compete for trophies. I can't wait to get started."

Farbrace said Walker possessed the "experience, energy and enthusiasm" that the club had been looking for.

"I'm really looking forward to working alongside him as we tackle the challenges in front of us and build a team capable of competing for trophies," he added.

Sussex returned to Division One as champions in 2024 under the guidance of Farbrace, before finishing fourth in 2025.

The club were then hit with a 12-point deduction for the 2026 season after "sustained operating losses" in the previous financial year.

Walker and new club chairman Ed Warner will have to work within tight financial restrictions going forward.

The club is under a player salary cap until 2028, and need to provide the ECB with a three‑year business plan, and deliver sustainable year‑on‑year operating profits for the same period.

Young fast bowler Henry Crocombe is joining Surrey in 2027, and BBC Radio Sussex understands that John Simpson, Tom Clark, Fynn Hudson-Prentice, Danny Lamb and Charlie Tear are all out of contract at the end of the season.

Sussex interim chief executive officer, Mark West, said the revamped management team would be key to the club turning the corner following their recent financial problems.

"Matt's appointment is another important step for Sussex Cricket as we continue strengthening the club both on and off the field.

"He is joining a talented group of players and staff, and we believe he is the right person to help drive the next phase of our cricket programme".

title: Tottenham sign France defender Sombath from Lyon
description: Tottenham have signed France international Alice Sombath for a club record fee from Lyon.
fullText:
Tottenham have signed France international defender Alice Sombath for a club record transfer fee from Lyon.

The 22-year-old joins for an undisclosed fee and the length of her contract is unknown.

The previous club record signing is believed to have been Norway international Signe Gaupset's arrival for around £375,000 last year.

Sombath has spent five seasons at European giants Lyon, making 102 appearances and breaking into the France senior squad.

During her time in France, she won five consecutive Premiere Ligue titles and was part of the successful 2021-22 Women's Champions League squad.

Promoted Crystal Palace sign striker England

"I want to do my best here at Tottenham. I think the league is very competitive, every game is tough and that's what I'm most excited about," said Sombath.

"I saw that Spurs had a very good season last year. My qualities can bring something to the team, even though I know they already have a lot."

Sombath came through Paris FC's academy and joined Paris St-Germain's youth team set-up before her move to Lyon in 2020.

She can play at centre-back or right-back and has represented France at every age group from the under-16s to the senior squad.

The defender featured in all of France's matches at Euro 2025, starting three games in the group stages.

"She's a player we've followed closely for some time and one who fits the profile of what we're building here," said Tottenham manager Martin Ho.

"She has already shown real maturity in her performances, and we believe she has all the qualities to continue developing into a top player within our environment.

"Alice is an aggressive defender who is comfortable in possession, competitive in her duels, and has the ambition to keep improving every day.

"This is another important addition as we continue building for the future. We can't wait to get started with Alice and welcome her into the group."

Tottenham have been successful in the pursuit of their key targets during the transfer window, bringing in six additions including Sombath.

They have also signed Shekeira Martinez, Kirsty Hanson, Victoria Pelova, Caitlin Dijkstra and Selma Panengstuen, getting their business done early.

title: Japan goalkeeper Okuma signs for Aston Villa
description: Aston Villa announce the signing of 21-year-old Japan goalkeeper Akane Okuma.
fullText:
Aston Villa have signed Japan goalkeeper Akane Okuma, subject to visa approval.

The 21-year-old joins the Women's Super League side from Japanese club INAC Kobe Leonessa, where she had played since 2024.

Okuma began her senior career at JEF United Chiba.

She represented Japan at youth level and was part of the senior squad that won the 2026 AFC Women's Asian Cup alongside new Villa team-mate Maya Hijikata.

Villa director of women's football Marisa Ewers said: "Akane is an exciting young talent with a lot of potential and we're delighted to welcome her to Aston Villa.

"As a club, we're always looking at opportunities to identify talent in different markets around the world, and we're pleased to bring a player of Akane's profile to Villa."

Okuma said she was looking forward to the next step in her career.

"I'm very happy to be here," she said. "I feel really fortunate to be a part of this club, and I can't wait to play with the team."

Villa finished ninth in the 12-team WSL last season. The league's 2026-27 fixtures are scheduled to be released on 30 July, with the competition expanding to feature 14 teams.

title: From childbirth to Commonwealth Games in four months
description: Para-swimmer Rebecca Redfern admits her Commonwealth Games training has been "unconventional" as she heads to Glasgow just four months after having a baby.
fullText:
Para-swimmer Rebecca Redfern has admitted her Commonwealth Games training has been "a little unconventional" as she heads to Glasgow just four months after childbirth.

The 26-year-old, who has a condition which limits peripheral vision, will line up in the 50m freestyle in Glasgow, having given birth to a baby girl in March.

Many sportswomen face an agonising choice - to put their sporting career on pause to have children, or even put their family plans on hold.

But for Redfern, who also has a six-year-old son, the decision was very clear - she wanted to do both.

"For me it was important to have kids quite young because my vision is going to deteriorate and I could go blind one day, and I didn't want the worry that I'm not going to see my kids grow up," she told BBC Hereford and Worcester. "That was my biggest fear.

"So to balance being able to have my kids and still being an elite athlete is an incredible opportunity and I didn't want to compromise on either of them."

With her training time cut short, Redfern will race over 50m rather than her preferred 100m distance - just as she did at Birmingham four years ago.

Despite the physical challenges all mums face after childbirth, the 2024 Paralympic gold medallist has adjusted quickly to life back in the pool.

"I've not been able to build up the full 100 yet so it's just sticking with the 50 and sticking with the speed," she explained.

"I've surprised myself actually. I thought I might just be bumbling along, but I've actually been able to pick up a lot of speed.

"In the training I've been doing I work closely with the physios to make sure I'm not overdoing it and it all seems to be falling into place."

The switch to hold the Games in Glasgow after the Australian state of Victoria's withdrawal means Redfern will have childcare close at hand.

"I'm still breastfeeding, so the team has been really supportive and put me up in a hotel so I can have the little one with me," she said.

"Australia would have been absolutely incredible but having it a little bit closer to home has worked out really nicely for me."

Gold medal Paralympian gets freedom of hometown

'My MBE means elite sport pain is all worthwhile'

Redfern won her second Paralympic silver medal in Tokyo in 2021, just nine months after becoming a mum for the first time.

But with just a four-month gap this time around, she is tempering her ambitions.

"I'm not putting any expectations on," she said.

"As long as I can get from the start to the finish and have a decent go at it, we'll just have a little bit of fun and we'll see what happens."

She added: "I'm quite fortunate that the Commonwealths isn't my main event so there's almost no pressure on me to perform to my best.

"It's just about going and enjoying, and showing my kids that they've got a supermum."

Why swimmer Clark won't let epilepsy or day job slow her down

title: GB's Bentley elevated to WBO middleweight champion
description: Britain's Denzel Bentley is elevated to WBO middleweight champion after Janibek Alimkhanuly relinquished the belt.
fullText:
Britain's Denzel Bentley has been elevated to full WBO middleweight champion after Janibek Alimkhanuly relinquished the belt.

The WBO said, external Kazahkstan boxer Alimkhanuly, who was suspended for a year after testing positive for a banned substance, external in December, wants to move up to super middleweight.

Bentley, 31, beat Endry Saavedra in April to become the interim WBO middleweight champion.

"Great news for Denzel Bentley," said promoter Frank Warren. "A huge achievement for him, [manager] Martin Bowers and the whole team.

"Said back in April he grabbed the opportunity when it came, one that was fully deserved. Congratulations, champ!"

Bentley has 22 wins, three defeats and a draw from his 26 professional fights.

A statement from the WBO said that "no mandatory championship defence has been satisfied in the middleweight division for more than two years" and that it "reserves determination of whether the champion's first defence shall be mandatory or voluntary by subsequent ruling".

It added that the mandatory challenger for the belt was 29-year-old Yoenli Hernandez, of Cuba.

title: Why England teenager Parkinson has a mindset coach
description: Why England teenager Erica Meg Parkinson has a mindset coach who has worked with the All Blacks and ex-England cricketer Ashley Giles.
fullText:
Erica Meg Parkinson was an unknown quantity when England manager Sarina Wiegman unexpectedly named her in the senior squad in March.

The teenager was playing in Portugal and had only made the jump up to England's Under-23s a few months earlier.

People are now starting to take notice as the midfielder secured a move to the United States this summer, joining North Carolina Courage on a three-year deal.

Wiegman was in the stands watching on Saturday as the 17-year-old made her debut, coming on as a half-time substitute in a 3-0 loss to Bay FC and performing well.

But behind the raw talent is an ambitious footballer who has planned out her route to the top by training her mind in order to stand out from the rest.

BBC Sport explores how she is developing off the pitch as well as on it.

'I like surprises' - Wiegman calls up 17-year-old Parkinson

Why multilingual rising star Parkinson picked England

Parkinson was born in Singapore to an English father and a Japanese mother, and is eligible to represent both nations, as well as Portugal, where she spent most of her playing career.

She was with Leixoes from the age of 10 and had some involvement with Benfica before breaking into Valadares Gaia's first team in the 2023-24 season.

Since the age of 12, Parkinson has been working with a mindset coach, developing skills to handle pressure and expectation off the pitch, and to improve her leadership, decision-making and awareness on it.

She connected with Martin Fairn, founder of Gazing Performance, who has worked with a number of elite athletes including the New Zealand men's rugby team and former England cricketer Ashley Giles.

Fairn was approached by Parkinson's father, Richard, and quickly began working with Erica and her brother Dennis.

"I knew straight away that Erica had a determination not everybody has," Fairn told BBC Sport.

"I said to Erica: 'What do you want to do?' And she said: 'I'd like to play football for England.' That was how it all kicked off.

"It's relatively rare someone that young seeks out mindset training. Do they need it? There won't be one person who couldn't benefit from it.

"In football particularly, it's viewed as a problem that needs fixing rather than a skill that needs developing."

Parkinson and Fairn have worked together for over five years, catching up in person at the end of each season and holding regular meetings over Zoom.

In a pre-season session before the 2025-26 campaign - which BBC Sport has seen summary notes of - Parkinson focused on raised expectations and on developing more clarity of her role on the pitch.

On her flight to her first senior Lionesses camp in March, Parkinson wrote a list of the pressures and emotions she could feel - a common process with Fairn.

They reflected on how she might react to the challenges, what her ambitions were during those moments and how she could prepare.

Anticipation of walking into a camp with star-studded names

Increased intensity of training

Media attention and increased scrutiny

One of the biggest discussions Fairn and Parkinson had was about the work she had done to get herself into a position to earn an England call-up.

Parkinson can speak four languages (Portuguese, French, Japanese and English), has trained with boys in Portugal and cemented her place in the senior women's team at Valadares as a 15-year-old.

She has almost 50 caps for England's youth teams and will be taking another step-up now she has joined North Carolina Courage.

But there is still a way to go if Parkinson wants to become a regular in Wiegman's squad - so how can Fairn help her prepare for that?

"The pathway to the top is notoriously challenging. The acceptance Erica had of that reality was one of the first breakthroughs," said Fairn.

"She's really clear about the people around her and she comes to each session with a list of things she's going to work on.

"Her own expectations are the biggest source of pressure. Naturally, that's her strength - she wants to get better - but it can be a vulnerability if it just sits in your head.

"She's built confidence to feel comfortable performing at the level she's at. Some people feel they have to be perfect but you need to realise you won't be."

Fairn says Parkinson excels at "zooming out" which allows her to recognise her progress, where she needs to develop and the impact of any mistakes she makes.

It is clear that despite her young age, Parkinson has matured rapidly and Fairn believes she has not only the talent, but the mental tools for continued success.

"Her determination is clear when you meet her. She's prepared to work as hard as she needs to achieve her dreams," he added.

"It's a real privilege to be involved in part of that story. When you watch her play - and at every level I've watched her - she looks like she belongs.

"I suspect she will feel, already where she is with the Lionesses, is where she's meant to be."

title: Why Newcastle are changing club badge
description: Newcastle United supporters are set to vote on a new crest as the club look to change their badge for the first time since 1988.
fullText:
Newcastle United supporters are set to vote on a new crest, with the club poised to change their badge for the first time since 1988.

The existing emblem is popular with supporters, but Newcastle feel it "struggles to perform in a modern setting".

"Its intricacies and irregularities make consistent, high-quality reproduction impossible - from modern digital platforms to broadcast environments and merchandise," the club said.

Newcastle's plans to update the crest were first publicly mooted in May 2025.

The club have discussed "potential routes forward" with the fan advisory board and surveyed 19,500 members and season ticket holders.

Supporters' feedback helped shape the options, which will ultimately be presented on the ballot.

A total of 84% of those surveyed made it clear they wanted Newcastle to "honour our heritage" rather than completely overhaul the crest.

Supporters also ranked which elements of the current crest they believed were most important, including the castle, seahorses, black and white shield and ribbon.

Eligible voters - a group that comprises season ticket holders, members and anyone who purchased tickets for two or more women's home matches last season - will pick their favoured design during a two-week window in August.

BBC Sport asked supporters for their views and one respondent, Ian, suggested voters should have a "none of the above option".

"It's hard to say supporters are getting their say when the club came out and said this is happening whether anyone likes it or not," added another, Matt.

"These new crests represent a simplified, homogenised version of everything that has ramped up post-Covid.

"I can already see the final product; a stripped-down version of a crest that is more caricature than representative of anything meaningful."

However, Newcastle supporter Phil highlighted how the club "at least seem to be listening to fans instead of barrelling into some soulless, corporate design".

Another supporter, Andy, "loves" the existing badge, but also stressed "we have to understand that things change".

"Whatever brand you look at, there has been evolution to meet the demands of the modern world," he added.

"We should be no different. It seems like they're trying to go about it in the right, respectful way, but we have to let it happen."

Gifted, charismatic and passionate amid highs and lows - Keegan obituary

How have missed targets affected Newcastle's summer rebuild?

title: Richardson unfazed by any Australia animosity over Team GB switch
description: Cyclist Matt Richardson says he has "moved on" from a storm over his nationality switch as he aims to defend his Commonwealth Games titles.
fullText:
Double Olympic silver medallist Matt Richardson says he has "moved on" from the furore surrounding his switch to Great Britain as he prepares to defend the Commonwealth Games cycling titles he won while representing Australia four years ago.

Richardson - who was born in Maidstone, Kent, but moved to Australia at the age of nine and maintained dual citizenship - changed his cycling nationality just days after winning two silvers and a bronze at the Paris Olympics in 2024.

As a result, he was banned for life from rejoining the Australia team and will face former team-mates in Glasgow when he rides for Team England.

"I tried to block out all the negativity, and all the noise," the 27-year-old told BBC Radio Kent.

"I experienced the way they decided to handle it at the time, and moved on as quickly as I could.

"Ultimately, it has zero effect on me. If, for some reason, the British public didn't take it well, that wouldn't have been very good, but it wasn't the case."

Richardson said his results since joining the Team GB squad have justified his decision.

He won two silver medals in sprint and team sprint at the 2025 Track Cycling World Championships and later clinched the European sprint and keirin titles.

GB's Richardson wins historic European track gold

'High school to Hogwarts' - will GB magic bring Richardson gold?

Australia's Richardson switches to GB cycling team

He has also twice broken the men's flying 200m time trial world record, becoming the first cyclist under the nine-second barrier.

Richardson believes the greater resources and training facilities in Britain have allowed him to progress his career.

"No expense is spared when it comes to trying to achieve success, and it really feels like that," he said.

"We fly with more bikes, and more equipment - anything we need to perform at our absolute best at a given race.

"I remember doing World Cups for Australia, and I would only fly over with my race bike. I fly to races now with about four or five bikes.

"I've got spares, I've got bikes for team sprint, I've got bikes for sprint, I've got a road bike, I've got a bike that I just warm up on for activations. Those things really make a difference, when a final comes down to a thousandth of a second."

Richardson won gold in individual and team sprint events at the 2022 Commonwealth Games in Birmingham and says he is not worried about having a target on his back.

"I use it as fuel," he said. "I always find motivation in something, whether it's trying to hunt someone down, trying to be better than someone or trying to stay better than other people."

However, his busy schedule may play a part in how many events he can take part in at the Games.

"I'll be travelling from [racing in] Japan so it's nip-and-tuck on which events I can actually make," Richardson admitted.

"If I was to race in the team sprint, I will fly in on the day and literally go straight to the velodrome and race.

"Whether or not that's the best thing to be doing physically, I'm undecided yet, but there is theoretically time for me to race everything.

"My plan currently is to race in the team sprint, keirin and the sprint. The kilo is undecided. If I do race everything, the goal is to win everything."

title: Bournemouth midfielder Scott rejects new contract
description: Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott rejects a new contract amid growing Premier League interest in the England Under-21 international.
fullText:
Bournemouth midfielder Alex Scott has rejected a new contract amid interest from rival Premier League clubs in the England Under-21 international.

The Cherries made it a summer priority to tie Scott down to fresh terms, but as things stand there is no agreement.

Arsenal, Manchester United, Tottenham, Manchester City and Chelsea have an interest in Scott.

BBC Sport reported in June that Arsenal had an approach for Scott turned down by Bournemouth.

The 22-year-old, who joined Bournemouth from Bristol City in 2023 for £25m, has two years left on his current deal with the Premier League side.

Bournemouth remain steadfast in their position that Scott is not for sale, despite him rejecting the contract offer that had been on the table for at least two months.

Bournemouth have insisted publicly, directly to the player and to interested clubs that he will remain on the south coast, even on his current contract.

That stance has been consistent and, despite talk of an £80m valuation, they have continued to reject approaches.

The south coast club are under no pressure to sell after qualifying for the Europa League and generating significant income through the sales last summer of Dean Huijsen, Milos Kerkez, Illia Zabarnyi and Dango Outtara.

They have taken a similar stance with highly rated forward Junior Kroupi, who is attracting interest from Arsenal and Tottenham.

Brazil international Rayan is also not expected to leave and has given no indication that he wants to, having only joined the club in January.

Why are Premier League transfer fees soaring?

How the World Cup has affected your Premier League team

Scott and Kroupi are with the squad for new manager Marco Rose's pre-season training camp in Austria, with Rayan due back at a later date having played for Brazil at the World Cup.

Scott was selected in his first England squad last November and was then called into Thomas Tuchel's extended pre-World Cup training camp in Florida but is yet make an appearance at senior international level.

In a busy Premier League midfielder market this summer, Nottingham Forest's Elliot Anderson and Aston Villa's Morgan Rogers are set to complete moves to Manchester City and Chelsea for £116m and £117m respectively.

Tottenham have spent £100m on Newcastle's Sandro Tonali and £85m on West Ham's Mateus Fernandes.

Scott joined Bristol City from Guernsey FC in 2020, going on to make 91 appearances for the Championship side in all competitions.

Having been named the division's young player of the season in 2022-23, he joined Bournemouth where he has made 89 appearances and scored six goals.

He helped Bournemouth finish sixth in 2025-26 under Andoni Iraola, qualifying for European football for the first time in their history.

title: Keeper Martinez may 'step aside' from Argentina
description: Argentina goalkeeper Emi Martinez says he is considering whether it is "time to step aside" from international football after his side's 1-0 defeat to Spain in the World Cup final.
fullText:
Argentina goalkeeper Emi Martinez says he is considering whether it is "time to step aside" from international football after his side's 1-0 defeat to Spain in the World Cup final.

The 33-year-old has won 67 caps for his country and played every minute of Argentina's eight World Cup games, keeping two clean sheets and conceding eight goals.

He was a World Cup winner in 2022, also claiming the golden glove award as the tournament's outstanding goalkeeper.

"I dreamt we'd win it again, I dreamt of bringing it back to Argentina and making history once more," Martinez wrote in a post on Instagram., external

"The truth is, the pain is hard to explain. There are many things to reflect on and how to move forward, and if it's time to step aside.

"I'm so sorry, I truly tried my best to help my country and my team-mates."

Martinez won the Yashin Trophy, awarded to the world's best goalkeeper at the Ballon d'Or awards, in 2023 and 2024.

He has played for Aston Villa since 2020 and has a contract until 2029, but has been linked to a move to Italian side Juventus this summer.

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Bethell ruled out of The Hundred with knee injury
description: England all-rounder Jacob Bethell, who was due to captain Birmingham Phoenix, is ruled out of The Hundred with a knee injury.
fullText:
England all-rounder Jacob Bethell has been ruled out of The Hundred with a knee injury sustained during the recent one-day international series against India.

Bethell had been named as Birmingham Phoenix captain for the season.

The England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB) said that the 22-year-old had an MRI scan on Monday but they have not indicated when he will return.

England's Test series against Pakistan starts on 19 August.

It is a blow for Bethell to miss out on the experience of captaincy, as he is regarded as a future England leader, having already led the side in a T20 series against Ireland last year.

He has also been in fine form, including a player of the match performance in Sunday's series decider at Lord's where he struck 91 at the top of the order and took 1-49.

He will be replaced by South Africa's Donovan Ferreira as Phoenix skipper, who is a two-time Hundred champion with Oval Invincibles.

Phoenix's opening match is against Trent Rockets at Edgbaston on Friday (18:30 BST).

Bethell, who plays county cricket for Warwickshire, was retained by Phoenix in the auction earlier this year with a contract worth £340,000.

Get cricket news sent straight to your phone

title: Infantino's 'time is up' with Fifa - La Liga boss
description: La Liga president Javier Tebas calls for Fifa president Gianni Infantino to quit, and claims the governing body is "destroying the football industry".
fullText:
La Liga president Javier Tebas has called for Fifa president Gianni Infantino to step down, claiming the world governing body is "destroying the football industry".

In an interview with Italian newspaper La Gazzetta dello Sport, external, the head of the Spanish league also criticised World Cup hydration breaks, the final's lengthy half-time interval, the postponement of a suspension for United States striker Folarin Balogun, and the future possibility of a 64-team summer tournament.

When asked if Infantino should quit, Tebas said: "In my opinion, yes, I think his time is up."

However, he stated that the 56-year-old has the support of national federations, and claimed Fifa's election system is "rotten at its core".

"There's no opposition candidate, nobody wants to stand just to lose," Tebas said.

"He shouldn't stay, but the current state of affairs means he won't leave."

The head of Spain's top flight was in the US to see his country win the 2026 World Cup.

"These past few days here in America, I've heard many people who are against Infantino, who disagree with what he's doing," Tebas said.

"They say it, but then they do nothing. I don't know what's worse, the silence or the complicity, because those who stay silent are perfectly aware of the damage football is suffering."

Infantino is expected to be re-elected for a fourth term at Fifa's March congress, although Tebas believes Infantino could have been brought down as a consequence of his involvement in the case around Balogun.

The 25-year-old US player escaped a suspension for his side's last-16 tie with Belgium following an intervention from US President Donald Trump.

"The suspension of the American player's ban was absolutely serious. They were lucky that Belgium eliminated the USA, because otherwise a case could have arisen that might have cost Infantino his job," Tebas said.

"Since Belgium won, they managed to bury the matter. But these things are just the tip of the iceberg."

European football's governing body Uefa criticised Fifa's decision at the time, calling it "unprecedented, incomprehensible and unjustifiable", and the decision was a factor in the organisation's president Aleksander Ceferin not attending the World Cup final.

Infantino opened the door to a 64-team global tournament during this year's event, but Tebas argued that "not everything can revolve around the World Cup".

"Increasing the number of national teams makes no sense. The football industry isn't just the World Cup," Tebas said.

"It's the national competitions that sustain this sport. They are destroying the football industry, which generates tens of thousands of jobs, for an event that lasts 40 days and involves a minority of players.

"Are the best players there? Clearly, but they are also in our leagues. We need fewer international matches and more protection for national football, at all levels. They don't realise they are making irresponsible decisions."

Fifa brought in mandatory three-minute hydration breaks for the World Cup, regardless of conditions, for all matches played at stadiums in the United States, Mexico and Canada.

The governing body said they were brought in as a commitment to player welfare, but critics have pointed to broadcasters using the breaks as a way to cash in with commercials.

Meanwhile pop music giants Madonna, BTS, Shakira and Justin Bieber were among the acts to perform in the World Cup final half-time show, which extended the usual 15-minute half-time break to 27 minutes 22 seconds.

"Fifa arranges things as it pleases, for what it wants, for its own interests, certainly not for football," said Tebas. "So, if they need a 27-minute half-time break, they'll have it.

"Hydration breaks are a lie. We have them in La Liga, but only when it's genuinely hot.

"Here, the pitches in Dallas, Los Angeles, Atlanta had air conditioning; I had to put on a jumper in the stands. It was a lie, a break for advertising.

"It's proof that we are governed by an institution that does and decides what it wants, when it wants, following only its own interests without considering the rest of football. And in many cases, it acts to the detriment of football."

BBC Sport has contacted Fifa for comment.

title: Why World Cup can be game-changer for ambitious MLS
description: Can the success of the 2026 World Cup help MLS become one of the world's elite leagues?
fullText:
"Thanks world, We'll take it from here."

That's the message from Lionel Messi, Son Heung-min, David Beckham and dozens of Major League Soccer players in an advertising campaign urging new football fans created by the World Cup to check out MLS.

The slick film showcases World Cup stars who play in the US top flight, inviting fans to join them as the regular season resumes. The league is even offering free tickets to new fans.

"The World Cup has now exposed millions of people to Major League Soccer," says MLS executive vice-president Dan Courtemanche, who joined the league in 1995 before a ball was kicked.

"We're going to continue to capitalise on that momentum."

Could the lasting legacy of this World Cup be the rise of MLS into the band of elite leagues?

Major League Soccer is itself a legacy product from the 1994 World Cup, when Fifa mandated the US to establish a professional league as part of its bid to host the tournament.

The league started in 1996 with 10 teams and zero purpose-built stadiums. Thirty years later, MLS has 30 clubs and almost as many football-specific stadiums.

Despite predictions of empty World Cup seats because of sky-high ticket prices, the stadiums were packed, and broadcast ratings were impressive as football joy spread across the United States - even after the USA were knocked out of the tournament.

The US team's loss to Belgium at the last-16 stage delivered the most-watched football game on one network in US history - with more than 33 million watching on Fox.

England's last-16 win over Mexico became the most-watched football game in US history with a combined 45 million viewers in English and Spanish watching across Fox and Telemundo.

The ratings compare with games in the NFL - what Americans consider football - but are not up to the level of the showpiece Super Bowl.

In 2025, the most-watched Super Bowl in history attracted 127.7 million viewers across English and Spanish broadcasts and streaming in the US, although many Americans tune in solely for the half-time show, which was performed by Kendrick Lamar that year.

MLS, which has shown strong growth since its first season 30 years ago, aspires to become an elite league on par with Europe's top competitions.

"I think we're looked at as the top, one of the top leagues commercially," MLS commissioner Don Garber said at an MLS event to discuss the future of the league and the post-World Cup legacy. "We've cracked that code. Now we've got to crack the rest of it."

In some ways, MLS is already up there with the big leagues across the world.

There were 45 MLS players represented at the 2026 World Cup - including two in the final for Argentina, Messi and Rodrigo de Paul.

MLS has a reputation for being almost a retirement home for superstars - a lucrative place to play for legends like Beckham and Messi after peak careers in Europe.

But that reputation is outdated, Courtemanche says, with the average age of new international players in the league now 23.8 years. Though many of them have dreams of playing in Europe after experiencing MLS.

MLS is changing its calendar in 2027 to adopt a summer-to-spring season, more aligned with Europe to make it easier for players to move between the leagues.

And the competition's leaders are investing in youth development leagues.

All MLS teams run youth academies. As LA Galaxy hosted a viewing party for the US victory over Bosnia-Herzegovina on a big screen, dozens of children ignored the match, instead battling it out themselves on three separate pitches, occasionally running to the big screen when the sound of a goal spread through the crowd.

These were rare pick-up games in a culture full of highly scheduled children - and the skill level was high. Some of the kids played for MLS youth teams.

Eight-year-old Landon - named after US star Landon Donovan - was one of the more skilled, moving from goalkeeper to forward with glee. His mother Jen Wing is a coach and says her children have attended MLS camps and play on travel teams at their home in New Jersey.

Landon dreams of being a professional soccer player one day in MLS.

"LAFC," he says, referring to his dream team.

But critics say MLS will never compete with Europe until it sorts out its "pay-to-play" culture in youth development.

At the top level, MLS academies train and develop youth players for free, much like their European counterparts.

But to get to that level, where an MLS academy team would notice a teenage player, families typically pay thousands of dollars for private lessons and access to travel teams, a common theme in US sports.

MLS officials say the development leagues are constantly evolving and improving when it comes to developing young talent, and that every team has scholarships available to talented players.

Commissioner Garber says the league is always improving and is determined to get even better at developing young players so they can deliver "more and more to the men's national team and just be better, bigger, stronger, faster, all those things that give you energy to think of a better future".

When it comes to attracting audiences, Messi is the league's biggest draw.

But the cult of celebrity runs throughout the league. More than two dozen entertainers and athletes hold ownership stakes in MLS clubs, including Will Ferrell (LAFC), Reese Witherspoon (Nashville SC), Matthew McConaughey (Austin FC) and Magic Johnson (LAFC).

Drew Carey was an original Seattle Sounders owner when the club joined MLS in 2009 for a $30m (£22m) expansion fee. The Sounders are now valued at more than $900m (£668m). David Beckham's 2007 LA Galaxy contract included the option to buy an MLS expansion team for $25m. He now owns Inter Miami CF, a club that experts have valued at more than a billion dollars.

But as much as the US is experiencing a football frenzy right now, many believe US culture will always prioritise American football.

Brian Udovich, whose 12-year-old son Croil plays in an MLS Next Level league in Southern California, says many talented youngsters leave the sport for American football when they become teenagers because of all the fanfare.

On Friday nights, American football players get their names up in lights with marching bands and cheerleaders urging them on to glory in giant stadiums. Whereas soccer is typically played on Saturday mornings with mums and dads in folding chairs.

"It just means so much more to other cultures than us," Udovich says.

Michael Bradley, coach of the New York Red Bulls, says American culture is just different and that the legacy of the World Cup will make association football more prominent.

"We don't have the long, storied football history that other countries do. I think for a long time that led to an inferiority complex that maybe football would never really survive in the United States," Bradley told the BBC, adding that now so many more fans in the US feel proud.

"I think we can all unequivocally say in 2026, football is not going anywhere."

Best goal, best player, best fans - BBC reporters' World Cup awards

Messi says 'pain is immense' after losing World Cup final

Uefa president boycotts World Cup final in protest against Fifa

title: Former Portugal coach explains why Bernardo Silva will thrive at Real Madrid: ‘They have signed a phenomenal player’
description: Real Madrid’s decision to sign Bernardo Silva has received a major vote of confidence from one of the people who knows him better than most. Despite the midfielder enduring a difficult 2026 FIFA Wo...
fullText:
Real Madrid’s decision to sign Bernardo Silva has received a major vote of confidence from one of the people who knows him better than most.

Despite the midfielder enduring a difficult 2026 FIFA World Cup with Portugal, former Portugal manager Fernando Santos believes the 31-year-old remains one of the world’s finest footballers and expects him to flourish under Jose Mourinho.

Bernardo Silva’s move to Real Madrid has sparked plenty of debate following his arrival at the Santiago Bernabeu.

While some questioned whether the veteran midfielder is still capable of performing at the highest level, Santos has dismissed those doubts and insists Real Madrid have signed one of the best midfielders in world football.

Having worked closely with Bernardo Silva for years at international level, Santos believes Real Madrid have secured far more than just a technically gifted footballer.

“As a footballer, everyone already knows him; he has immense quality and sees the game in a way that very few players in the world do.

“His standards as a player are so high that it’s hard to say he’s on the same level as a person, but in his case it’s true. He’s considerate, polite, friendly and always has a smile on his face.

“On a personal and human level, he’s one of the finest footballers I’ve ever met in my entire career. Real Madrid have signed a phenomenal player in every sense of the word,” Santos told AS.

Despite turning 31, Santos believes the Bernardo Silva Real Madrid transfer has come at exactly the right time.

“From what I’ve seen of him this season at City, I’d say so. I know him inside out, and I’m convinced he still has some very good years ahead of him.

“He’s still at the peak of his career and he’s going to prove it. For me, he remains one of the best in the world.

Bernardo Silva had an underwhelming World Cup campaign. (Photo by Dan Mullan/Getty Images)

“I think Real Madrid have made the right move in signing him and he’s going to contribute a great deal to their team.”

One of the biggest talking points following the Real Madrid transfer has been how Bernardo Silva will fit into a squad that already features Kylian Mbappe, Vinicius Jr. and Jude Bellingham.

Santos believes that will never be an issue.

“Great players know how to adapt to every situation and circumstance in a match.

“He’s very good at that and won’t have any trouble playing wherever he’s asked to. I’m sure Mourinho has a specific role in mind for him, and Bernardo will fulfil it perfectly,” he explained.

Competition for places is expected to be fierce at the Bernabeu, but Santos believes Mourinho signed the Portuguese international because he sees him playing a major role.

“He’s used to competing, and his quality will see him earn his place. I think he’s destined to play a very important role; that’s why Mourinho has signed him.

“Perhaps he might play closer to the right flank and drift into the centre from time to time, but wherever he’s deployed, he’ll do brilliantly thanks to his tactical intelligence and his reading of the game.

“Mourinho is shrewd, highly skilled in strategic concepts, and he’ll bring out the best in him.”

Santos also rejected suggestions that the pressure of playing for Real Madrid could affect the midfielder, especially after two disappointing seasons for the club.

Fernando Santos has backed Bernardo Silva. (Photo by Leonardo Fernandez/Getty Images)

When asked whether Bernardo Silva shares similarities with Luka Modric, he preferred not to make direct comparisons.

“I don’t really like comparing footballers because each one has their own characteristics, but it’s clear that they share similarities in their style of play.

“I’d say the two main ones, which they both have in common, are their immense technical ability and their commitment to the team.”

He also expressed complete confidence that the Portuguese star will embrace the challenge of playing at the Santiago Bernabéu.

“That won’t be a problem for him at all. He has plenty of experience, he’s played for some of the biggest clubs and he’ll know how to cope with the exacting demands Real Madrid will place on him.

“Bernardo will rise to the challenge.”

Santos finished with another glowing endorsement of his national compatriot.

“I am. I think the Madrid fans – who are very knowledgeable and have seen the world’s best footballers pass through their stadium – will love Bernardo Silva.

“I don’t think he’ll let anyone down and that he’ll more than live up to the expectations placed on him.

“I’m sure he’s highly motivated because he loves a challenge, and this one is a massive one. He’s a magnificent player, of the highest calibre. And he’ll prove it in white,” he concluded.

title: K-1 cruiserweight champ announces transition to MMA
description: One of kickboxing's fastest-rising talents appears ready to test himself in a new arena.
fullText:
K-1 cruiserweight champion Thian de Vries revealed via Instagram Stories that he plans to transition to mixed martial arts after completing two more kickboxing bouts following his latest win in Japan.

Thian de Vries on what’s next after kickboxing: ‘MMA is the future’
 The Dutchman, who has enjoyed a meteoric rise in kickboxing, recently announced that he plans to move to MMA soon. While de Vries did not reveal when or with which promotion he intends to make his MMA debut, he indicated that he first intends to fulfill two remaining kickboxing commitments before making the switch.

 “I am so f*cking happy and grateful guys,” the 25-year-old wrote in a new Instagram story. “It's been a tough year for me with injuries, a mental struggle sometimes. King of the Jungle is back, 2 more kickboxing fights and afterwards full focus on MMA, MMA is the future.”

 He’ll look to follow in the footsteps of former Ultimate Fighting Championship champions Alex Pereira and Israel Adesanya, who enjoyed successful transitions from kickboxing to MMA.

title: Report: Man United are leading the race to sign World Cup midfielder
description: Manchester United Hold Early Edge in Manu Kone Transfer RaceManchester United appear to be in a promising position as the market for Manu Kone begins to develop, with L’Equipe reporting that the Fra...
fullText:
Manchester United appear to be in a promising position as the market for Manu Kone begins to develop, with L’Equipe reporting that the France international is giving serious thought to the next step in his career.

Kone, now 25, comes into this summer at an important moment. After two solid seasons with Roma, where he has made 82 appearances, and after helping the Italian side secure third place with 73 points last term, the midfielder has strengthened his standing. His World Cup ended painfully with France’s 2-0 semi-final defeat to Spain, and the emotional aftermath underlined how deeply he had invested in the campaign. Yet that tournament also seems to have reinforced the sense that he is ready for a bigger platform.

The central point is clear. Kone wants to test himself at a higher level, ideally with a club carrying strong European ambitions. Inter Milan have tracked the situation for some time, according to reporting in Italy, but the strongest likelihood now appears to be a move to the Premier League.

Among the English clubs with interest, Manchester United are described as having something of a head start. Initial talks have taken place, although the situation has not yet moved into advanced territory. That reflects a wider theme in this summer window, with clubs across Europe operating carefully after a market slowed by the World Cup.

United’s recruitment team are understood to be assessing a number of midfield options, and Kone is firmly among the priority names on that shortlist. The club’s stance is measured. There is a determination to avoid overpaying and to weigh up each move properly before accelerating. In that context, interest should be taken seriously, but not yet viewed as definitive.

From Kone’s side, there is believed to be genuine openness to Manchester United. The attraction of English football has been there for some time, and his current market is strongest in the Premier League. Arsenal have also been mentioned previously, while two other English sides are said to have expressed interest, even if their identities remain undisclosed.

The player would prefer clarity sooner rather than later, particularly after a demanding international summer. Whether that arrives quickly is another matter. For now, Manchester United seem well placed, but this remains a transfer race still in its early stages.

As a Manchester United supporter, this is exactly the sort of report that grabs the attention. Manu Kone feels like a player who fits what the squad has lacked in midfield for too long, energy, athleticism, intensity and the ability to cover ground without sacrificing quality on the ball. If the club are serious about refreshing the engine room, he looks like a very sensible target.

What stands out most is his apparent openness to the move. That matters. United have spent too many windows chasing players who needed convincing, and that rarely ends well. If Kone sees Old Trafford as the right next step, then the club should recognise that opportunity and act decisively.

At the same time, there is some reassurance in the idea that United do not want to rush or overpay. Supporters have seen too many expensive deals made from panic rather than planning. A calmer process is welcome, as long as it does not become hesitation for its own sake.

From a football perspective, Kone could bring balance and drive. He looks ready for the Premier League, and his age suggests there is still another level to come. If United want midfielders who can compete physically, progress the ball and grow into major roles, this feels like one worth pushing hard for.

title: The day’s 5⃣ stories: Spain celebrate the World Cup, Ferrán’s future...
description: This Tuesday, July 21, still begins with the hangover from the World Cup, but also with the revival of a transfer market that has been quiet so far.So, to bring you up to speed, we’re going to tell ...
fullText:
This Tuesday, July 21, still begins with the hangover from the World Cup, but also with the revival of a transfer market that has been quiet so far.

So, to bring you up to speed, we’re going to tell you the five biggest stories of the day.

📸 Pablo Blazquez Dominguez - 2026 Getty Images

After winning their second star, the streets of Madrid were packed with celebrations for the world champions. The entire national team toured central Madrid showing off the trophy and receiving the support of their fans.

📸 David Ramos - 2026 Getty Images

As reported by 'L'Equipe', Luis Enrique has spoken personally with Ferrán Torres to explain PSG’s project to him and hopes he can sign for the Parisian side.

On the other hand, according to 'SPORT', Barcelona hope to renew his contract in the coming weeks, so there is set to be a tug-of-war over the goalscorer who won the World Cup for Spain.

📸 CHARLY TRIBALLEAU - AFP or licensors

Boca Juniors midfielder Leandro Paredes played in Argentina’s last three World Cup matches with a crack in his ribs. It was not leaked so that opponents would not find out.

📸 Luke Hales - 2026 Getty Images

After several rumors, such as the report from 'Romain Molina' claiming there was no 'feeling' between the coach, the Belgian FA, and the players, Rudi Garcia is no longer Belgium’s manager.

On the other hand, Van Bommel is expected to be the one to replace him in the role.

📸 OSCAR DEL POZO - AFP or licensors

'Fabrizio Romano' reported it in the last few hours: Rodri will receive a contract renewal offer from Manchester City, but he wants to join Real Madrid.

However, things are cool for now because Florentino Pérez is not convinced.

title: Bayern Munich exec Max Eberl understands treacherous terrain of the modern transfer game
description: The FC Bayern board member for sport is a grizzled veteran of the transfer window.
fullText:
Transfer fees have become one of football’s hottest talking points, with every transfer window bringing eye-popping figures, massive signing bonuses, and endless debate over whether the market has gone too far. Bayern Munich board member for sport Max Eberl recently offered his perspective on why the economics of transfers have evolved so dramatically.

“The sums involved have certainly changed, and in this age of transfer journalism, there’s more discussion about them, but bonuses have always played a role in such negotiations. Especially since the Bosman ruling in 1995, they’ve become increasingly important, and all parties involved play to their advantage. Many years ago, a player was usually only available on a free transfer if he was deemed surplus to requirements by the club. Now, many players simply take the risk of entering the final year of their contract and see what happens. This gives signing fees a particular significance,” Eberl told Sport1 journalist Stefan Kumberger (as captured by @iMiaSanMia).

Eberl’s comments highlight just how much the transfer landscape has shifted over the last three decades. While transfer fees continue to grab headlines, they are often just one piece of a much larger financial puzzle. Signing bonuses, agent commissions, loyalty payments, and performance-related incentives have become just as important as the headline fee itself.

His reference to the Bosman ruling is particularly noteworthy. That landmark decision fundamentally changed the balance of power between clubs and players, allowing footballers to leave at the end of their contracts without a transfer fee. As a result, clubs have increasingly found themselves competing with lucrative signing bonuses and salary packages rather than simply negotiating transfer fees with another club.

That trend has become even more pronounced in recent years. Rather than signing long-term extensions early, some elite players are willing to let their contracts run down, knowing they could command significantly larger financial packages as free agents. For buying clubs, paying a substantial signing bonus can still be more appealing than spending €80 million or €100 million on a transfer fee.

For Bayern Munich and clubs across Europe, adapting to this reality has become essential. The modern transfer market is no longer just about buying players—it is about navigating an increasingly complex financial ecosystem where timing, leverage, and contract strategy can be just as valuable as the transfer budget itself.
