title: Chaos Reveals Surprising Post-Match Message To Arman Tsarukyan
description: Colby Covington walked away impressed from his RAF 11 matchup versus Arman Tsarukyan. “Chaos” is an infamous trash talker and habitual line-stepper, yet the lead up to his high-profile RAF title match opposite Tsarukyan was fairly devoid of insults. Well, Covington spent time attacking fellow RAF 11 competitor Belal Muhammad, but he didn’t direct his […]
fullText:
Colby Covington walked away impressed from his RAF 11 matchup versus Arman Tsarukyan.

“Chaos” is an infamous trash talker and habitual line-stepper, yet the lead up to his high-profile RAF title match opposite Tsarukyan was fairly devoid of insults. Well, Covington spent time attacking fellow RAF 11 competitor Belal Muhammad, but he didn’t direct his venom at “Akhalkalakets.” Following a competitive match that saw Covington reverse a pair of takedowns to outpoint Tsarukyan (highlights here), Covington has nothing but positive things to say about the Armenian standout.

In fact, he’s now confident that Tsarukyan is destined for UFC gold.

“Unbelievable match. I told him, man, you’re gonna be the UFC Lightweight champ,” Covington explained Submission Radio. “I’m so convinced. No one’s gonna beat him at Lightweight, that’s a perfect division. Justin Gaethje has no chance. The pace, the wrestling, the way he mixes it up with everything — no one’s gonna stop him right now.”

He continued, “I was in peak form last night. It didn’t matter who was gonna be in there, I was going to get my hand raised because I wanted it so bad … but I felt how good Arman was. And I just let him know how much I respect him and how much I appreciate the opportunity to compete against him. I just know he’s going to go on to be the UFC Lightweight champion.”

Of course, it’s widely agreed that Tsarukyan — who has won five straight fights and was supposed to rematch Islam Makhachev for the belt in January 2025 — is an elite talent. Unfortunately, he’s yet to secure the next title shot or even a title eliminator matchup. He’s been much more active in RAF, but it feels like UFC is reluctant to book the noted hothead.

title: Bills newcomers wanted Tyler Bass' number, now the team needs his leg
description: The Bills are hoping Tyler Bass can return to form after missing all of 2025, and they also need to determine who their punter will be.
fullText:
This is the fifth in a 10-part series of questions facing the Buffalo Bills as they head into training camp at St. John Fisher University.

If Tyler Bass had known the Buffalo Bills were going to sign edge rusher Bradley Chubb in free agency, he would have been wise to hold off on relinquishing his No. 2 jersey so quickly to wide receiver DJ Moore.

He could have conducted a bidding war and pocketed a nice little bonus if both newcomers were adamant about purchasing No. 2 from the man who has worn it in Buffalo since 2020.

Alas, that did not happen. Moore joined the team March 11. Chubb arrived March 12 and, because he wore No. 2 with the Dolphins, was asked whether Bass had set a price for the number.

“Oh no, I’m already out of the running,” Chubb said with a smile shortly after he signed his free agent contract. “I got No. 9 so I’m good. That was my college number, I feel like it’s my ninth year and it’s all coming full circle.”

It’s unknown what Moore had to give up for No. 2, but Bass’ switch to No. 16 is the least of his concerns heading into the start of training camp as he tries to return to form after missing all of 2025 due to hip and groin injuries that required surgery.

According to Rotowire, the Bills lost 246 regular-season man games to injury last season which was the fifth-highest total in the league, and 17 of those belonged to Bass.

He first felt pain in training camp, tried to work through it and actually kicked in the preseason finale at Tampa Bay, making 3 of 4 field goals and both of his extra points.

But his discomfort returned in the days after the game and when things were not improving as the season opener against Baltimore approached, the team put him on injured reserve and signed 41-year-old free agent Matt Prater.

It could have been a bad outcome for Buffalo because in his first five seasons Bass made 84.5% of his field goal attempts which ranks 29th in NFL history, and 96.4% of his extra points which ranks 13th among active kickers who now have to attempt the try from 33 yards out.

But Prater stepped in and drilled a game-winner as time expired opening night to beat the Ravens, went on to make 18 of 20 field goals and 46 of 49 extra points, then made all 11 kicks he attempted in the postseason including a game-tying 50-yarder with five seconds remaining to send the Denver game to overtime. It was about as good a relief job as the Bills could have dreamed.

“Just grateful for the experience,” Prater said on the day the Bills cleaned out their lockers after losing the Broncos game, knowing his time with the team was likely done. “Got to come out here, short notice, be a part of this team, this organization that is, in my opinion, the best place I’ve ever been – top to bottom. Enjoyed every bit of it, and I’m sad it’s over.”

Prater has not signed with a new team but he hasn’t announced his retirement, either. Brandon Beane said back in February that he expects Bass to return as good as new and be the Bills kicker at least through the end of his contract in 2027 and perhaps well beyond, but he has Prater in his iPhone contacts just in case.

Bass’ performance this summer will be interesting to track, as will the punting competition between veteran Mitch Wishnowsky and rookie seventh-round pick Tommy Doman.

Like Prater, Wishnowsky rescued the Bills last season after they rolled through Brad Robbins and Cameron Johnston in the first month. Wishnowsky signed before the Week 5 Patriots game and stabilized the position for the rest of the season. His performance was mostly league average, but he got the job done.

The Bills re-signed him for 2026, but did so figuring they would pick someone in the draft and that turned out to be Doman.

Doman began his college career at Michigan, then transferred to Florida for his final two seasons. Last year he averaged 44 yards per punt, had only six touchbacks and forced 17 fair catches with 17 punts downed inside the 20.

“Tommy was a guy that we had on our draft board, and as Joe (has) said, we're looking for depth and competition everywhere,” Beane said at the end of the draft. “Mitch is a proven guy here. We wanted to go to camp with two punters, that's our plan.”

Sean McDermott placed heavy emphasis on the kicking game and new head coach Joe Brady needs to do the same. The first step would be making sure Bass is good as new, and the next would be choosing the right punter.

Sal Maiorana has covered the Buffalo Bills for more than four decades including 37 years as the full-time beat writer/columnist for the D&C. He has written numerous books about the history of the team, and he is also co-host of the BLEAV in Bills podcast/YouTube show. He can be reached at maiorana@gannett.com, and you can follow him on X @salmaiorana and on Bluesky @salmaiorana.bsky.social.

This article originally appeared on Rochester Democrat and Chronicle: Bills newcomers wanted Tyler Bass' number, now the team needs his leg

title: Mercedes engine claim piles pressure on FIA while Red Bull pushes for sweeping wing ban
description: Formula 1's political battle over the 2026 regulations has intensified on two fronts, with Mercedes team principal Toto Wolff making a striking admission about his team's power unit while Red Bull reportedly pushes the FIA to introduce a blanket ban on rotating rear-wing concepts. The developments come at a crucial stage of the season, with the FIA's second Assessment of Dynamic Unit Output (ADUO) measurement period set to close after the Hungarian Grand Prix.
fullText:
Formula 1's political battle over the 2026 regulations has intensified on two fronts, with Mercedes team principal Toto Wolff making a striking admission about his team's power unit while Red Bull reportedly pushes the FIA to introduce a blanket ban on rotating rear-wing concepts.

The developments come at a crucial stage of the season, with the FIA's second Assessment of Dynamic Unit Output (ADUO) measurement period set to close after the Hungarian Grand Prix. The deadline could prove pivotal in determining whether any intervention is required to balance performance before the sport enters its new engine era in 2026.

Speaking to Sky Sports, Wolff appeared to contradict the FIA's current assessment by declaring that Mercedes has "the most powerful engine."

The comment immediately attracted attention because the FIA's ADUO process was introduced specifically to monitor the competitiveness of manufacturers and identify whether any power unit supplier has gained a significant advantage.

Currently, the FIA assessments have not publicly established Mercedes as the outright benchmark, but instead identified Red Bull's co-produced with Ford engine as the most powerful, making Wolff's remark all the more noteworthy.

His comments prompted an immediate response from Red Bull's team principal Laurent Mekies, who urged the FIA to take the available data seriously before the current assessment window closes after Hungary.

According to Mekies, the governing body should ensure that any competitive imbalance is identified early enough to act if necessary, rather than waiting until the regulations are already in force.

The exchange adds another layer to the increasingly heated debate surrounding Formula 1's future power units. Concerns over energy deployment, engine parity and possible FIA intervention have dominated discussions for months, and Wolff's candid assessment is likely to increase scrutiny on Mercedes' development programme.

Ferrari's innovative rear wing design introduced during testing in Bahrain - Image: Race Pictures

At the same time, another political battle is unfolding over Formula 1's flexible rear-wing designs. According to reports, Red Bull has urged the FIA to go further than targeting individual concepts if changes are introduced. Instead of banning only specific solutions, the Milton Keynes-based team is reportedly lobbying for a blanket prohibition on all rotating rear-wing designs.

The debate follows increasing attention surrounding the so-called "McMacarena" concept, with Ferrari and McLaren as the teams most closely associated with innovative rear-wing movement under load, while Red Bull has struggled with the concept.

GPblog previously analysed the technical principles behind these designs, explaining how carefully controlled wing rotation can reduce drag on straights while remaining within current regulations. That analysis also highlighted why any FIA intervention could have implications well beyond a single team. And how Red Bull's design has caused problems.

If the governing body were to outlaw every rotating rear-wing concept rather than focusing on one particular interpretation, the competitive picture could shift dramatically. Such a decision would affect multiple teams currently developing similar aerodynamic solutions, rather than targeting a single design philosophy.

That would also leave Red Bull in an unusual position. With rivals continuing to refine concepts that could offer performance gains, the team is reportedly seeking a regulatory solution that applies equally across the grid instead of allowing alternative interpretations to survive.

With the Hungarian Grand Prix approaching, Formula 1 faces important decisions on both technical regulations and competitive fairness. Between questions over Mercedes' true engine performance and the growing dispute over rear-wing legality, the FIA is once again under pressure to prove that its regulatory framework can keep pace with the sport's rapidly evolving technology.

title: AFTER ISTANBUL, THERE IS ALWAYS ATHENS: AC MILAN AND PUMA UNVEIL THE NEW 2026/27 AWAY KIT
description: AC Milan and PUMA have unveiled today the new 2026/27 Away Kit. Classic white, the colour of the biggest occasions. A jersey that embodies the Club's winning mentality: the resilience of those who tur...
fullText:
AC Milan and PUMA have unveiled today the new 2026/27 Away Kit. Classic white, the colour of the biggest occasions. A jersey that embodies the Club's winning mentality: the resilience of those who turn every setback into the momentum to rise again and return to winning ways.

It is a mindset that transforms every setback into a new starting point - an idea AC Milan fans have distilled into a single phrase: "Dopo Istanbul c'è sempre Atene" (After Istanbul, there is always Athens). Printed on the back of the collar, these few words connect those who experienced the light of Athens after the darkness of Istanbul with the new generations who now carry that legacy forward.

The face and voice of the campaign is Filippo Inzaghi, a key figure in the 2007 final. Throughout his Rossoneri career, he embodied the mentality that has always defined the Club: obsessive preparation, an unrelenting hunger to win, and the ability to turn every setback into a new beginning. His story today is proof that this attitude transcends generations and stands the test of time.

Every element of the kit reinforces this message. The golden details and the laurel wrapped around the badge speak of a history of success that the Club intends to keep writing on the pitch. That never-surrender attitude takes shape in the modern Greek inscription "Δύναμη Ψυχής" (Dýnami Psychís, "strength of spirit") inside the collar of the Authentic jersey and in the phoenix wing that emerges from the fabric's weave. The symbol of a journey that never ends, and that grows stronger with every new challenge.

Maikel Oettle, Chief Revenue Officer of AC Milan, said: "Every element of this jersey embodies the identity and mentality that have defined this Club for more than a century. White is the colour that has carried that identity onto the world stage for generations, and wearing it today means carrying forward a story that is still being written. It means belonging to something that connects those who experienced our greatest nights with those who inherit that legacy."

Dominique Gauthier, PUMA VP Teamsport said: "The story this shirt tells is one of the most powerful in European football. This club has always found a way to rise when it mattered most, and we wanted this kit to carry that spirit forward. The phoenix wing, the laurel, the inscription: every element has a reason. We are proud to have created something that honours what this club has proven it is capable of."

The Authentic jersey is constructed with PUMA's ULTRAWEAVE fabric, an ultra-lightweight, precision-woven performance material engineered for unrestricted movement, combined with dryCELL and ThermoAdapt technologies to manage sweat and regulate body heat. The Replica jersey brings the same identity to the terraces and the streets, pairing the match-worn aesthetic with everyday wear, made with RE:FIBRE technology - 95% recycled polyester sourced from textile waste.

The new 2026/27 Away Kit will be available from 21 July at AC Milan Official Stores, store.acmilan.com, puma.com and at selected retailers worldwide.

title: 3 Explosive Reasons To Watch The Inner Circle 23 On July 24
description: This week's event puts gold up for grabs and a legends status on the line.
fullText:
Bangkok’s Lumpinee Stadium plays host to another blockbuster night when The Inner Circle 23 arrives live in Asia primetime, on Friday, July 24.

The card, which streams exclusively for members at live.onefc.com, features five kickboxing and Muay Thai battles, with plenty of world-class talent looking to make a statement inside the historic venue.

Here are three key reasons to tune in to this week’s edition of The Inner Circle.

With divisional king Prajanchai PongSuphan PK sidelined due to injury, two men at very different stages of their careers collide for the ONE Interim Strawweight Muay Thai World Title.

Sam-A Gaiyanghadao requires no introduction to fight fans. The 42-year-old Thai veteran built his legacy by capturing multiple belts across the sport’s most prestigious stadiums. He further elevated his legendary status by capturing the organization’s kickboxing and Muay Thai crown at strawweight after holding the ONE Flyweight Muay Thai World Championship.

Representing Evolve MMA and carrying a staggering 378-50 record, his world-class arsenal sharpened over three decades has made him a nightmare matchup for virtually every opponent placed in front of him. Most recently, he blitzed Elmehdi “The Sniper” El Jamari inside two rounds, the latest reminder that his pursuit for 26 pounds of gold remains very much open at 42.

Standing in his path in this headliner attraction is Aliff Sor Dechapan, a young star ready to craft the most memorable chapter of his career. The 22-year-old has been a revelation since arriving on the global stage, pairing relentless forward pressure with heavy, speedy combinations that have overwhelmed far more experienced fighters.

He enters this clash riding a five-fight winning streak. A victory here would not only make him a ONE World Champion, but it would also make him the first-ever athlete from Malaysia to hoist the promotion’s 26 pounds of gold.

Whoever leaves Bangkok victorious will secure a unification war against Prajanchai, adding another layer of stakes to an already must-watch main event pitting legacy against ambition.

“The Angel Warrior” Panpayak Jitmuangnon has spent his career proving he belongs among Muay Thai’s very best. That pedigree has translated seamlessly into kickboxing in ONE, where the seven-time Muay Thai World Champion remains undefeated.

His first two victories in the discipline arrived at flyweight, before he made the jump to bantamweight and continued his roll with a hard-fought split-decision win over Othman “The Ironside” Rhouni in the co-main event of ONE Friday Fights 155 this past May.

Boasting a combined striking record of 251-44, the 30-year-old’s game is built on composure and technical mastery, a product of a career spent adapting across disciplines while continuing to beat world-class competition at the highest level.

Looking to prevent “The Angel Warrior” from flying up the charts is a hungry, ambitious talent eager to make a name for himself at the Thai’s expense. Anuar “Bad Boy” Cisneros brings youthful aggression and a genuine desire to make a statement in this bantamweight kickboxing battle at The Inner Circle 23.

The 25-year-old Mexican, who represents Silk Muay Thai, has earned this opportunity in his own right, and sharing the ring with a fighter of Panpayak’s stature is exactly the kind of stage he has been chasing. He appears fully intent on making the most of it.

However, should the Thai add another impressive victory to his ledger, it would send a real message to the rest of the bantamweight kickboxing division currently ruled by Jonathan “The General” Haggerty, who will defend his coveted belt against Hiroki Akimoto at ONE SAMURAI 4 this October.

Few debutants arrive with as much weight on their shoulders as Ryujin “Shindo” Nasukawa, who opens his ONE account against Thai slugger Thongpoon PK Saenchai in a strawweight kickboxing tiff.

The younger brother of retired kickboxing icon Tenshin Nasukawa, the 20-year-old carries a 17-3 record and an already decorated resume that includes the ISKA K-1 Strawweight World Championship and RISE Flyweight Title.

His three-year, 14-fight winning streak only came to an end this past June, and he now arrives in Bangkok determined to launch an entirely new run on martial arts’ grandest platform.

He has made no secret of his desire to build a legacy entirely his own, separate from the shadow cast by his brother’s flawless 42-0 career. His assignment at The Inner Circle 23, however, will be no walk in the park.

Thongpoon brings 80 career victories and a flair for theatrics to match his devastating finishing ability, having racked up four knockout wins since arriving in the organization. The PK Saenchai Muaythaigym representative is eager to move past recent adversity and remind the promotion of the danger he still poses, particularly against a hyped newcomer looking to make an immediate name for himself.

As such, fans should expect an all-action war between two fighters with everything to prove. For Thongpoon, spoiling the party would reaffirm his own place in the division’s pecking order. In the opposite corner, victory for Ryujin would help him deliver on the hype and mark the first major step in a career he hopes will one day stand entirely on its own.

title: Why was Hamilton penalised but not Leclerc? - F1 Q&A
description: BBC Sport F1 correspondent Andrew Benson answers your latest questions before the Hungarian Grand Prix.
fullText:
Mercedes' Kimi Antonelli won the Belgian Grand Prix from pole position on Sunday to extend his lead at the top of the drivers' championship to 45 points.

Antonelli's team-mate George Russell retired from the race following an opening-lap collision with Ferrari's Lewis Hamilton.

Russell has now dropped to third in the standings, with Hamilton replacing him in second place after finishing fourth.

Ferrari's Charles Leclerc was second behind Antonelli and Red Bull's Max Verstappen completed the podium in third.

BBC Sport F1 correspondent Andrew Benson answers your latest questions after the race at Spa-Francorchamps.

Antonelli near-perfect but too much drama for Hamilton - driver ratings

Russell 'numb to disappointment' after bitter blow to title hopes

If Lewis Hamilton received a five-second time penalty for what appeared to me to be a racing incident between himself and George Russell, then how did Charles Leclerc get away with the Oscar Piastri incident where he clearly didn't leave enough room for the McLaren driver? - John

The stewards' justifications for these two different incidents were based on a number of factors, many related to the Formula 1 drivers' guidelines.

In the case of the collision between Hamilton and Russell on the first lap, the decision stated that Russell had got his car far enough alongside Hamilton's Ferrari on the outside as they approached the corner to be making a legitimate overtaking attempt and therefore was "entitled to racing room".

They went on to say that the collision between the two cars was triggered by Hamilton's car sliding and that he hit Russell's car despite "applying corrective steering".

Russell "remained within the available racing room and the collision was caused by [Hamilton] being unable to maintain sufficient separation".

Hamilton was therefore "wholly at fault for the collision".

The normal penalty in these circumstances is 10 seconds. This was reduced to five in Hamilton's case because he "acknowledged the presence of [Russell], attempted to leave appropriate racing room and made a genuine attempt to avoid contact".

In addition, the incident was not caused by "an aggressive or deliberate manoeuvre", and it was on the opening lap, when more leeway is applied.

As for the incident between Leclerc and Piastri, again the racing rules were applied to the letter.

The stewards ruled that Piastri was not far enough alongside the Ferrari to be entitled to the same treatment as Russell.

This was because while he had "some overlap", Piastri's "front axle… was not ahead of the front axle [of Leclerc] and there was no possibility of completing an overtake from that position".

Objectively, it's hard to disagree with their conclusion up to that point.

The next part of the ruling was more subjective.

"[Leclerc] did not deliberately crowd [Piastri] off the edge of the track. [He] followed the racing line before the entry to Turn Five by moving slightly to the left… and side to side contact occurred between the two cars".

There was an interesting episode in the green room before the podium. This incident played out on the television while Leclerc was sitting watching with Max Verstappen and Kimi Antonelli.

Leclerc's eyes widened and he sucked in a breath as he watched the contact, and he said: "This one was…"

Verstappen interrupted and said: "Was calculated." Leclerc looked at him, they both laughed and then Leclerc sat back looking sheepish and put his cap over his face.

It's clear he recognised the move was on the edge, and a case can be made that he did not comply with the rule that says a driver defending their position by changing line must leave a car's width on the outside if they move back to the racing line.

McLaren team principal Andrea Stella was asked for his view after the race.

He said he had not yet reviewed the stewards' reasoning, but added: "When the cars are staying alongside each other for such a long time, we should make sure that the car that is not on the racing line, because it's defending the inside, does leave enough space, because if there's a contact that is not controlled, then the crash is going to be very big."

I'm sure I read somewhere that this weekend was a record attendance for a Belgian Grand Prix. Given that, and the clear love that drivers and fans share for Spa, it seems ridiculous it won't be on the calendar every year, while far less popular circuits are remaining. Does the FIA care about the views of the fans and drivers or is it all about what races pay the most? - Tom

F1 did say that the attendance at the Belgian Grand Prix this year was an all-time record at 400,000 over the weekend as a whole.

And on a point of fact regarding the question, the calendar is compiled by F1, not the FIA, which merely rubber stamps the schedule at its world council.

In determining which races feature, F1 weighs a number of different factors. Of course, finances are one of them, but money is not everything.

Some races pay smaller fees than others, and one of the reasons for this can be that they are considered to have a greater intrinsic value to the sport.

This would apply to Spa, which has a history and circuit layout that surpasses the vast majority of the rest of the calendar.

However, it is the case that the historic European classic tracks such as Spa and Silverstone - and other events such as Austin - simply cannot afford to pay the same fees as races funded by nation states using them for PR purposes, such as Saudi Arabia and Bahrain, for example.

This is not an easy conundrum to resolve. F1 wants tracks such as Spa on the calendar for all the obvious reasons, but they also have to make financial sense.

It is in trying to find an acceptable compromise to this scenario that the latest situation has arisen, with Spa hosting the Belgian Grand Prix for four out of six years of its current contract.

That means that in 2028 and 2030 Spa will be replaced by another race.

Of course, from the point of view of anyone who appreciates the sport and what it's about, that's not ideal. But it's better than having no Spa at all.

Why are Mercedes struggling to understand George Russell's straight-line deficit? They must have all the data on his battery deployment, ICE output, e-motor power, plus how much drag his aero and car set-up, such as rake angle, generates - Sean

At the Belgian Grand Prix, Mercedes experienced unexplained behaviour from their power unit.

Russell suffered for this, by having a mystery lack of energy over a lap in qualifying. It cost him some but not all of his deficit to team-mate Kimi Antonelli. Mercedes team principal Toto Wolff put the loss from the engine at about 0.2-0.25 seconds.

A pretty much identical thing happened at McLaren to Oscar Piastri in relation to Lando Norris.

Both drivers also experienced a shortfall of energy on the first lap that contributed both to Russell's incident with Hamilton and Antonelli being passed by Max Verstappen on the approach to Eau Rouge.

As Wolff said: "All Mercedes engines had a lack of energy out of Turn One so that wasn't only George. It affected him more than Kimi but also Kimi had that issue to a certain degree."

The one in the race was, Wolff said, "a new issue".

Mercedes said they did not understand why either situation had happened.

The fundamental issue is that the latest F1 hybrid engines are highly complex, as are the rules by which they have to be operated, and the manufacturers and teams are still learning about them.

One of the impacts of this is that small nuances of driving can have a disproportionately large impact on energy availability. It's one of the reasons why the drivers do not like these engines.

McLaren team principal Andrea Stella, among the best technical communicators in the sport, has talked about this extensively this season.

At the weekend in Spa he said: "To operate these power units, you don't operate them in an open-loop way, whereby I would be able to have an off-line simulation and say, 'Oh, that's how the electrical power will be deployed and it will always be identical to itself'.

"There's a large component of the controller that operates the power unit that happens with live calculations and the power unit forward-thinks, forward-calculates what it has to do. There's a lot that happens as the car goes.

"This is why it's not so easy to understand what kind of calculations have been made as the car was going.

"I'm sure we will understand the reason, but at the same time you need to be able to anticipate and make sure that the operation of the power unit unfolds as expected.

"Because this is not only for the influence it has in the power-limited sections, [and] in the straights, but also because it affects the braking points.

"Because if you have an additional amount of harvest before braking, then you are approaching the braking 10km/h slower and your braking point changes.

"This is quite difficult to master for the drivers, and it's one of the reasons why they talk about the difficulties of exploiting the power unit from a driving point of view.

"It's not only to get the most out of the power unit, but also because the variations of the power unit affect the references as you approach a corner.

"But the variations that are significantly relevant are so small that they may happen from one lap to the other, even if you have learned from the previous laps."

This complexity also explains why McLaren have been frustrated this season by some aspects of their relationship with Mercedes, particularly in terms of how much information they have been given about the operation of the engine.

As Stella said: "It's only now, and we are mid-season, that we are starting to have the required tools in order to be able to anticipate, optimise, and study these sensitivities."

Why are drivers allowed to sacrifice their qualifying session to provide a tow to their team-mate? It is understandable during a race, but it feels against the spirit of qualifying - Sylvie

This is a reference to the way Red Bull ran qualifying in Belgium.

Isack Hadjar gave Max Verstappen a tow in the final session that made a difference of about 0.3secs. It allowed Verstappen to qualify on the front row rather than sixth.

If this was to happen routinely, then there might well be consequences.

But this particular station was entirely understandable, and completely normal.

Hadjar had a 30-place grid penalty for exceeding his allowance of power unit components. So, having made it into Q3, he was only ever going to be starting the race at the back, no matter what lap time he set.

In those circumstances, it made complete sense for Red Bull to ask him to give Verstappen a slipstream to maximise his chances.

Antonelli near-perfect but too much drama for Hamilton - driver ratings

title: Roma interested in Mbaye on loan from PSG
description: Roma is finalizing the deal for Crysencio Summerville with West Ham, but the incoming transfers to improve their attack don’t end there. Gian Piero Gasperini was clear in his press conference: he’...
fullText:
Roma is finalizing the deal for Crysencio Summerville with West Ham, but the incoming transfers to improve their attack don’t end there.

Gian Piero Gasperini was clear in his press conference: he’s looking for two attacking wingers, despite the departure of Matias Soulé, which is far from a given.

Among the players being monitored is PSG’s Ibrahim Mbaye. The 2008-born player is locked into the French club, facing stiff competition: Dembele, Barcola, Kvaratskhelia, and Doué. This is why the French club could part ways with the Senegalese talent on loan.

Mbaye stood out during the World Cup with a stunning goal against France, and not just any national team. The Senegal winger didn’t miss a single World Cup match: 155 minutes and one goal.

As Corriere dello Sport reports, Roma isn’t the only club interested in the 2008-born player; Borussia Dortmund, Tottenham Hotspur, Leipzig, and Atlético Madrid are also monitoring him.

The deal could be a loan deal with an option to buy for €25-30 million, with a counter-purchase option to PSG for €35-40 million. The French club’s winger finished the season with three goals and two assists in 30 games: 1,152 minutes, 101 of which were in the Champions League.

title: Ben Askren had the lead in final wrestling match after double lung transplant; then his body gave out.
description: Former Ultimate Fighting Championship fighter Ben Askren says his body simply ran out of gas in the final wrestling match of his decorated career.
fullText:
Former Ultimate Fighting Championship fighter Ben Askren says his body simply ran out of gas in the final wrestling match of his decorated career.

 Just over a year after surviving a life-threatening bout of pneumonia that resulted in a double lung transplant, the former UFC welterweight contender returned to the mat at RAF 11 in Milwaukee, where he dropped a 6-3 decision to ex-UFC champion Belal Muhammad.

Askren had a dream start after taking a lead through the opening two periods. However, Muhammad rallied in the third, scoring a takedown and reversal before pulling away late. Reflecting on the match, Askren told FloWrestling that his body could not keep up.

 “The match was going relatively well,” he recalled. “At the end of the second period, when I walked over to my corner, I’m like, ‘sh*t! My legs have nothing left.’ I’m gonna have a hard time being in my stance. I’m gonna have a hard time not getting pushed around.”

 Muhammad then raised the pace of the match, which further caused problems for Askren.

 “He came out really hard, and I was like, ‘sh*t! My body doesn’t have it left in me.’ I did the best I could,” he added. “I knew my legs don’t have any more.”

 This defeat marked the end of Askren’s competitive wrestling career. He confirmed this to Chael Sonnen in the post-match interview after his match.

 “For sure, it’s my last time on the mat,” he said. “I couldn’t find a better place to do this. I worked hard over the last year. It was tough. There were times I couldn’t feed myself; I couldn’t stand up or go to the bathroom.”

What a legend @Benaskren pic.twitter.com/Wur9Se527X— FloWrestling (@FloWrestling) July 20, 2026

Ben Askren just retired after his wrestling match against Belal Muhammad

 “Every single day I wake up and thank God for the chance to do it one more time.” pic.twitter.com/9QBe8n6Dmt— Happy Punch (@HappyPunch) July 19, 2026

title: Lionel Messi’s Wife Sends Heartfelt Message After World Cup Final Loss
description: After what was likely Lionel Messi’s final World Cup appearance for Argentina, Antonela Roccuzzo shared a touching post about her husband. The post Lionel Messi’s Wife Sends Heartfelt Message After World Cup Final Loss appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
Although Argentina fell short of a second consecutive World Cup victory on Sunday, Lionel Messi had no reason to hang his head—a sentiment shared by his teammates, fans and most of all, his wife.

After what was likely Messi’s final World Cup appearance for La Albiceleste, Antonela Roccuzzo shared a two-photo post featuring a teary-eyed image of her husband alongside one of his teammates tossing him into the air. It was her way of suggesting that the good comes with the bad, while celebrating just how proud she was of him.

The photos were accompanied by a lengthy message directed to Messi.

“You will always be the best, @leomessi,” she beegan.

“Not just because of your talent, but because you have never stopped being yourself. Because no matter what happens, you never give up; you always fight to the very end and give it your all until the final second,” the caption read in Spanish (h/t NYPost).

“That strength, that mindset, and the way you pick yourself up time and time again are what make you unique. 🤍 Thank you for showing us every day that true success is built through hard work, sacrifice, and perseverance—without ever losing sight of who you really are.”

Antonela went on to praise the always-humble Messi for being a model citizen and a role model to their three children and millions of young fans across the world.

“You are the best example for our children and an inspiration to millions of people,” she continued “I admire you more than words can express, and I feel immensely proud to walk through life by your side. I love you so much. 🤍”

While Messi has yet to officially announce his retirement from international football, his social media post following the loss hinted at just that.

“The pain is immense, and it will take time for this wound to heal,” he wrote on IG.

Much like his wife, Messi cherished the good times leading into the World Cup final, which saw Argentina lose 1-0 to Spain in extra time.

“But I also hold on to all the good things. Today it is difficult to appreciate what we accomplished, but this group reached two consecutive World Cup finals,” he wrote, beaming with pride about his country’s accomplishments.

Messi went on to thank his teammates and fans for the wonderful journey

“Thank you from the bottom of my heart for every greeting and every message,” he added. “Once again, we managed to unite as a country and stand together, sharing the immense pride of being Argentine.”

title: IDP Prospects Flying Under The Radar
description: Steve Hungarter makes his debut with The Huddle and discusses five of his favorite undervalued IDP prospects.
fullText:
In the television show, "American Pickers," we have learned that one man's garbage is another one's treasure and that one person's forgotten item sitting in an old barn can become a prized possession.

Early in the offseason, IDP managers become treasure hunters.

The obvious IDP studs are already in plain sight: a couple of All-Pros, a sack leader and a few green-dot linebackers. Everyone thinks they know value. Everyone wants them. With that said, championships are rarely won by collecting the most recognizable names.

Here are five undervalued players in line with IDP:

Did you know the top eight elite players at the top of their defensive line position last year in pass-rush win rate were as follows: Will Anderson (25.3%), Myle Garrett (24.6%), Aidan Hutchinson (22.9%), Micah Parsons (22.6%), Nick Bosa (20%) Trey Hendrickson (20%) and Josh Hines-Allen (19%). Yet, Hines-Allen will go a round or two later than any of these names in most drafts. Even more interesting is if you break this down to true pass-rush sets (a stat designed to take away screens, play action, RPOs and double teams), Allen finished as the third-best defensive lineman - trailing Garrett and Anderson.

IDP Fun Fact: Hines-Allen was second in pressures last season and owns a 15.8% pressure rate over the last three seasons.

While it is true he only had eight sacks last year, impact "splash" plays are fluky. One player is being drafted as a DL2 with DL1 upside that no one is talking about. Hines-Allen is that player.

Remember that true pass-rush stat I mentioned earlier? Well, Uche is one of the better pure pass rushers in the league. What is really surprising is that you can get him almost everywhere off waivers.

IDP Fun Fact: Uche was fifth in true pass-rush win rate only behind Garrett, Anderson, Hines-Allen and Hutchinson. Now that is good company.

Why is he not owned in almost every league then? He is more of a journeyman at this point, as he has only logged more than 250 snaps in a season twice. One of those years was in 2022 when he posted 11.5 sacks with the Patriots.

As for this season, he will line up opposite Chop Robinson for the Dolphins and should see plenty of opportunity with the Miami offense expected to be one of the worst in the league. He is essentially a free player that no one is thinking about in 2026. Consider him free candy.

Continuing with the theme of buried ADP prospects, one of the latest round draft picks you could make is on Carter. Usually, green-dot defenders are not handed out this early in the offseason. That is not the case here.

Not only did the Bengals announce Carter as their green-dot, but they also did not add anyone in the offseason to contend with him or fellow linebacker Demetrius Knight. As a rookie, Carter finished third amongst rookies in total tackles and 41st overall with 106 tackles (53 solo).

As logic dictates, it should only get better from here. Carter is a player you could get in the latest of deep leagues. Best of all, he is cheap.

Elliss returns to the team he started his career with after finishing LB23 in Atlanta under 1-2-3 IDP scoring last season. He is the direct replacement for Demario Davis, who finished as the LB27 in the same system last season. This is a simple math problem: one hand replaces the other. The math is simple: one linebacker's volume leaves, another linebacker's volume steps in. Sometimes, IDP analysis really isn't fantasy algebra; it comes with fewer pencils and more missed tackles.

I think IDP folks are getting this one wrong, and the market is in dire need of a reset. Twitter streets are talking about the arrival of Jaquan Brisker, but he plays more of a free-zone role. It won't affect Elliott. They also talk about Jalen Ramsey, who again is assigned the nickel role and occasional safety snaps. Neither of these players/roles affect Elliott. Before anyone starts talking about Joey Porter Jr., he plays outside corner. Elliott's box role should remain intact and the preseason will show us his value soon enough.

As a reminder, Elliott produced 38 tackles, a forced fumble and an interception in five games before an injury ended his season in 2025. In 2024, he converted 108 out of a possible 111 tackle opportunities (97.3%). It is not just the 108 tackles that have me interested, but also the 61 solo stops. Very few players can play a unique box role like that and produce at that level in consecutive seasons. Elliott is going to destroy his current ADP; he is another steal to grab in drafts.

Winston is a player I have mentioned a few times this offseason. He only missed one career tackle in college (Penn State). He delivered mixed results last season. With that said, new defensive coordinator Gus Bradley loves playing single-high safety and his role is better suited as a box defender. He is an elite box defender while being a liability in coverage. This is a deep stash but one worth making.

This article originally appeared on The Huddle: IDP Prospects Flying Under The Radar

title: Barcelona midfielder Gavi shares heartfelt message after World Cup glory: ‘I owe a great deal to everyone at La Masia’
description: Spain’s historic FIFA World Cup triumph continues to produce unforgettable moments, and Barcelona midfielder Gavi has now shared one of the most emotional stories to emerge from the celebrations. J...
fullText:
Spain’s historic FIFA World Cup triumph continues to produce unforgettable moments, and Barcelona midfielder Gavi has now shared one of the most emotional stories to emerge from the celebrations.

Just hours after helping Spain defeat Argentina 1-0 in the 2026 FIFA World Cup final, the 21-year-old reflected on his remarkable journey from a young footballer in Los Palacios to a world champion.

The midfielder shared a heartfelt social media post, where he thanked the people who influenced his career while looking back on the sacrifices that helped him reach football’s biggest stage.

Following Spain’s victory over Argentina, Gavi shared two photographs that perfectly captured his football journey.

One showed him playing as a youngster for La Liara Balompie in his hometown of Los Palacios, while the other featured him kissing the FIFA World Cup trophy after Spain were crowned world champions.

Accompanying the images was an emotional message that quickly resonated with supporters across the football world.

“May no one wake me from this dream – the dream of a boy from Los Palacios who dreamed of winning the World Cup with his country…”

The Barcelona midfielder also reflected on the setbacks he overcame before reaching the pinnacle of international football.

After battling injuries during his young career, Gavi admitted that every difficult moment had been worthwhile as he celebrated becoming a Spain World Cup winner at just 21 years of age.

Gavi made sure to acknowledge everyone who helped him along the way, explaining the role his family, close friends and former clubs played in his development.

Gavi has thanked everyone who influenced his career. (Photo by David Ramos/Getty Images)

The Spanish international thanked Real Betis, where his football journey began, before reserving special praise for FC Barcelona and La Masia, the academy that helped transform him into one of Europe’s brightest midfielders.

“I owe a great deal to everyone at La Masia who welcomed me from day one, and to the coaches and staff at FC Barcelona, who helped me grow as a footballer and as a person, and enabled me to become a world champion,” he wrote.

The Barcelona academy graduate also dedicated part of his message to his grandmother, thanking her for “that extra strength you gave me over the last few months.”

Although Gavi was not a regular starter during the tournament, he still contributed whenever called upon by Spain manager Luis de la Fuente.

The midfielder played 76 minutes across the competition, including a start against Cape Verde and a late appearance in the knockout victory over Austria.

title: Good news as Liverpool beat Bayern Munich to sign "new Kaka"
description: Liverpool has become a top destination for emerging young talents from all over the world. Rio Ngumoha is showing exactly what kind of pathway exists for the best young British prospects - set for a k...
fullText:
Liverpool has become a top destination for emerging young talents from all over the world.

Rio Ngumoha is showing exactly what kind of pathway exists for the best young British prospects - set for a ket role under Andoni Iraola next season aged only 17.

As well as securing big-money deals for Jeremy Jacquet and Giovanni Leoni the future of the backline has also been safeguarded by under-the-radar signings like Mor Talla Ndiaye and Ifeanyi Ndukwe.

The process is not limited to defenders only with Liverpool recruiters hard at work scouring the globe for the best young talent.

Of course overseas players are not permitted to officially sign for Liverpool until the age of 18 no matter what age they are identified.

In the case of Samuel Martinez for example he will be joining the club once he turns 18 next year. The Colombian playmaker, 17, will make the move from Atletico Nacional in his homeland next summer - for an initial fee of around £750k.

Martinez has been impressive at underage levels for his country - leading to Liverpool and other European clubs coming forward with their interest.

He jetted into England over the weekend to complete a medical and is expected to play a key first-team role for his original club before embarking on a Premier League adventure in 2027.

The news has been reported by TZ in the German media - where Bayern Munich were also thought to be in contention for Martinez’s signature.

The Bavarians have got the ideal mentor for the young schemer in Luis Diaz - who moved from Liverpool to FC Hollywood in 2025.

But Bayern are now regretting missing out on Martinez - whose talents have been talked up in the TZ report. Indeed it goes as far as describing Martinez as “the new Kaka” owing to his particular footballing characteristics.

Liverpool therefore look like they have signed an absolute gem - one of the best-rated young players on the South American continent. The 2024/25 Premier League champions have stolen a march on several top European rivals in order to land a deal.

Let’s hope he can go on to deliver - first for the youth and underage sides before making the step-up to the first team.

title: Red Sox ‘Very Interested’ In Nationals’ Breakout Infielder
description: Saying the Boston Red Sox are on a heater would be an understatement–and Nationals’ Luis Garcia Jr. might be the piece to push them over the edge. Recently, @dugoutreport made a post on Instagram, talking about the Red Sox’s trade deadline aspirations. View this post on Instagram They wrote: “The Red Sox continue to search for […] The post Red Sox ‘Very Interested’ In Nationals’ Breakout Infielder appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
Saying the Boston Red Sox are on a heater would be an understatement–and Nationals’ Luis Garcia Jr. might be the piece to push them over the edge.

Recently, @dugoutreport made a post on Instagram, talking about the Red Sox’s trade deadline aspirations.

They wrote: “The Red Sox continue to search for upgrades ahead of the trade deadline. According to Héctor Gómez, Boston is showing “a lot of interest” in Nationals infielder Luis García Jr. The 26-year-old would give the Red Sox another young, controllable bat as they continue to push for a postseason spot. Would Luis García Jr. be a good fit in Boston? Via x/BOSSportsGordo.”

Garcia Jr., 26, still has team control remaining and is in the midst of a career year. This season, he sports an .857 OPS and has anchored a suddenly potent Nationals’ lineup.

Throughout his career, Garcia Jr. has been a threat–he’s just starting to condense the success. Fewer slumps, more contact, and elite power production have him positioned for the best season of his career.

Nationals’ announcer Kevin Fransend was quoted as saying that this is “the best” Garcia Jr. will likely get.

With that being the case, whoever acquires him may have to overpay. That’s great for the Nats, but could hinder Boston’s pursuit.

Jeff Passan: “The Boston Red Sox have won 14 consecutive games. It’s the second-longest winning streak in the 126-year history of the franchise. They can tie the record, set in 1946, with a victory against Baltimore tomorrow. A remarkable turnaround that just keeps getting better.”

Talkin’ Baseball: “THE RED SOX HAVE THEIR LONGEST WINNING STREAK IN 80 YEARS!”

Bob Nightengale: “The hottest team on the planet? Say hello to the Boston Red Sox, who keep on winning. That’s 14 victories in a row, their longest streak since 1946 when Ted Williams was the AL MVP.”

Tyler Milliken: “This Red Sox team has gone from the most feeble group to apex predators in a flash. Never seen an identity switch this fast in my life.”

Zach Bollinger: “You can see how the Red Sox have won 13 straight. If you give them opportunities, they’re gonna take advantage of them. Give them extra outs and base runners, and you’re gonna really struggle keeping a lead.”

Jake Rill: “The Orioles‘ 7-game winning streak is over. O’s lose 6-5 to Red Sox, who win their 14th in a row. Caleb Durbin’s 8th-inning solo home run off Tyler Wells was the difference. Baltimore committed a season-high 4 errors. Orioles fall to 49-52.”

The Red Sox just won their 14th game in a row, and are MLB’s hottest team.

The Nats enter the second half with a flurry of deadline-inspired questions, and will find answers in their own performance. Plenty of All-Stars could be on the move.

Garcia Jr. would likely fit in as either a platoon or everyday second baseman in Boston. The move makes sense. Can Craig Breslow pull it off?

title: 'Keegan's got to be put on a pedestal of legends' - Anderson
description: Newcastle fans and former players are continuing to pay tribute to Kevin Keegan, who has died aged 75.  "You hear the word 'legend' banded around all the time now - but Kevin Keegan's got to be put on a pedestal of legends."  Lee Clark, who played under Keegan in the 90s said he was "very heartbroken" by the news.
fullText:
Newcastle fans and former players are continuing to pay tribute to Kevin Keegan, who has died aged 75.

BBC Radio Newcastle co-commentator and ex-player, John Anderson, arrived at Newcastle a couple of weeks before Keegan in 1982.

"It was a culture shock for me, I have to say, somebody who had won what he'd won and achieved what he had achieved in the game," Anderson said.

"But he made everyone feel so comfortable, he was such a humble guy and it didn't matter who you were, whether you were a team-mate, whether you were someone in the street, he spoke to everybody exactly the same.

"You hear the word 'legend' banded around all the time now - but Kevin Keegan's got to be put on a pedestal of legends."

Lee Clark, who played under Keegan in the 90s said he was "very heartbroken" by the news.

"He wasn't just a manager to me, he was a hero at first and then he became my manager and my boss and friend, but then he became like a father figure," Clark said.

The now-Hartlepool manager said when he first passed his driving test, Keegan saw him behind the wheel of a sports car and made him take it back to the garage because he did not like the way he was driving it.

He did the same again with Clark's next car.

"He was like the Pied Piper to the Geordie public," Clark said. "They adore him.

"I was lucky enough to witness him revitalise not just Newcastle United Football Club but also the city on two separate occasions, once as a player in the early 80s when I was just a youngster standing on the terraces and then through to having him as my boss in the early 90s. He did it again as a manager - it was just unbelievable.

"This city will be in mourning for a long time, they've lost a special person.

"They always say no one person is bigger than a football club but if there was ever one person who was close to that with Newcastle United it was Kevin Keegan."

Listen to the special episode of the Football Daily podcast that remembers Keegan

Riders such as Isaac Del Toro and Juan Ayuso will also be looking to gain valuable time before the Tour heads back into the high mountains for the decisive Alpine stages.

title: Supporters to vote on new club crest - what do you think?
description: Newcastle say eligible supporters will be able to vote on new club crest options in August as work continues to update the existing badge.  Eligible supporters will consist of men's and women's season ticket holders, Mags and Mags+ members as of 18 July 2026 and anyone who purchased tickets for two or more women's matches during the 25-26 season at Gateshead International Stadium.
fullText:
Newcastle say eligible supporters will be able to vote on new club crest options in August as work continues to update the existing badge.

The club announced they were undergoing a "thoughtful evolution" process to update their crest, moving away from the design they have used since 1988, in May 2025.

Following meetings with a variety of audiences at supporter events and focus groups, the club said there was a clear message to "simplify and protect what already exists".

"Do not reinvent and do not start again," the club added. "Craft an elegant, flexible, modern visual identity, but only if the club's core heritage is protected."

Eligible supporters will consist of men's and women's season ticket holders, Mags and Mags+ members as of 18 July 2026 and anyone who purchased tickets for two or more women's matches during the 25-26 season at Gateshead International Stadium.

What do you make of the situation? Are you glad supporters are being allowed to have their say on the future crest, or do you still think it shouldn't even be being changed?

Get in touch with your views here

title: Joshua v Prenga - all you need to know
description: All you need to know about Anthony Joshua's return to the boxing ring against Kristian Prenga in Saudi Arabia on Saturday.
fullText:
Anthony Joshua returns to the ring against Kristian Prenga on Saturday in a warm-up bout for his planned super-fight with fellow British heavyweight Tyson Fury.

Joshua, 36, last fought in December when he beat Jake Paul with a devastating sixth-round knockout in Miami.

Albania's Prenga, 35, carries knockout power but has never established himself as a world level contender in the heavyweight division.

Will Joshua get the win required to keep alive hopes of the fight with Fury? Or can Prenga pull off a major upset?

Here is all you need to know about the bout in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia.

Radio commentary from Jeddah will begin from about 20:30 BST on BBC Radio 5 Live, BBC Sounds, and the BBC Sport website and app, on Saturday, 25 July.

Coverage will be presented from ringside by Jonathan Overend, alongside two-weight world champion Natasha Jonas and boxing expert Steve Bunce.

Live text commentary will begin at 20:00 BST on the BBC Sport website and app.

Briton Hamzah Sheeraz makes the first defence of his WBO super-middleweight title against Germany's Simon Zachenhuber in the co-main event.

Fellow Briton Josh Kelly also defends his IBF light-middleweight belt against Ireland's Caoimhin Agyarko.

We will bring you round-by-round updates for both world title fights and the headline act of Joshua v Prenga.

You can also follow the action through fight week on BBC Sport via the 5 Live Boxing podcast with daily episodes.

Joshua and Prenga are not expected to make their walk to the ring before 23:00 BST.

Anthony Joshua v Kristian Prenga - heavyweight

Hamzah Sheeraz (c) v Simon Zachenhuber - WBO super-middleweight title

Josh Kelly (c) v Caoimhin Agyarko - IBF light-middleweight world title

Reito Tsutsumi v Alvino Herrera - super-featherweight

Jacob Bank v Pawel August - super-middleweight

Joshua is just shy of three months older than Prenga, but their careers have followed very different paths.

After winning gold at the 2012 Olympics, Joshua turned professional the following year and went on to become a two-time heavyweight world champion.

Joshua won each of his first 16 fights inside the distance in a run that culminated in him beating Charles Martin to claim the IBF title.

His crowning moment came in 2017 when he stopped Wladimir Klitschko in the 11th round at Wembley to unify titles - adding the WBA belt to his collection.

In 2018, Joshua outpointed Joseph Parker - winning by unanimous decision - to claim the WBO title.

Joshua tasted defeat for the first time against American Andy Ruiz Jr in 2019 but earned revenge to reclaim the titles in a rematch.

He was beaten in successive fights by Oleksandr Usyk in 2021 and 2022, before embarking on a four-fight winning streak to earn the chance to become a three-time world champion.

However, fellow Briton Daniel Dubois, who held the IBF belt, put in a career-best performance to beat Joshua by sixth-round knockout in September 2024 at Wembley Stadium.

Joshua returned to the ring in December 2025 and stopped YouTuber-turned-boxer Paul to take his overall record to 29 wins and four defeats, with 26 of his victories coming by knockout.

Prenga made his professional debut three years after Joshua and won his first four fights by knockout.

The Albanian fell to his first and only loss in his fifth bout and steps into the ring on Saturday on a 16-fight winning streak, although he does not have any recognisable names on his record.

Prenga does carry a knockout threat after picking up all 20 of his victories in that fashion.

There are no belts on the line, but there is a big prize waiting on the other side of the contest for Joshua if he wins.

After a decade-long saga, Joshua and British rival Fury have signed a deal to finally get in the ring.

Fury came out of retirement to beat Arslanbek Makhmudov in April and Joshua was expected to be his next opponent.

But Joshua has not fought since he was a passenger in a fatal car crash in late December and wanted to take a warm-up fight with Prenga first.

Fury has also agreed to a warm-up bout and fights 46-year-old Mariusz Wach in Thailand on Friday.

If both Britons come through victorious then they are expected to share a ring later this year.

A date or venue has not been announced, though Matchroom promoter Eddie Hearn has previously suggested it could land in November on streaming giant Netflix.

Saudi powerbroker Turki Alalshikh, who is co-ordinating the all-British bout, said it could take place at Wembley Stadium if local authorities are happy to stage the event in the early hours of the morning to accommodate a global audience.

Sources close to London mayor Sir Sadiq Khan have told BBC Sport such a proposal is "doable" while bosses of Cardiff's Principality Stadium also said they would be ready to host.

Wednesday - grand arrivals from 17:00

Thursday – news conference from 18:00

Saturday - fight expected at 23:00

(all times are approximate and in BST)
