title: Could Infantino really push through a 64-team World Cup?
description: If we have learned one thing from Gianni Infantino's Fifa tenure, it is that more is better. That could mean 64 teams at the World Cup.
fullText:
If we have learned one thing from 10 years of Gianni Infantino as Fifa president, it is that more is better.

More tournaments, more games, more money.

The new-look Club World Cup, the expanded World Cup, the inflated ticket prices.

And, of course, the Super Bowl-style show that meant the half-time break at the World Cup final lasted 27 minutes and 22 seconds.

Infantino appears to have thrown the door wide open to a 64-team World Cup.

"If you don't give smaller countries a chance to participate, they'll lack the incentive to keep improving," he said.

Except, of course, smaller countries are not denied the chance. If they are good enough, they qualify. See Cape Verde, Curacao, Jordan and Uzbekistan.

Purely in terms of the competition format, it does make sense.

The top two teams in the 16 groups would go through to the knockout stage. No longer would there be a need to send a few third-placed teams through.

But is there really any other logical reason to expand to 64 teams?

Let's start with how we got here.

The 2030 World Cup will mark 100 years since the first World Cup, which was held in Uruguay.

The tournament will primarily be hosted by Morocco, Portugal and Spain - but the opening three matches will be played in Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay as centenary hosts.

Conmebol - the South American confederation - wants more games.

In April 2025, it requested a one-off tournament of 64 teams so it could take on the full programme of the additional four groups.

Its president, Alejandro Dominguez, said it would ensure "nobody on the planet is left out of the party".

Within hours of the 2026 tournament ending, Dominguez was again reiterating his desire for an expanded World Cup. In a series of posts on social media he said Conmebol was "working to ensure that the 2030 World Cup features 64 teams".

"International football grows when it opens up to the world," he added.

There has been little support from elsewhere.

Uefa president Aleksander Ceferin dismissed it as a "bad idea" for both the tournament and qualifying process.

Victor Montagliani - president of the governing body of North and Central America and the Caribbean (Concacaf) - said last year it would damage "the broader football ecosystem".

Asian Football Confederation (AFC) president Sheikh Salman bin Ibrahim Al Khalifa agreed, saying further expansion could bring "chaos".

None of which means it will not happen, with the decision taken by the 37 members of the Fifa Council.

A 64-team tournament has two main plus points: a more natural format, and more opportunities for emerging nations.

It is the latter which Infantino is championing, especially after the heroics of Cape Verde.

Fifa faced criticism before the tournament, with suggestions the additional teams would not be competitive.

Then Cape Verde drew with Spain and Uruguay to finish second in their group. And the Blue Sharks gave Argentina an almighty scare by taking them to extra time in the last 16, before losing 3-2.

But the fairytale stories must be balanced with the competitive edge of the tournament.

Haiti, Iraq, Jordan, Panama, Tunisia and Uzbekistan failed to pick up a point - and this summer's group stage had minimal jeopardy for the top nations.

Add more teams and it would be difficult to see a repeat of top-four seeds like Belgium (2022), Germany (2018), Spain (2014), Italy (2010) and France (2002) failing to make to knockout stage.

Then there is the question of where the extra places would go.

Based on this year's qualifying, these are the additional countries who would have made it into a 64-team World Cup 2026 (with their Fifa ranking at the start of the tournament in brackets):

AFC: Indonesia (118), Oman (79), United Arab Emirates (68).

Uefa: Denmark (21), Italy (12), Poland (36).

Concacaf: Honduras (65), Jamaica (71), Suriname (125).

Conmebol: Bolivia (77), Venezuela (49).

Africa: Burkina Faso (62), Cameroon (44), Gabon (86), Nigeria (26).

Six of those teams are from the top 50 of Fifa's world rankings - three from Europe, two from Africa and one from South America.

And therein lies the problem. Asia and Concacaf are weaker confederations, as was proven by performances this year.

Nine of Africa's 10 countries got out of the group stage. But only two of nine Asian countries advanced, and just three of the six Concacaf countries - the co-hosts - made it through.

New Zealand, who got one point and finished bottom of their group, are the only country from Oceania in the world's top 150.

There is an argument that if you extend again, you should give a greater weight of places to Europe just for competitive balance.

But, on the other hand, there are those who would argue that bringing more teams into the World Cup ultimately allows the game to develop further in those countries.

Infantino will sell the expansion as being good for the global game, but there have to be doubts that it is good for the competition.

Almost a third of Fifa's 211 nations would be present.

Would the group stage not simply become a glorified pan-confederation qualifying competition for what is, essentially, a knockout tournament?

Expanding the World Cup to 64 teams is really just a case of bolting on four additional four-team groups.

But more groups means more time.

This year's tournament lasted 39 days. An additional 24 matches would require 20 schedule slots. Or another five days.

But this summer's event was unique for two reasons: multiple time zones and indoor stadiums.

Fifa was able to elongate a matchday at either end, with games scheduled over a 14-hour period so it could stay true to exclusive time windows for television coverage.

That will not be so straightforward in the future, even with 48 teams.

Climate change is making playing in the daytime more challenging - see the record heatwaves in Europe in recent weeks.

Other countries are far less likely to have the covered venues of Atlanta, Dallas Houston and Vancouver to mitigate it.

The Saudi Arabia World Cup is likely to move to January 2035 but it is not clear how other countries will get around this growing problem.

Until this year, only once had there been dual hosts of the World Cup - Japan and South Korea in 2002.

It is hard to imagine any one country ever being capable of hosting again. Even Saudi Arabia.

Could we be looking at World Cups hosted by, for instance, the UK and Scandinavia? Or Eastern Europe?

How would an Asian or African country be able to fund hosting the World Cup?

Let's look a just a few issues that would need to be resolved.

This summer there were 16 host cities with stadiums having capacities ranging from 43,000 to 81,000. More would be required.

There would need to be 64 individual, elite-level training facilities for the qualifying nations.

Then there is the provision of hotels for the fans, and the infrastructure and support network to move them around.

Expanding the World Cup is not as simple as giving more opportunities to more countries.

One of Infantino's key promises when he was elected was to bring more money into the game.

Every football association now has access to $8m (£5.9m) in funding over a four-year period, with a further $1.2m (£890,000) on top for the smaller nations.

Confederations get $60m (£44.5m) to develop, promote and organise football.

It is why Infantino is likely to be re-elected unopposed next year.

This money has to come from somewhere, primarily the World Cup.

In 2017, Fifa projected a 48-team World Cup would bring in $6.5bn (£4.8bn).

In 2022, it revised the format again and added 24 games. Now, it expects to rake in $9bn (£6.7bn) this year.

A 64-team event is likely to raise more from television, sponsorship and ticket sales.

Fifa says it has a responsibility to grow the game, and its tool for this appears to be more and more football.

But critics would argue there has to be a point where expansion is no longer good for the game.

Why European backlash over Trump intervention won't worry Infantino

When the World Cup finishes, the football continues on BBC Sport

title: Ferran Torres seemingly mocks Donald Trump with ‘Make Spain Great Again’ at FIFA World Cup parade
description: Ferran Torres bagged the winner for Spain in the FIFA World Cup final, and used the parade to make a statement against Donald Trump. Spain were presented the World Cup trophy by Trump, who was then at the centre of an awkward moment as he refused to leave the Spanish National Team’s trophy-raising moment.
fullText:
Ferran Torres bagged the winner for Spain in the FIFA World Cup final, and used the parade to make a statement against Donald Trump.

Spain were presented the World Cup trophy by Trump, who was then at the centre of an awkward moment as he refused to leave the Spanish National Team’s trophy-raising moment.

It appears this, along with some other moments of controversy throughout the tournament, has not sat well with Ferran Torres, as the Barcelona star took his chance at the parade to mock the current US President.

After the FIFA World Cup concluded, the Spanish National Team were given the customary heroes’ welcome when they returned to Madrid.

As is tradition, the Spanish National Team held a parade to celebrate their monumental victory. During the parade, Torres could be seen sporting a red baseball cap that read ‘Make Spain Great Again.’

The cap was an obvious parody of the red ‘Make America Great Again’ caps that are associated with Donald Trump, who coined the slogan as part of both his 2016 and 2024 Presidential campaigns.

Given the highly scrutinised nature of Trump’s involvement in the FIFA World Cup, including the trophy ceremony, it is very likely that Torres is taking a dig at the 45th and 47th President of the United States.

Donald Trump has been visibly involved with the FIFA World Cup throughout the tournament, but his involvement goes way back.

Trump spearheaded the United States’ bid for the tournament back in 2017 and was instrumental in making sure that the bid was successful.

Since then, FIFA has presented him with the inaugural FIFA Peace Prize, a move that garnered a lot of criticism from fans and officials around the world.

Of course, the most notable incident came earlier this summer, when Trump reportedly influenced FIFA to overturn Folarin Balogun’s ban in time for their Round of 16 tie against Belgium.

Many saw it as Trump leveraging his political and financial power over FIFA president Gianni Infantino to give the USMNT an edge.

While the USMNT didn’t win with Balogun on the field, many felt that Trump and the USMNT had violated the spirit and ethical code of the game and the tournament.

Why UEFA president Aleksander Ceferin boycotted the FIFA World Cup final

Three new US cities that should host the 2038 World Cup as Donald Trump pushes FIFA for USA return

British media mock Donald Trump after what he did at FIFA World Cup final

title: ‘Words the Khoslas need to hear’ – Seattle Seahawks issue clear demand to new owners
description: The Seattle Seahawks are in the process of an ownership change, which will see Vinod Khosla and family take over in the Pacific Northwest. It is still early days in the process, with the Khosla family winning the bidding at an NFL record $9.
fullText:
The Seattle Seahawks are in the process of an ownership change, which will see Vinod Khosla and family take over in the Pacific Northwest.

It is still early days in the process, with the Khosla family winning the bidding at an NFL record $9.61 billion.

There is undoubtedly some big shoes to fill for the Khoslas following an incredibly successful tenure from the Allen family, which provided two Super Bowl trophies.

As the ownership change continues to progress, supporters in Seattle are gearing up to defend their Super Bowl crown in the 2026 season.

However, those supporters have already made something crystal clear to the Khoslas ahead of the week one opener in September against the New England Patriots.

It is proving to be one of the hot topics of the NFL following the recent World Cup games being hosted in the United States.

The Dallas Cowboys have already ripped up their grass field at AT&T Stadium on the back of their final World Cup game being held in Arlington.

However, players across the league are protesting online to see their respective owners keep the real surfaces ahead of the new season getting underway.

There is a belief that playing on grass will reduce the number of serious injuries that occur in the league, with Seahawks fans sending the same message to their new owners.

Having already seen Seahawks safety Julian Love protest online, the fans have now sent their thoughts to the new owners.

“Words the Khoslas need to hear,” replied one fan to Love’s post on X.

“Bet it would extend careers by at least 2 years average,” added another.

The Khoslas have broken the NFL record for a franchise sale by purchasing the Seahawks, which shows the level of investment behind them.

“Let the new owners know!!” said one passionate Seahawks fan.

“World champs deserve the best,” said another.

One of the issues regarding the installation of grass turf in the NFL is the cost to maintain it at a high level.

Another fan even directed their thoughts towards the NFL, they said: “@NFL get your players some real grass, don’t just cater to other sports and countries.”

“Are the about-to-be-new #Seahawks owners hearing this?” said another.

How well the grass turf could be maintained in the rainy Pacific Northwest is a big question that may well see the artificial surface return to Lumen Field for the time being.

How Ryan Clark’s awkward interaction on ESPN TV led to him being fired during the show

NFL star George Kittle brings viral ‘Tarps Off’ baseball fan craze to the World Cup final

Cam Skattebo addresses viral backflip video and makes promise to New York Giants fans

title: 'Kick Argentina out of the World Cup' petition nearly breaks Guinness World Record
description: FIFA has confirmed that Argentina will face an investigation into the “disgusting” scenes at the end of the World Cup final. The team has gone viral for all the wrong reasons at this year’s tournament; and now, a petition to kick them out of the World Cup has amassed more than 23 million signatures.View on euronews
fullText:
What a fun World Cup this has been... If only FIFA and Argentina hadn't ruined the whole thing.

It's a sentiment shared by many online, who believe that this year's tournament has brought the culture of football further into disrepute - whether it's the exorbitant ticket prices, the controversial "hydration breaks", the Trump-Infantino relationship leading to the infamous red card scandal, some dubious refereeing and allegations of favouritism... The list goes on, and went on up until the final, in which Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 in extra time.

Indeed, FIFA has confirmed that Argentina will face an investigation regarding altercations with Spain players, with players like Nahuel Molina, Leandro Paredes and Thiago Almada appearing to be involved, as well as assistant coach Roberto Ayala.

Molina appeared to throw a punch at Spain’s captain Rodri as he ran past him, while Paredes clashed with Spain’s Eric Garcia.

Faced with these pathetic scenes, FIFA's disciplinary committee has appointed an ethics prosecutor to assess what happened.

The actions of the Argentinian team could be judged as "violating the basic rules of decent conduct" and "behaving in a way that brings the sport of football and/or Fifa into disrepute".

Vikings, Trump cards and baby fans: The 10 biggest viral moments of the World Cup 2026

Donald Trump photobombing La Roja goes viral following Spain's World Cup victory

This has been a long time coming, as supporting Argentina during the World Cup became increasingly problematic as the competition progressed. The team and their fans went viral for all the wrong reasons – be it racist chants, some questionable calls during the quarter-final game against Egypt which seemed to suggest favouritism, a banner that read "Las Malvinas son Argentinas" ("The Falklands are Argentine") during their post-semifinal celebration against England - to name but a few.

The conspiracy theory that the World Cup was rigged in Argentina's favour gained much traction online, with many expressing their "disgust" and anger through AI-generated videos and photos.

Take a look at some examples below:

The aforementioned disciplinary and ethics prosecutor has the power to enforce a ban globally, and a petition titled "Argentina Out" has called for the country to be kicked out of the tournament permanently.

The petition description accuses FIFA and the referees of being "biased toward Lionel Messi and Argentina", adding: "Why should the rest of the world compete when the winner has already been decided? Kick Argentina out of the World Cup and give everyone else a fair chance."

Now closed, the appeal has more than 23 million signatures - 23,316,108 to be precise.

Most impressive of all is how this has nearly broken a world record, as the Guinness World Record for the most signatories stands at 24,319,181.

That record still belongs to the 1997 Jubilee 2000 movement, which called for the cancellation of unpayable debts owed by the world's poorest countries by the end of the year 2000.

"Over 23 million people from 170 countries signed this petition in under a week," states the viral Argentina Out petition. "FIFA ignored us but Spain delivered. Thank you Spain."

title: 'Football has lost one of its true greats' - Dalglish pays tribute to Keegan
description: Sir Kenny Dalglish says he is "very saddened" by the passing of former Liverpool forward Kevin Keegan in a personal tribute on Instagram.  "Kevin was one of the very best players of his generation and wore Liverpool's famous number seven shirt with great distinction," Dalglish wrote.
fullText:
Sir Kenny Dalglish says he is "very saddened" by the passing of former Liverpool forward Kevin Keegan in a personal tribute on Instagram.

"Kevin was one of the very best players of his generation and wore Liverpool's famous number seven shirt with great distinction," Dalglish wrote. "His achievements on the pitch were exceptional, but he was also a wonderful person and genuine gentleman.

"Kevin's qualities didn't stop when he finished playing. He went on to become an outstanding manager, and what he built at Newcastle United brought belief, excitement and pride back to the football club.

"He gave so many people wonderful memories and his teams played the game in a way that supporters loved to watch.

"Football has lost one of its true greats."

Keegan's former Liverpool and England team-mate Phil Thompson echoed Dalglish's sentiment and said "it's a difficult time for everybody".

"When I first heard the news, I was absolutely devastated," Thompson said on BBC Radio 5 Live's Football Daily podcast. "But I've been listening to all the tributes and love for Kevin, and about all the love that he gave to everybody else.

"He was a lovely man and that is above all his footballing exploits.

"Humility poured out of Kevin's veins. He was just such a nice guy.

"He never turned down an autograph or photograph. He was the best of all the people I have known in football and in sport.

"There was an honesty in everything about him. He didn't have the greatest ability in the world, but he made the best of what he had which drove him forward. He knew how good he was.

"Even as England manager, he knew his limitations and that is Kevin in a nutshell.

"When he came to Liverpool, he was a star waiting to happen. We got a superhero in 1971 and he was a revelation. He was inspirational.

"He hit it off with Bill Shankly and the two of them were inspired and it took us to new levels. He was the spark the football club needed at the time."

Listen to a special Football Daily podcast which reflects on the life and legacy of Kevin Keegan on BBC Sounds

title: Chelsea unveil 2026-27 away kit by Nike
description: Black and yellow, black and yellow!
fullText:
Chelsea’s away shirt for the upcoming season will be a fairly snazzy-looking black number, with just enough design features and subtle patterning to make it interesting — unlike, say, last year’s third shirt, which was just a plain black tee at the end of the day.

While the classic color for a Chelsea away shirt, I’d argue, is yellow, both black and white have become popular design choices in more modern times, so this one fits the trend. (The rumored third kit for this season is white.)

The obvious standout feature of the shirt is the bold yellow pattern on the collar in sleeve trim. Sorry, I should say bold “Midwest Gold lustre” pattern on the collar and sleeves. It’s the same accent color as on the home shirt, and it’s a quality hue.

Also like the home shirt, we get just the rampant lion instead of the complete badge, which I appreciate from a fashion perspective (and it clashes less with the Club World Champions badge) but don’t really appreciate from a football club perspective. Would be nice if at least one of the shirts carried the full badge still. Please don’t mess with this; we get enough CFC LDN shoved in our faces elsewhere.

Whatever our away form’s going to be this season, at least we’re going to look very good.

You can spend far, far, far too much money on this shirt at the Megastore.

Unleash The Lion.

The Chelsea Away Kit 26/27 is here. Created with @NikeFootball.

The lion on our chest. Identity. A unity you can feel. A pride that refuses to stay quiet. That stands as one, wherever we go.

Away Kit 26/27. Available now in-store and online.#UnleashTheLion pic.twitter.com/wTJFFP0kg8

— Chelsea FC (@ChelseaFC) July 21, 2026

title: Picture: Barcelona unveil special edition shirt to celebrate historic European milestone
description: FC Barcelona have unveiled a special commemorative shirt to celebrate one of the greatest milestones in the club’s history. After reaching 50 European Cup titles across their various sporting teams...
fullText:
FC Barcelona have unveiled a special commemorative shirt to celebrate one of the greatest milestones in the club’s history.

After reaching 50 European Cup titles across their various sporting teams, the Blaugrana have released an exclusive jersey that pays tribute to the success that has made the Catalan giants one of the most decorated multisport institutions in world sport.

The latest Barcelona news sees the club mark the historic achievement with a collector’s item that is now available for fans worldwide.

The commemorative shirt celebrates every European Cup won by Barcelona’s professional teams, underlining the club’s rich sporting tradition beyond football.

Available through the club’s official online store and the Botiga del Spotify Camp Nou, the launch allows fans to own a unique piece of Barça history.

The new Barcelona shirt has been created to honour all 50 European Cup triumphs achieved by the club across six different sports.

Barcelona’s men’s football team has contributed five European Cup titles, while Barça Femeni has added four.

The basketball team has lifted the trophy twice, the handball section has collected 13 European crowns, the futsal team has won four, and the roller hockey side leads the way with an incredible 22 European Cup triumphs.

Those achievements have been brought together in a single design that reflects the club’s identity as a true multisport powerhouse.

Explaining the launch, Barcelona said,

“It’s a great gift to wear this summer with the round number of 50 to show everyone that Barça is the best multisports club in the world.

Barcelona have released a special edition shirt for the fans. (Photo by Maja Hitij/Getty Images)

“A garment that stands for excellence, ambition and a multisport spirit that makes Barça ‘More than a Club.’”

The Barcelona commemorative shirt has been produced using premium-quality cotton and includes several design elements linked to the club’s European success.

On the front, supporters will find the iconic FC Barcelona crest alongside the Nike logo, accompanied by symbols representing each of the sporting sections responsible for the 50 European Cup victories.

The standout feature appears on the back of the shirt, where a large number “50” dominates the design.

Alongside it is a complete list of every European Cup won by Barcelona in men’s football, women’s football, basketball, handball, roller hockey and futsal.

title: Report: Clubs queuing up to sign two Liverpool defenders who’ve been included on US tour
description: Two Liverpool defenders who’ve been included in the squad for the pre-season tour to the United States are reportedly attracting interest from several clubs across the UK.The Reds have made the tran...
fullText:
Two Liverpool defenders who’ve been included in the squad for the pre-season tour to the United States are reportedly attracting interest from several clubs across the UK.

The Reds have made the transatlantic journey without a few of their senior defenders to call upon, with Virgil van Dijk still on holiday after playing at the World Cup. Conor Bradley and Giovanni Leoni have made the trip but will continue their rehabilitation from long-term injuries rather than being involved on the pitch.

That could afford opportunities to two youngsters who’ve had scant senior game-time at LFC and may use the upcoming friendlies to either stake their claim to Andoni Iraola or put themselves in the shop window for a move elsewhere.

In the latest edition of The Athletic‘s Transfer DealSheet, Gregg Evans touched upon the futures of some young players in the Liverpool squad who could potentially depart in the coming weeks.

Calvin Ramsay and Luke Chambers have travelled to the USA, although the club could ‘listen to loan offers’ for them later in the transfer window.

The Scottish right-back is attracting interest from multiple clubs in his homeland, while the Preston-born left-back is reportedly liked by Queens Park Rangers.

Want more Empire of the Kop coverage? Add us as a preferred source on Google to your favourites list for news you can trust

Ramsay – who turns 23 at the end of this month – was strangely overlooked by Arne Slot last season despite the injury woes to Bradley and Jeremie Frimpong, and the then-Liverpool head coach even took to undermining the Scot in public.

Now that Iraola is in charge and the Northern Ireland captain isn’t yet fit to play, the Aberdeen native may be confident of playing some part in the pre-season fixtures and potentially being kept at Anfield if the two men ahead of him in the pecking order continue to be plagued by injury.

For Chambers, though, his prospects of playing on tour aren’t helped by the inclusion of Milos Kerkez and Kostas Tsimikas, and you’ve to go back to December 2023 for the most recent of his four senior appearances for the Reds.

Having had a spell on loan at Charlton last season, the 22-year-old may well end up in London once more if QPR follow through on their reported interest in him.

The door should never be closed to any player at Liverpool, especially in defence where we’ve had so many injury problems over the past year, but realistically it seems more likely than unlikely that the full-back duo will be playing their football elsewhere in 2026/27 – unless they dazzle Iraola in pre-season to the extent that he refuses to countenace them leaving.

title: South American football boss calls for 64 teams in 2030
description: South American football president Alejandro Domínguez says it would be a great opportunity to mark the World Cup’s 100th anniversary in 2030 with 64 teams. The next men's tournament will held in Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay.
fullText:
South American football president Alejandro Domínguez says it would be a great opportunity to mark the World Cup’s 100th anniversary in 2030 with 64 teams.

The next men's tournament will held in Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Argentina, Uruguay and Paraguay. The latter three will have opening games to commemorate the first ever tournament in Uruguay in 1930, before action switches over the Atlantic.

But the number of teams has yet to be confirmed and FIFA president Gianni Infantino hinted during the recent 2026 edition, held with a record 48 teams, that he could envisage 64 in four years' time.

Domínguez is adamant it can work.

"The next match will be played at home!", the head of the South American continental confederation CONMEBOL wrote on X late on Monday.

"In 2030, the World Cup will be held in Uruguay, Argentina and Paraguay – a great opportunity for football to celebrate the World Cup’s 100th anniversary with a tournament featuring 64 teams."

The 2030 event is currently planned for June 8 to July 21 and would be the longest World Cup in history at 44 days.

The 48-team tournament meant an extra round of 32 and had the eight best third-placed nations from the groups going through. A 64-team event would make more sense mathematically with only group winners and second-placed teams going through to the knock-outs and no extra games per team compared to this year.

title: Bristol City sign striker Tolaj from Plymouth
description: Bristol City sign Swiss striker Lorent Tolaj from Plymouth Argyle on a four-year deal for an undisclosed fee.
fullText:
Bristol City have signed Swiss striker Lorent Tolaj from Plymouth Argyle on a four-year deal for an undisclosed fee.

The 24-year-old scored 38 goals in all competitions across the past two seasons.

Tolaj quickly found form at Port Vale after arriving from non-league Aldershot Town in 2024, netting 14 goals in 37 league appearances for the Valiants as they won promotion to League One before moving to Home Park last August.

At Argyle, the centre forward formed a formidable strike partnership with Bim Pepple, scoring 17 league goals last term.

Despite suffering a muscle tear in his leg in late February, Tolaj returned to full fitness by Easter, and scored four goals and provided four assists in Argyle's final six matches of the season as they missed out on the play-off places by two points.

He becomes Bristol City's seventh signing of the summer following the acquisitions of players including Sam Tickle, Jed Wallace, Sam Greenwood and Lisav Eissat.

It is understood that Vale will receive a portion of the transfer fee from Argyle due to a sell-on clause included in Tolaj's contract last summer.

Tolaj told the club website: "It is a great club with a great fanbase and I spoke with the manager about the plan. I am ambitious and this club is ambitious, so that's why I wanted to come here.

"To make the step up is important for me and I will give everything for this football club."

Robins boss Michael Skubala added: "We are really pleased to add Lorent's physicality and intensity to our front line.

"His progression over the past few seasons is testament to both his talent and work ethic. He will add a different dimension to our squad and we believe he will thrive working with the group."

Brent Pilnick, BBC Sport, Plymouth.

While not unexpected, Lorent Tolaj's departure will still be a blow to Plymouth Argyle.

The Swiss striker proved to be a revelation in less than one season at Home Park (he played four times for Port Vale before his move in late August).

He proved himself to arguably be League One's top striker last season - had he not missed the best part of two-and-half months with injury from late January he would have probably topped the goalscoring charts and earned a place in the League One Team of the Season.

It also represents good business for Argyle - the fee thought to be in excess of £4m quadruples the £1.1m they paid less than a year ago.

Two questions now persist - can Argyle keep Tolaj's highly-rated strike partner Bim Pepple and can they find a replacement as good?

Argyle waited until late in the window to get their man last summer, they will hope they can bring in someone a lot quicker this time around.

Latest Bristol City news, analysis and fan views

title: Thad Jean predicts Shamil Musaev will get ‘greedy’ before PFL title fight
description: Thad Jean is confident that he can land a fight-ending blow in his upcoming bout against Shamil Musaev on July 25.
fullText:
Thad Jean is confident that he can land a fight-ending blow in his upcoming bout against Shamil Musaev on July 25.

 The undefeated 2025 Professional Fighters League welterweight tournament champion will challenge former tourney winner Musaev for the vacant PFL welterweight championship in the main event of PFL Washington D.C. at CareFirst Arena.

Jean enters the title fight with an unblemished 11-0 record, while Musaev has an 18-1-1 slate and captured the 2024 PFL welterweight tournament before falling short in his bid for the inaugural divisional crown earlier this year. Ahead of their championship bout, Jean explained how he plans to make a statement against Musaev.

 When asked if he has a prediction for the bout, Jean claimed he’ll take advantage of the fact that Musaev is coming off his first pro defeat.

 “I think after his last loss, he will get greedy and I will catch him,” he said. “He is gonna get greedy, and I am going to catch him, or he’s gonna get frustrated, and I am going to catch him. I mean, if you watch him back, you’ll see what he does before he starts. He gets frustrated, then he goes.”

 A welterweight title fight headlines PFL Washington DC: Tune on Saturday, July 25 at 10 p.m. ET on ESPN.

title: What is the latest on Rashford's United future?
description: Marcus Rashford is contracted to Manchester United until 2028.  Barcelona had an option, in the loan deal they agreed for Rashford last season, to turn it into a permanent transfer for £26m.  There was another clause in Rashford's contract that allowed other clubs - Liverpool and Manchester City excepted - to buy him for £40m, which expired earlier in July.
fullText:
Marcus Rashford is contracted to Manchester United until 2028. His salary of £325,000 a week makes him the club's top earner at a time when minority owner Sir Jim Ratcliffe is trying to stay on top of salary costs, which are, by some distance, the club's highest expenditure.

Barcelona had an option, in the loan deal they agreed for Rashford last season, to turn it into a permanent transfer for £26m.

As expected, that deadline passed in the middle of June without it being activated.

There was another clause in Rashford's contract that allowed other clubs - Liverpool and Manchester City excepted - to buy him for £40m, which expired earlier in July. As expected, no-one triggered that either.

Sources close to the player felt it was illogical to think a club would come in and offer more than Barcelona would have had to pay.

With England's World Cup campaign at an end, Rashford is now entitled to a three-week break.

Unless the timings are altered, or his situation changes, he will return to Manchester United just as they begin a six-day training camp in the Republic of Ireland, scheduled around an historic meeting with old rivals Leeds at Croke Park on 12 August.

Suggestions around Rashford have been that he is relaxed about the prospect of returning to training at United.

For a start, there is not the same confrontational relationship between the player and his coach.

Ruben Amorim fell out with the forward within weeks of his arrival from Sporting Lisbon. Amorim eventually exiled him, which triggered a loan move to Aston Villa.

Amorim's replacement, Michael Carrick, is a former team-mate of Rashford's. He has also been his coach and was, briefly, his manager, following Ole Gunnar Solskjaer's dismissal in 2021. The pair are said to get on well.

Carrick's only public statement around Rashford, towards the end of last season, was conciliatory in nature, which is in keeping with his general personality.

For their part, United are sticking to their refusal to renegotiate a deal with Barcelona on reduced terms or consider a second loan to the Catalan giants.

The consistency of United's messaging is being articulated in a summer where they are keen to prove they will not be bullied into overpaying to get deals over the line.

title: 'Tielemans needs a more defensive-minded partner'
description: BBC Radio Manchester's Gaz Drinkwater and Joe McGrath discuss transfers in the latest episode of The Devils' Advocate.  Gaz believes United manager, Michael Carrick, might have some hard decisions to make when it comes to midfield this season.  "I've got a lot of thoughts on our centre-midfield and some concerns," he said on the BBC Sounds podcast.
fullText:
BBC Radio Manchester's Gaz Drinkwater and Joe McGrath discuss transfers in the latest episode of The Devils' Advocate.

Gaz believes United manager, Michael Carrick, might have some hard decisions to make when it comes to midfield this season.

"I've got a lot of thoughts on our centre-midfield and some concerns," he said on the BBC Sounds podcast.

"In an ideal world, Youri Tielemans needs a more defensive-minded partner, which Andrey Santos is. As a centre-midfield pairing, it works quite well.

"But there's just no way United don't play Kobbie Mainoo. Michael Carrick loves him. If Mainoo plays he needs Santos, so a Tielemans and Mainoo partnership doesn't quite fit.

"So it makes me think we should go for another centre-midfielder who would be our marquee signing. Someone like Eduardo Camavinga. But I think that's it."

Listen to the podcast below or on BBC Sounds

title: Report: Aston Villa considering move to sign World Cup forward
description: Aston Villa Eye Crysencio Summerville as Morgan Rogers Exit NearsAston Villa are weighing up a move for Crysencio Summerville, with the West Ham winger emerging as a serious option if Morgan Rogers co...
fullText:
Aston Villa are weighing up a move for Crysencio Summerville, with the West Ham winger emerging as a serious option if Morgan Rogers completes his blockbuster switch to Chelsea. According to talkSPORT, Villa are now considering whether to enter a growing race for the £40m-rated Netherlands international.

The timing is significant. Rogers is reportedly closing in on a £117m transfer to Stamford Bridge, a deal set to become a record fee for a British player. With that expected departure likely to alter Villa’s attacking structure, attention has quickly turned towards possible replacements and Summerville appears firmly in the frame.

There is already established competition. Roma have tabled an offer worth around £40m for the 24-year-old, while Manchester United and Tottenham have also been linked. Villa’s interest, therefore, places them in a crowded market for a player whose situation has changed sharply following West Ham’s relegation to the Championship.

Summerville is set to leave the London Stadium after the Hammers dropped into England’s second tier, and that has opened the door to a transfer through a release clause in his contract. For clubs looking for a dynamic winger entering his peak years, that naturally makes him an attractive proposition.

The former Leeds forward managed seven goals in 34 appearances in all competitions during a difficult campaign for West Ham. Even so, he was described as “a bright spark in an otherwise disappointing season”, and there is a feeling across the market that his best football remains ahead of him.

His profile has only strengthened after the World Cup in North America. Summerville contributed two goals and two assists as Holland reached the round of 32 before losing to Morocco on penalties. Those displays appear to have reinforced the belief among interested clubs that he can operate at a higher level and within a more stable attacking unit.

From Villa’s perspective, the appeal is obvious. Summerville offers directness, carries genuine one-v-one threat and can stretch opponents from wide areas. If Rogers leaves, replacing output is important, but so is replacing unpredictability, and Summerville brings exactly that.

This will not be straightforward. Manchester United are understood to have opened talks earlier this month, although any move is said to depend on the future of Marcus Rashford. Tottenham also hold a long-standing interest and had already made an enquiry in January.

Roma, meanwhile, are pushing hard. The Italian club have already shown a willingness to do business around Villa, having taken Donyell Malen on an initial loan in January before completing a permanent £21.3m deal in May. Malen’s 15 goals in 20 games in all competitions only strengthens Roma’s confidence in targeting Premier League-based talent.

That leaves Villa needing to decide whether to move quickly or risk missing out. If Rogers does depart for the reported £117m, there would be money available, though spending power must still be viewed through the lens of squad planning and profit and sustainability rules.

That point was raised directly when the potential fit was discussed earlier this month. Speaking to talkSPORT, Andy Brassell said: “You just wonder if the right players are around him, the right stage on which to compete, he could really get there.” He added: “I wonder if he could be a potential Aston Villa player going into next season.”

Those comments go to the heart of this story. Villa are not simply searching for a like-for-like replacement, they are looking for someone who can be elevated by the environment around him. Brassell also said: “Now of course we know that their hands are tied a little bit with PSR and they can’t go out splashing out infinite amounts to improve their squad.”

That feels especially relevant. Even if the Rogers deal goes through, Villa will want to be precise rather than extravagant. Summerville may sit in the right bracket, both financially and tactically. As Brassell put it: “I tend to see him as more of an inside player than an outside player, which Summerville is even though he can come into those central areas as well. So I wonder if that could be a good get for them.”

There is also a wider endorsement of the player’s trajectory. Brassell concluded: “But there are a number of teams in the Premier League who would really benefit from Summerville, and I think with the right situation around him he’s ready to go and become the player that a lot of people believed he could be when he came into the Premier League with Leeds in first place.”

From an Aston Villa supporter’s perspective, this is the sort of link that immediately grabs the attention. If Morgan Rogers is genuinely on his way for £117m, replacing that quality and energy is no small task, but Summerville feels like a clever name to consider. He is proven in English football, still only 24, and there is a sense that his ceiling has not yet been reached.

The most encouraging part of the report is the fit. Villa need wide players who can attack space, commit defenders and give the side more thrust in transition. Summerville does all of that. He has had to perform in difficult circumstances before, and that can harden a player. Put him into a stronger side with better structure and more support, and there is every chance his numbers rise.

There will be understandable concerns about the £40m valuation, especially with PSR in the background, but if Rogers goes for the kind of fee being discussed, Villa have to be ambitious. This is not about replacing one player pound for pound, it is about ensuring the squad remains dangerous and progressive.

Supporters would probably accept that there are bigger names on the market, but this kind of move often makes more sense. Summerville has hunger, Premier League experience and room to improve. If Villa can move decisively, this has the look of a smart post-Rogers rebuild move.

title: Hull target Tzolakis is 'good with pressure'
description: Hull City boss Sergej Jakirovic said last week the Tigers were close to completing a deal for Olympiakos goalkeeper Konstantinos Tzolakis.  In light of this, BBC Radio Humberside's James Hoggarth spoke to Greek football journalist Konstantinos Lianos to find out more about Tzolakis.  Every time a Championship club returns to the Premier League, they're automatically dubbed the favourites for relegation.
fullText:
Hull City boss Sergej Jakirovic said last week the Tigers were close to completing a deal for Olympiakos goalkeeper Konstantinos Tzolakis.

In light of this, BBC Radio Humberside's James Hoggarth spoke to Greek football journalist Konstantinos Lianos to find out more about Tzolakis.

"We are talking about a player who is very good with pressure," Lianos explained.

"That's exactly what Hull are about to deal with on their return to the Premier League. Every time a Championship club returns to the Premier League, they're automatically dubbed the favourites for relegation.

"We have historically seen defenders and goalkeepers do better in the Premier League. When it comes to Greek players it's always a safe bet. Tzolakis has played in the Champions League.

"He was the youngest ever Greek goalkeeper to compete in a Greek cup final, aged 17, 10 months and four days. He had the most clean sheets in the 2024-25 Europa League with six clean sheets in 10 games."

title: 'Virat Kohli aur Rohit Sharma ko touch nahi kar payenge': Ashwin makes bold statement
description: Ravichandran Ashwin supports Rohit Sharma and Virat Kohli's continued selection for India. He cited their extensive career achievements and significant fan support as key factors. Ashwin noted that bowlers do not receive the same level of public backing. Rohit Sharma recently silenced retirement speculation with a century against England. The captain stated that external criticism has always been part of his career.
fullText:
NEW DELHI: Former India spinner Ravichandran Ashwin has thrown his support behind Rohit Sharma and Virat Kohli, saying the two senior batters cannot simply be dropped if they still want to play for India. 

Ashwin's comments came after Rohit silenced retirement talk with a brilliant century in the third and final ODI against England at Lord's. Although India lost the match by 27 runs and the series 2-1, Rohit's 138 reminded everyone why he remains one of India's biggest match-winners.

Speaking on his YouTube channel Ash ki Baat, Ashwin said: "'Rohit Sharma aur Virat Kohli ko touch nahi kar payenge (They will not be able to touch Virat Kohli and Rohit Sharma). They cannot touch Virat and Rohit if they want to play. It is because of their credentials. Rohit has scored almost 12,000 runs; they cannot touch him."

Ashwin said there was another major reason why the two stars are difficult to leave out.

"The other thing is, they cannot be touched because they are batters. They have an army, a battalion. People come to see them. If you drop them, it will just break the roof down."

Ashwin also pointed out that bowlers do not receive the same level of public backing as star batters.

"But if Shami, Siraj or Bhuvneshwar are not picked, 10 people will question. 100 people, 1000 people, maybe 10,000 people will question. But they won't make noise. If a day comes when people would make noise for bowlers, it would be difficult to drop them as well."

Rohit's place in the ODI side had come under intense scrutiny after reports claimed that the BCCI selection committee was not considering him for the 2027 ODI World Cup.

The India captain had scores of 11 and 26 in the first two ODIs against England before producing a stunning knock in the series decider.

Chasing 388 at Lord's, Rohit smashed 138 off just 110 balls, hitting 17 fours and five sixes. He kept India's chase alive almost until the end, even though the visitors eventually fell 27 runs short.

After the match, Rohit made it clear that speculation over his future has never affected him.

In an interview with BCCI.tv, he said external criticism has always been part of his career and that his only focus is performing for India.

"Look, my job is with the bat... Represent my country... That's what I'm gonna do. The noise... Since I made my debut, was there and till the time I'm gonna stay here, it's always gonna be there. So it doesn't really matter too much. What matters is what I do on the field," Rohit said.

title: Barcelona captain set for crucial medical assessment after World Cup injury – report
description: Barcelona are closing in on a crucial decision regarding Frenkie de Jong’s injury, with the Dutch midfielder set to return to the Ciutat Esportiva Joan Gamper in the coming days for further medical ...
fullText:
Barcelona are closing in on a crucial decision regarding Frenkie de Jong’s injury, with the Dutch midfielder set to return to the Ciutat Esportiva Joan Gamper in the coming days for further medical assessment.

According to SPORT, the Barcelona midfielder will undergo another round of evaluations before the club’s medical staff and the player jointly decide on the most suitable treatment plan.

The latest Barcelona news comes after De Jong returned from the 2026 FIFA World Cup carrying a knee problem that worsened during the Netherlands’ campaign.

Although the initial outlook remains concerning, Barcelona have already received one significant piece of encouraging news as they continue to monitor one of Hansi Flick’s most important players.

Frenkie de Jong’s injury has been one of the biggest concerns for Barcelona since the midfielder returned from international duty.

De Jong played the final two matches of the Netherlands’ World Cup campaign despite experiencing considerable discomfort in his right knee.

Frenkie de Jong played the World Cup with a knee injury. (Photo by David Ramos/Getty Images)

Following the tournament, he reported to the Ciutat Esportiva on the opening day of Barcelona’s pre-season, where club doctors immediately examined the affected knee.

Medical staff initially identified an injury, but the level of swelling prevented them from completing every necessary test.

Once the inflammation reduced, further examinations confirmed that the problem was more serious than first hoped.

However, the biggest positive for Barcelona was that doctors ruled out any major damage to the knee ligaments, avoiding what could have been a far longer spell on the sidelines.

The Dutch international was subsequently advised to continue his holiday while following conservative treatment and allowing the knee to rest before returning for a final assessment later this week.

De Jong could be out for three to four months. (Photo by Molly Darlington/Getty Images)

The latest Barcelona injury news suggests De Jong is expected to miss between three and four months, meaning he is likely to sit out the opening phase of the new campaign.

Once he returns to the training ground, Barcelona’s medical department and the midfielder will agree on the next stage of his rehabilitation programme before confirming the definitive treatment strategy.

Despite losing one of the team’s most influential players, Hansi Flick is not expected to receive a replacement in the current transfer window.

The report states that Barcelona have no intention of signing another midfielder despite Frenkie de Jong’s injury, with the club continuing to trust the existing options already available in the squad.

At the same time, Barcelona’s sporting department is maintaining its plan regarding Marc Casado, with the midfielder still expected to leave the club on loan or in a permanent transfer if the right opportunity arrives.

title: Nine to axe 30 jobs at the Age and SMH due to ‘extreme’ AI disruption
description: Staff told positions will be cut via voluntary and targeted redundancies as part of...
fullText:
Staff told positions will be cut via voluntary and targeted redundancies as part of ‘evolution’

Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Australia’s biggest media company, Nine Entertainment, has blamed the “extreme state of disruption” from AI for its decision to cut another 30 newsroom jobs at the Sydney Morning Herald and the Age.

On Tuesday, the managing director of publishing, Tory Maguire, told staff the company was looking to cut “around 30” positions, made up of voluntary and targeted redundancies. Maguire said the exercise was not simply a matter of cutting costs, but was an “evolution” to adapt to structural change.

“In the last 12 months, as we have discussed many, many times at the publishing town halls, we are in an extreme state of disruption because of AI,” she said.

The company has plans to retrain and to hire people for “digital-first, reader-savvy, data-informed roles”.

Maguire said the Australian Financial Review, which won’t be affected by the restructure, is more evolved than the Age and the Sydney Morning Hereald in terms of a digital-first approach, as it has had a paywall since 2007.

The announcement came two years after Nine Entertainment cut 200 jobs, including 90 positions from the legacy mastheads and the AFR.

But Maguire said “we are in the middle” of a major disruption caused by AI.

“The internet and social media did not disrupt us as much as … AI,” Maguire said.

According to the Reuters Institute’s Digital News Report 2026, 10% of news consumers around the world use AI chatbots to get their news. For those aged under 35, it climbs to 16%.

There have also been warnings that fewer people are clicking through to news stories from search engines, because Google is giving them an AI summary.

According to research by the University of Sydney, Australian journalism was largely “invisible” in AI-generated news summaries from Microsoft Copilot, which overwhelmingly favours US or European media.

Nine, the largest locally owned media company is made up of the publishing division, Nine Television, streaming service Stan and the digital division, nine.com.au.

Nine’s digital subscription revenue was down by 6% last year. Maguire said reader revenue stalled for the first time in three years, and the division was under “enormous pressure”, partly due to the popularity of AI search results.

In 2024, job cuts sparked industrial action and resulted in dozens of senior journalists taking voluntary redundancies from Nine’s mastheads.

At the time, Nine said the cost-cutting was necessary after Meta walked away from the news media bargaining code.

Later that year the chief executive, Matt Stanton, said the company was facing $100m in cuts over the next two years.

When Nine merged with Fairfax Media in 2018 a total of 144 jobs were lost.

title: Not enough water for UK’s datacentre plans, trade body says
description:
Industry says government’s failure to address cooling demands means AI growth plans are ‘fatally flawed’
The UK will not have enough water for future datacentres, the water industry has said in stark criticism of the government’s AI growth plans.
Datacentres rely on large amounts of water to manage the heat generated by densely packed servers. Cooling towers, chillers and humidification systems make direct use of this water, with large volumes also consumed indirectly through high electrical power needs.
 Continue reading...
fullText:
Industry says government’s failure to address cooling demands means AI growth plans are ‘fatally flawed’

The UK will not have enough water for future datacentres, the water industry has said in stark criticism of the government’s AI growth plans.

Datacentres rely on large amounts of water to manage the heat generated by densely packed servers. Cooling towers, chillers and humidification systems make direct use of this water, with large volumes also consumed indirectly through high electrical power needs.

Water UK, the trade body that represents water companies in Britain and Northern Ireland, said in a publicly available written briefing to MPs that the government’s water forecasts were “fatally flawed” and “explicitly exclude” datacentres.

It wrote that the government “makes not a single mention of water” in its policy for delivering AI growth zones, its AI for science strategy or its AI opportunities action plan.

It added that the Environment Agency’s most recent national framework for water resources, published in June 2025, also failed to include an estimate of the water demands of datacentres.

“There appears to be an assumption that the country will always have enough water for its economic needs,” the briefing said. “Nothing could be further from the truth.”

In May, a House of Lords report said England faced a 5bn litre a day shortfall for public water supplies by 2055.

Water UK said datacentres were already being built in water-stressed areas, with more than three-quarters located in the south and east of England. Millions of people in these regions have been placed under a hosepipe ban by water companies after prolonged extreme heat and lack of rainfall.

The trade body said any new datacentre growth in these areas “may be impossible” without policy change because of existing pressure on the network.

“As leakage and demand targets are already very ambitious, the only solution to deliver new supply is to increase the amount of water taken from the environment – which may be harmful – or to build new supplies, and major reservoirs can take 15 years to plan and build,” Water UK wrote.

It added: “Water companies have cut leakage by around 40% since privatisation and plan to reduce it by a further 17% by 2030.”

However, Greenpeace said last week that water companies were wasting five times more water through leaky pipes than even a national hosepipe ban would save.

The UK’s highest concentration of datacentres is in Slough, which is served by Affinity Water. About 125 datacentres are proposed or under construction in Affinity’s region, much of which is subject to a hosepipe ban.

In May, the company told MPs that some of these datacentres were requesting up to 3m litres of water a day, equivalent to the peak water demand of 3,500 homes.

“The planning framework is built around household growth and per-capita consumption, and has not adapted to commercial users whose water needs can match those of a market town,” Affinity said in a written submission to the environmental audit select committee.

Because of Environment Agency permitting, datacentres are currently required to make use of drinking water only. Water UK estimated that datacentres in England use 6.6m litres of drinking water every day. With the government’s ambition to triple datacentre capacity by 2030, this could increase to 19.8m litres a day.

Jon Chappel, Water UK’s deputy director, said this was “unforgivable” and a “genuine failing of government”.

England’s current water shortfall is 220m litres more a day than was assumed in water forecasts. Water UK said this deficit was currently being met by “accepting a greater risk of environmental harm, and restrictions on water use in the event of drought”.

Similar concerns have been raised in Ireland, where hosepipe bans have ignited criticism of water companies and the government over a lack of clarity about datacentres’ water usage.

The government has designated datacentres as critical national infrastructure, on the basis that their loss or compromise could result in “major detrimental impact on the availability, integrity or delivery of essential services” and “significant impact on national security, national defence, or the functioning of the state”.

Oliver Hayes, head of big tech and campaigns at the campaign group Global Action Plan, said it was not clear why datacentres had been given this status, but that water companies may be powerless to restrict their water use as a result. This could risk datacentres being prioritised over households at times of peak water demand.

“I think it is highly likely that we’re going to end up seeing datacentres that are actually running chatbots, or enabling people to do benign and very wasteful, pointless things, protected and given favourable treatment when it comes to resources,” Hayes said.

Chappel said Water UK had asked the government to publish a drought hierarchy to set out which sectors are prioritised in times of water shortages, but that ministers had yet to do so.

“Without this there will be a trade-off across society” he said.

At the end of June, water companies, the water regulator Ofwat, the Environment Agency, the Met Office and the Welsh government-sponsored body Natural Resources Wales announced a £75m campaign to encourage people to use less water. Water use per person in England and Wales is among the highest in Europe.

The government was expected to publish a national policy statement for AI growth in February, providing an overarching strategy and defining how cumulative impacts are considered, but this has been delayed.

A government spokesperson said: “The environment and sustainable development must go hand-in-hand and we will protect our water supply while unlocking growth, including looking at alternatives or ways to minimise impact on supplies and output of wastewater from datacentres.

“We are also taking decisive measures to secure water supplies and meet higher demand, including record levels of infrastructure investment and building nine new reservoirs.”

title: CodeSizer: Why is that binary so big?
description: Why is that binary so big? Contribute to Wren6991/CodeSizer development by creating an account on GitHub.
fullText:
CodeSizer is a static code size profiling tool.

In size-focused embedded firmware development, the final binary is heavily inlined and link-time-optimised. A single function symbol may cover dozens of inlinees. CodeSizer uses objdump and addr2line to unwind the inline call stack at every instruction address, so code size can be attributed to the correct node in the inline call tree within each function.

The output is a static HTML report file, with a small amount of JavaScript for UI features like toggling expand/collapse of the tree view.

The correct toolchain must be present on your $PATH with the given prefix, or you must specify a full file path to the toolchain binaries. For example:

If you've installed the gcc-arm-none-eabi package on Ubuntu, then use --cross-prefix=arm-none-eabi-.

If you've installed a riscv-gnu-toolchain build at /opt/riscv/gcc15, then use --cross-prefix=/opt/riscv/gcc15/bin/riscv32-unknown-elf-.

This HTML report was generated by the following command:

The ELF file comes from the RP2350 A4 bootrom release.

title: Palantir in Europe: 40 documented customer relationships across 14 countries
description: A public-source review of 40 European organisations and public bodies publicly linked to Palantir. Evidence quality varies from confirmed deployments to pilots, partnerships, LOIs and infrastructure observations.
fullText:
CipherCue is a European infrastructure-observation platform. Most of what we publish is derived from our own continuous scanning. This article is different: it is a public-source review of one vendor's European footprint, assembled from contract records, parliamentary records, official announcements and independent reporting, with a small number of technical corroborations.

Palantir Technologies is a United States company founded in 2003 with early funding from the CIA's venture arm In-Q-Tel. Its principal products are Gotham (used by intelligence, defence and police agencies), Foundry (used across commercial industries and increasingly in government), Apollo (deployment and operational tooling) and AIP (Palantir's platform for integrating AI models with governed enterprise data, operational workflows, applications and automations). The article names the European organisations we could document, labels the evidence class behind each, and notes where a public decision has been taken to reduce, replace or block Palantir use.

Two limitations shape everything below. First, Palantir does not publish a customer list. Tenants sit behind private hostnames, some but not all of which live under palantirfoundry.com, and free certificate-transparency interfaces time out on that domain during enumeration. The article therefore relies mainly on desk research rather than probes, and a reader can reasonably describe that as a lit review with a small technical corroboration layer. Second, aggregator sites (bloomberry.com, appsruntheworld.com) and Palantir's own Impact case studies list further European entities not documented here; someone including those sources uncritically would produce a larger list. We chose the narrower cut because each entry below has a clickable source and a labelled evidence class.

Public-sector entries below span twelve countries plus Europol: the United Kingdom, France, Germany, Denmark, Netherlands, Spain, Greece, Poland, Ukraine, Belgium, Lithuania and Norway. Not all entries are current active deployments. Each row carries a status label to distinguish confirmed deployments from pilots, partnerships, letters of intent and blocked or historical procurements.

Every private-sector row below is a Foundry deployment or a Foundry-family relationship. AIP appears twice as an add-on to existing Foundry customers (Stellantis renewal in 2026, BP in 2024) but not as a standalone European sale in this dataset. Gotham does not appear in the private-sector table.

The following entry did not meet the evidence standard applied to the tables above and is listed separately.

Some Foundry environments use customer-specific hostnames beneath palantirfoundry.com. Palantir also supports custom or customer-managed domains, so absence of a resolving palantirfoundry.com hostname for a company is weak negative evidence. Two customer-named palantirfoundry.com hostnames resolved during testing on 20 July 2026:

A third Palantir-related hostname was identified in Merck KGaA's certificate-transparency history under the merckgroup.com apex: foundry-api-extender.p.uptimize.merckgroup.com. This corroborates Merck KGaA's own 2018 disclosure of the Syntropy joint venture.

The other candidates tried during research (Airbus, Stellantis, Ferrari, BP, Sanofi, Novartis, Swiss Re, Ringier, UniCredit, Société Générale, UK MoD, NHS England, FCA, DGSI, Rheinmetall, Helsing, several Länder police forces) returned NXDOMAIN under obvious tenant-name variants. That does not mean those customers have no tenant; tenant slugs are not required to match the company name, and Palantir supports custom domains. Full enumeration of the palantirfoundry.com namespace would require sustained certificate-transparency ingestion which is out of scope for this article.

Five European decisions between April and July 2026, together with one earlier historical cancellation, are relevant to Palantir's near-term footprint in Europe. They are materially different from each other and should not be lumped together as a single withdrawal trend.

Some Palantir contracts are drawing criticism that has nothing to do with US ownership. The £330m NHS Federated Data Platform contract is drawing independent delivery-and-value criticism: reporting by Democracy for Sale quotes internal NHS analysts describing the platform in critical terms, and select-committee submissions have called for review on delivery-value grounds. The Metropolitan Police case likewise reads as a procurement dispute (with an active High Court claim by Palantir) rather than a pure sovereignty rejection. Both examples broaden the picture: European Palantir contracts can come apart for procurement or execution reasons, not only for sovereignty.

We found no publicly documented Irish deployment meeting this article's evidence threshold. The absence of public evidence does not prove that no undisclosed deployment exists; it means none surfaced during research using the sources listed in the methodology. The obvious candidates (An Garda Síochána, Defence Forces, HSE, Revenue Commissioners, Ryanair, AIB, Bank of Ireland, CRH, Kerry Group, Kingspan, Smurfit Kappa, Flutter, DAA) returned no procurement notice, official announcement or credible named-source report.

Two Irish-adjacent items are worth mentioning even though neither is a customer relationship. The Irish Strategic Investment Fund, the state's sovereign wealth arm managed by the NTMA, holds a passive equity stake in Palantir Technologies. Sinn Féin justice spokesperson Matt Carthy TD raised the shareholding in the Dáil in December 2025; the Tánaiste defended it. Sinn Féin's public statement put the value at roughly €1m at that point. Later coverage by The Irish Times (Dec 2024) and The Canary (Jul 2026) reported larger figures at different points in time. The canonical source for holdings and valuation is the NTMA / ISIF annual report; the figures used above are the values reported by the sources cited and should be reconciled against the ISIF annual report before republication. An ISIF equity holding is not a purchase or deployment of Palantir software.

Separately, the Police Service of Northern Ireland maintains a public Freedom of Information disclosure log for Palantir-related queries and returns "Neither Confirm Nor Deny" to every request. That is the standard National Police Chiefs' Council response used across UK forces. It is not a confirmation of use and it is not a denial. Amnesty International UK is separately campaigning to keep Palantir out of Northern Ireland's health service, which is administered separately from NHS England under the Stormont Executive; the Federated Data Platform contract does not extend to Northern Ireland.

Grouping the entries above by the country of the awarding public body or the country of the private customer's headquarters:

The UK sits at nine, eight of which are in the public sector; the next country (Germany) is at five, and everything after that at four or below. The UK Ministry of Defence entered a Palantir enterprise agreement reported at approximately £240m; alongside the agreement, Palantir committed to invest up to £1.5bn in its UK presence and ecosystem. The £1.5bn is a Palantir investment commitment, not UK government contract revenue, and should not be counted as UK public-sector spend on Palantir. Germany's five entries include three Länder-level police forces (Hessen, NRW, Bavaria) and two private companies (Merck KGaA, Axel Springer); the Bundeswehr federal defence procurement was not awarded to Palantir. Switzerland reaches three entirely through the private sector (Novartis, Swiss Re, Ringier).

Sonnedix illustrates the difficulty of assigning a single country to a multinational deployment: the parent is headquartered in London, the operating base is in Madrid, and the resolving tenant hostname (sonnedix.palantirfoundry.com, 20 July 2026) points at AWS eu-west-3 in Paris. Country in a European corporate table is a choice of measurement, not a fact.

Inclusion criterion. An entry appears in the tables above when it is supported by at least one of:

Each row carries a status label so evidence class is visible per entry. Labels used: Confirmed active deployment; Confirmed deployment, current status unclear; Pilot; Strategic partnership / proposed regional hub; Letter of intent; Infrastructure observation; Existing deployment being replaced or reduced. Reported relationships that did not meet the evidence threshold are listed in a separate table rather than counted in the main total.

Technical observations are supporting evidence, not proof of a current commercial contract. A resolving palantirfoundry.com hostname does not by itself establish current use, product scope, or contract start date. NXDOMAIN for a company-name guess is weak negative evidence and does not establish absence of a deployment: Palantir supports custom customer-managed domains and tenant slugs do not necessarily match the company name.

Publicly linked does not always mean currently active customer. Contract status may be unknown; pilots may not convert; letters of intent may lapse without a signed contract. The dataset is a dated public-source snapshot at 20 July 2026.

Excluded from the tables: aggregator sites (bloomberry.com, appsruntheworld.com, TheirStack, WebSets) which list customers without primary sourcing; Wikipedia and analyst blogs when they were the sole source. Both were used as lead generators for follow-up research but never as sole citation. Where a City AM, The Register, Cybernews, NL Times, TIME, Bloomberg or similar outlet is cited, the label "Independent reporting" is used because the outlet is not a primary participant in the relationship.

If a European organisation's publicly documented Palantir relationship has been missed, we will consider adding it: contact research@ciphercue.com with the source link.

The public record shows that Palantir has established a broad but uneven European footprint across defence, policing, health, manufacturing, finance, pharmaceuticals and media. The strength of the underlying evidence varies: some relationships are confirmed active deployments, while others are pilots, partnerships, letters of intent or technical observations. Recent European decisions also do not form a single withdrawal trend. They include the replacement of existing systems (France, DGSI), efforts to reduce future dependency (Netherlands MoD), prospective procurements not awarded or blocked (Bundeswehr, Metropolitan Police), reported government guidance affecting some state-controlled entities (Spain), and delivery-value criticism of existing contracts (NHS Federated Data Platform). This article should be read as a dated map of publicly documented relationships, not as a definitive list of every current Palantir customer in Europe. Snapshot as at 20 July 2026.

title: KDE for Enterprise Needs a Strong PIM Infrastructure
description: Website Description
fullText:
Tags:

 kde,

 pim,

 business,

 enioka

This post has been long overdue, but let’s say I’ve been busy and the reason will be obvious in this short piece. ☺

As you might have heard a couple of months ago, the Sovereign Tech Fund did a large investment in KDE software development. From the announcement the details can be a bit sparse on what it actually entails. Especially regarding the part which says “the frameworks underlying its communication services”. It turns out this is what enioka Haute Couture is contracted by KDE e.V. to work on. Let me bring a bit more details about it.

The overarching theme of the investment is to make the KDE ecosystem more desirable for enterprise and public institution uses. The desktop shell (Plasma) and having a strong base to distribute it (KDE Linux) obviously comes to mind, but it’s also about the Personal Information Management (PIM) space. Nowadays a good chunk of the institutional life (public or otherwise) is to deal with email, contacts and calendars. So we set sail to strengthen Kontact, KMail, Korganizer and friends. More specifically, we want to strengthen its underlying infrastructure: Akonadi and its resources.

So what exactly are we working on currently? Well, I propose you attend the Akademy 2026 talk of my colleagues which will cover what we do on KDE PIM!

OK, OK, you’re still here? So read on for a few more details even though I obviously don’t want to steal their thunder.

There are three axis we’re focusing on:

Those who know me also know what it means: more tests!!!

To be fair on the Akonadi server and resources side, we’re not too bad in terms of unit tests. The underlying protocol libraries as well have some unit test. The DAV side is maybe a bit weak and we hope to fix that, but on the IMAP side things aren’t too shabby.

That being said, we didn’t really have a test suite which would cover all the components integrated together. Those are more expensive and less fun to develop, so we’d be working on that by adopting a proof of concept done by Dan Vratil a while ago. It changed quite a lot but is becoming stronger with a comprehensive test suite covering IMAP and DAV.

Unsurprisingly it uncovered quite a few bugs we didn’t know about before starting the work, and gave us a nice environment to reproduce long known bugs that we had a hard time to chase down previously.

Of course, we’re working through the list of failing tests to fix everything we can. Some of my long hated bugs are already gone! Well, I use the master branch… so I live in the future from you dear readers, and the future is definitely better here.

Our PIM suite supports many protocols… but in the case of enterprise use in a sovereign context (so having some control on the server side, and using open protocols), IMAP4, CalDAV, and iTIP (for invitations) are kings. So we’re focusing on modernising our support for them.

In particular we’re aiming at better support of IMAP4rev2 enabled servers, but also QRESYNC. This should bring a better use of network resources and faster resync.

On the DAV side, we’re looking at supporting push notifications. This is currently not completely standardised but we’ll be ready as soon as it is. Everything will be in place to track the specification as it matures. Thanks to this work, we’ll also improve the syncing code of the DAV resource, again using less network bandwidth.

Obviously iTIP support benefits from both. It also highlights issues on the DAV support which we’re fixing as we find them.

The architecture of Akonadi means that a user setup of the solution has quite a few components to configure. This is not necessarily a problem in itself, but we could make things easier and at times it’s mostly about configuring each protocol manually and separately.

Of course, there’s a specification proposed to support autoconfiguration based on just an email and a password. We got some partial support for it in KMail, but we’ll work on completing this and bringing it closer to System Settings. Couple that to some device management facilities in say… KDE Linux and that starts to look like a strong proposal for enterprise.

Finally, we’ll look into the Flatpak version of our PIM suite and see how to make it easier to integrate in the desktop. The possibilities are fairly limited for now and we’d like to make it first class going forward.

Obviously for the time being we can expect more activity in KDE PIM which is good. We have quite a few patches in the PIM repositories at this point and some of the improvement will be released really soon as part of 26.08! Some more will come in the next release (26.12) of course but it still required too much QA and missed the freeze. No matter, it’s not a long wait.

Also, we hope that it’ll be easier to deploy and setup when we get to tackle the Flatpak related tasks. Really looking forward to this! We hope to deliver this by the end of the year or early 2027. Which would mark the end of the project for us.

Later on, I hope this infrastructure work will attract more users and contributors again. Indeed, there’s still interesting work needed on the application side of things, but if the protocol support and quality of the base system is stronger, it makes it easier to try new things with the applications, less pieces to fight with will make the whole endeavour more desirable.
