title: Israel is building a miles-long earthen barrier inside Gaza, entrenching its division
description: The Israeli military is quietly but quickly building a giant earthen barrier separating the over 50% of the Gaza Strip it controls from the rest of the territory, according to satellite imagery, furth...
fullText:
CAIRO (AP) — The Israeli military is quietly but quickly building a giant earthen barrier separating the over 50% of the Gaza Strip it controls from the rest of the territory, according to satellite imagery, further entrenching the division of the tiny Palestinian enclave.

More than 23 kilometers (14 miles) have been constructed in recent months, running through Palestinian communities demolished by the military. That is equivalent to more than half the length of the coastal strip, which is some 40 kilometers (25 miles) long and 11 kilometers (7 miles) wide, and home to over 2 million Palestinians.

When presented with the satellite images, Israel's military confirmed to The Associated Press it has built a physical barrier in the area of the so-called yellow line, the boundary to which Israeli troops withdrew under the October ceasefire deal with Hamas. The line was envisioned in the U.S.-backed agreement as a temporary division of the territory pending a fuller Israeli withdrawal.

But as the truce stagnates, it appears Israel is digging in, heightening Palestinian fears that the line is morphing into a border. Neither U.S. Central Command, which is tasked with monitoring the ceasefire, nor the Board of Peace, the body led by U.S. President Donald Trump meant to eventually oversee Gaza, would comment on the barrier.

Along with the barrier, the military told AP it has developed a "security zone" around the line, equipped with intelligence and technological assets. It declined to give details on the route of the barrier when asked how far it would be extended. It said the barrier aimed to prevent infiltration and protect Israeli troops and Israeli communities near Gaza.

Since the ceasefire deal took hold, Palestinians in Gaza have feared Israel would not return the area of Gaza it controls. But few appear to be aware of the berms now being built around them — because of the danger of even approaching that area.

A portion of the berm network in southern Gaza was extended by more than 2 kilometers (1.2 miles) in two weeks this month, according to the satellite images provided by Planet Labs PBC.

The berm was about 500 meters (0.3 miles) long on July 1. By July 15, it was about 2.4 kilometers (1.5 miles), splicing the Israel-controlled ruins of the city of Rafah from Muwasi, site of some of the largest Palestinian tent camps.

If construction continues in its current direction, it will join up with the longest stretch of the barrier, which runs nearly unbroken for around 17 kilometers (10 miles) from the southern city of Khan Younis to near Gaza City, in the north.

Construction began on that stretch in February, and it appears work to extend it further south is continuing, according to the satellite images. More than a kilometer (0.6 miles) was added between June 21 and July 7, running close to an Israeli military base built on the ruins of the town of Bani Suheila, near Khan Younis.

In the far north of Gaza, several unconnected stretches of berms run just outside the largely leveled town of Beit Lahia.

The height of the berms in each place remains unclear. In some places, the barriers appear to run outside the yellow line.

Gaza's population has been crowded into the coastal area west of the yellow line, most living in squalid tent camps and dependent on aid.

On the other side of the line, satellite imagery shows that Israeli forces have leveled the vast majority of buildings using bulldozers and explosives, virtually erasing several towns and Rafah, once home to more than a quarter million people.

They have carved new road networks and erected new bases on the ruins of Palestinian towns. Agricultural land has also been destroyed. The Israeli military says it is demolishing infrastructure used by Hamas.

The yellow line was never precisely defined, whether in the ceasefire deal or by Israeli or U.S. officials, and it has been inconsistently marked on the ground. The military told AP it is working to clearly mark the route of the yellow line to "reduce friction and prevent harm to uninvolved individuals."

Israeli forces have killed Palestinians who came close to the line or crossed it. The Israeli military says it fires on those believed to be militants threatening its troops. But those killed have included children and other civilians seemingly unaware of the line. Some soldiers have said they don't always know who they are firing on and have orders to kill anyone crossing the boundary.

Israeli forces have killed more than 1,100 Palestinians since the ceasefire began, most in airstrikes and drone strikes around Gaza. The Israeli military says it is targeting militants. Militant attacks have killed five Israeli soldiers in the same period.

Under the ceasefire deal, Israel is eventually supposed to withdraw its troops further to the very edges of Gaza, to be replaced by an international security force. But implementation of the deal has been stalled for months.

A former U.N. diplomat now in charge of coordinating the ceasefire has blamed Hamas' failure to disarm. Sporadic talks to reach a formula for decommissioning its weapons appear to have made little progress, and Hamas accuses Israel in turn of violating the ceasefire.

The war began after Hamas-led militants attacked Israeli communities on Oct. 7, 2023, killing around 1,200 people, mostly civilians, and taking 251 others hostage. Israel responded with a punishing military offensive that has killed more than 73,000 people, according to the Gaza Health Ministry.

The ministry, which was part of the recently dissolved Hamas-run government, maintains detailed records viewed as generally reliable by U.N. agencies and international organizations. It does not distinguish between civilians and militants but says women and children make up around half of all fatalities.

Frankel reported from Jerusalem.

title: Salt's path home from Barbados to Welsh Fire captain
description: England star Phil Salt has had a circuitous route to captaining Welsh Fire in The Hundred, which starts this week.
fullText:
Born in Bodelwyddan, raised in Barbados, back in Wales for the summer as a genuine England star.

It's been quite a cricketing journey for Phil Salt, the big-hitting England T20 international who's preparing to captain Welsh Fire men in The Hundred.

The 29-year-old leads a team that includes former England captain Joe Root and ex-Test batter Chris Woakes, with their campaign starting with a men's and women's double-header at Southern Brave on 22 July.

Salt would seem a natural fit as skipper given his heritage - born in north Wales to a Welsh mother, raised initially in St Asaph - the birthplace of Welsh football icon Ian Rush - with a clear sense of his heritage despite living away for almost 20 years.

"When the auctions were happening earlier this year, it felt like a no-brainer to join Welsh Fire," says Salt, the top men's scorer in The Hundred with 1,138 runs since the competition started in 2021.

"I'm very proud of being a North Walian, of being born and brought up in that part of the world until I was 11.

"When I moved to Barbados, because of my dad's job, it was a bit of a job trying to explain to people where I was from.

"The other kids would say to me 'north Wales? Where's that?!' I was giving out geography lessons aged 11. But I always felt Welsh. 100%."

What has changed in The Hundred for 2026?

Salt goes into Welsh Fire's new campaign having played a key role for England as an opening batter in their 4-0 T20 series win over India.

The Lancashire batter, who top-scored in the third T20 with 70, has been a mainstay of white-ball English cricket since his debut in 2021.

Facing Pakistan in a one-day international in Cardiff made him the first Welshman to represent England since former Glamorgan pace bowler Simon Jones in 2005.

Salt, though, says his Welsh mum was not entirely comfortable with him pulling on the England shirt in the first place.

"Being a proud Welshman, but obviously representing England... sometimes it's a bit of a grey area," he said.

"[Mum was] always very supportive, got behind the boys, but that's about where it stops. I've got to be careful sometimes with how I word it."

It is possible Salt might have ended with a red Welsh dragon on his shirt instead of three lions had he stuck to his first sporting love as a child.

"As I kid, I was more into my football than anything else - I played for Denbigh Town and Rhyl," he said.

"I was a very honest footballer, shall we say - sitting in the middle of the park, doing a lot of running - I wasn't breaking any records.

"But I started playing cricket for North-East Wales Under-11s as an under-age player. That was like getting a ticket to a chocolate factory to me, it was unbelievable.

"Then I moved to Barbados and cricket kind of became the focus.

"Any time the West Indies were playing in Barbados, we'd go and watch them.

"It's ridiculous, the talent out there. They must produce the most cricketers in the world per square foot."

A cricket scholarship in Surrey brought Salt back to the UK at 15, before a professional breakthrough at Sussex and then on to Lancashire and the Indian Premier League.

Top run-scorer, twice a finalist: Salt certainly has pedigree in The Hundred.

The Welshman reached the final with Manchester Originals in 2022 and 2023 but believes he can go one step further by sparking a dormant Welsh Fire into life.

The Cardiff-based team are the worst-performing franchise in the tournament's short history, with both the men's and women's teams finishing rock bottom last year.

Neither side has won the tournament and the men are yet to reach the play-offs, but with tech billionaire Sanjay Govil buying a 50% share of the team for £40m there is hope that this year could be different.

"Historically, Welsh Fire haven't done that well, they haven't given fans much to get excited about, but I see this as a good opportunity to change things. It excites me," said Salt.

Salt said the Sophia Gardens pitch, along with dressing room music from indie-rock band Blossoms, should be conducive to high run-scoring.

Two other 2026 recruits - Glamorgan pair Ben Kellaway and Asa Tribe - will bring that local knowledge and a Welsh heartbeat to the team.

"We've got so much experience in the dressing room," said Salt.

"There's been a lot of talk about the wicket - it looks like a really good place to play cricket when you look at the Glamorgan scores this season.

"We've got to start fast, and play lots of front-foot, aggressive cricket.

"Can we win the competition? Definitely. I don't see why not."

Grace Ballinger column: How will a revamped Hundred play out?

title: Introducing football to the country with one pitch
description: Nauru is the only nation to have never played an official international football match and hardly anyone there plays the game. Is that about to change?
fullText:
Like everywhere, the world's smallest island nation has been gripped by football fever for the past six weeks. But once Spain lifted the World Cup trophy on Sunday, almost every trace of football on Nauru stopped.

Football is almost non-existent on this Pacific island. Nauru has never fielded a team in an official international match and it has just one – hard sand – pitch.

While there is interest in big Premier League matches, and children wear Lionel Messi and Cristiano Ronaldo kits, any attempts to actually play football tend to be mocked, unless it's Aussie rules.

But things may be slowly changing as a group of volunteers - led by British former academy player Charlie Pomroy - seek to introduce the game to what was once the world's richest nation per capita but is now heavily reliant on foreign aid and has one of the highest obesity rates in the world.

A social media post from the Nauru Soccer Federation congratulating the new world champions and setting out a dream that "one day we hope to be in Spain's position" may have been fanciful, but the groundwork is real in what it says is the last country not to have had a senior international side.

Laying the foundations, though, on an island where over-mining has left the landscape looking more like a moonscape, has been far from straightforward.

Nauru has tried to develop football previously, but it has never really taken off. The closest it has come to playing an international match was when representative teams faced a Solomon Islands expatriate team in 1994 and a refugee side in 2014.

Its complicated past goes some way to explaining why the world's most popular sport has struggled for participation.

Located in the Pacific Ocean 2,500 miles north east of Australia, Nauru's historical wealth was built on its vast deposits of phosphate from fossilised bird poo.

Nauru, which was a dependent territory of Australia until independence in 1968, was heavily mined in the late 20th Century. The minerals dried up, leaving the country once named Pleasant Island looking ragged, with large parts of its central areas uninhabitable and extremely challenging to farm.

This resulted in most of the 12,000-strong population clustering around the coastal edges with usable space at a premium on an island that measures only 21 square kilometres. And even that is shrinking, with climate change and rising sea levels threatening its very existence.

Its barren land has left the country importing more than 90% of its food – much of which is processed, contributing to 69% of the population being classed as obese and around 40% having type 2 diabetes.

One of its main sources of revenue now is from the Australian immigration detention centre it has housed for much of this century.

Everyday life on the island can be a struggle and sport has been a strong release for many, but the sport of choice is Aussie rules, which 30% of locals play. Also popular are athletics and weightlifting, which Nauru has competed in at the Olympics.

Nauru will host the Micronesian Games in 2028 – a multi-sport event that includes football and will showcase the island's desire to grow its international reputation through sport.

"They are trying to change the image for the better and get involved in more sport," says Pomroy, technical director for Nauru Soccer and one of eight volunteers aiming to develop football on the island.

"They [Nauru] want people to see the country in a more positive light rather than the punchline of a joke.

"So whether it's football, whether it's Aussie rules, whether it's rugby, basketball, cricket, weightlifting...  just to be part of building a healthier Nauru is enough.

"But being a football lover, the best way I know how to have an impact is through football."

And the Briton, who is originally from Stevenage and worked at his hometown club when they were still in non-league, has a track history to prove it.

He previously set up a charity in the Cambodian city of Siem Reap which ended up turning into a professional academy that has so far produced 47 players who have progressed to play professionally in the Asian country, and is also running successful programmes in India bringing football to underserved communities.

All of his projects pose different challenges. One of the first challenges in Nauru is getting some footballs on to the island.

The volunteers have launched a campaign to give every child aged four to 14 a ball – roughly 2,000 children – and are working with churches and the island's 11 schools to deliver them. The balls will get delivered 200 at a time by plane to help manage the distribution and avoid potential waste.

Past attempts to introduce football to Nauru have focused on one-off fixtures, including two years ago when former Premier League striker Dave Kitson was appointed to lead a group of 18 Nauruans into a game after a two-week training camp.

Their opponents were set to be a Sunday league team from Reading called XL FC who use football as a way to achieve a healthier lifestyle. That match never happened for financial reasons and so the wait for that inaugural game goes on.

But the ethos has since changed and Nauru Soccer are looking longer term.

"Dave Kitson would have gone, I would have gone, and it would have been great for a couple of months, but it's a lot of money," says Pomroy, who has worked in football in every continent bar South America and Antarctica.

"Let's start a culture on this island of football or sport and help it grow, because it's an important thing. Let's build the ecosystem, not just one-off games.

"My personal dream is that Nauru's first ever international game is played with all kids that are 18 years old that had been coming through our system.

"So, say we found a group of 14-year-olds now and we said, 'Right, let's work with this group over the next three or four years'.

"And by the time they're 17-18 years old, that will be Nauru's first ever national team game."

Once Pomroy arrives for a training camp in December, he will set up coaching programmes for players and aspiring coaches so the sport can continue throughout the year.

A lot of this hinges on being able to find places to play.

Much of the land is privately owned or uninhabitable, but volunteer Tyrone Deiye is hoping to use some of his family's land to create more pitches than the sole one that currently exists - a dusty surface of phosphate over crushed dirt. He also says playing on indoor pitches may be an option.

Pomroy is keen to stress that Nauru Soccer is not trying to "force football down everyone's throat" but says it's about laying a foundation for a sport, promoting healthy lifestyles and building communities.

"The life lessons that you learn through football aren't just on the pitch, not just about winning and losing," says Pomroy, who manages this project while coaching in India.

"It's the friends that you make. It is learning to be humble. It's learning to look after your body. It's learning to get up when you fall down.

"And that's how I've seen it change people across the world. That's the dream for Nauru.

"If we sat here in five years' time, I genuinely believe that we will have a senior team that will be filled with 18, 19, 20-year-olds.

"Then in 20 years' time, someone from Nauru comes up to me and says, 'my life changed through football and the development programme'... That would be the ultimate dream."

Other Pacific island nations play football and expats from these countries sometimes play on Nauru, although Deiye says Nauruans "watch the expats play but they sort of laugh at them because they don't understand the game, they think it's funny".

"A lot of the other Pacific islands have got really good football teams and it makes those guys really happy," adds Deiye, who balances work as an urban planner in Nauru alongside his football work. "Those countries, they're really big into football. I want Nauru to be like that."

Last year, the Marshall Islands played the US Virgin Islands in their first international match. The Pacific island had been the self-proclaimed last nation on earth without a football team, but Nauru have also been claiming this tag.

There was also confusion in the middle of the Covid pandemic when a fake domestic league in Nauru was announced, with the hoax being very detailed down to team names and registered players.

But the Nauru Soccer Federation now has a real website, where it outlines its plans and also sells a national team shirt to raise funds to grow the game. Its main source of money for the project is through fundraisers, but that can be difficult to organise as the volunteers are also balancing full-time work and different time zones.

Even communication between the project's team members can be tricky – sometimes Deiye will not be contactable for a couple of days, only to come back to say that a storm had wiped out the internet on the island.

"This project's not short of challenges, for sure," says Pomroy.

"I think from what I can understand, Nauruans have been promised quite a lot throughout their history and been let down many times.

"So their kind of attitude towards things tends to be 'we believe it when we see it' and they're very reluctant to get their hopes up.

"But in general, the feedback we get is everyone is very, very excited about it."

title: Fake kits worth £18m seized by UK police over safety concerns
description: Counterfeit football shirts worth over £18m were seized in the UK during the course of the World Cup because of safety concerns, with the Fire Service warning the outfits can be a danger to those who wear them.
fullText:
Counterfeit football shirts worth over £18m were seized in the UK during the course of the World Cup because of safety concerns.

The tournament produced a spike in the sales of fake shirts and kits, but the Fire Service has warned that these items are far more flammable and dangerous than the genuine articles.

More than 220,000 pieces of football merchandise were recovered by the Police Intellectual Property Office as part of Operation Bloxwich, a UK police enforcement effort targeting counterfeit goods.

One raid involved 1,618 fake strips being taken from a warehouse in Birmingham, including replicas of star players such as France's Kylian Mbappe and Norway's Erling Haaland.

Detective Sergeant Jamie Kirk, who took part in the operation, told BBC Sport: "I understand why people want to buy these shirts, people love football and the incentive is the lower price - but these shirts, they're not regulated and could be highly flammable.

"They have dyes in them which could be harmful to skin, particularly children's skin. While the cost is lower - and that's the attractive part - potential damages to you and the people making them is just far too high."

Cheshire Fire and Rescue Service tested the flammability of counterfeit shirts compared with genuine kit and found the fake kits were much more flammable.

"It's quite clear that the counterfeit one melted as soon as the burner went on it," said watch manager Dave Jackson, who carried out the test by hanging them two at a time on an iron bar and used blow torches to set them alight. "I really had to try to make the genuine one ignite and melt."

One of the factors driving the sales of counterfeit kits is their low price. Several websites sell them for as little as £12, while the cost of some official Premier League strips start at £85.

"It's extremely dangerous. It's something that can cause horrific injuries to your children. It's just not worth the savings," Jackson added.

Kate Caffrey, head of intelligence at the Intellectual Property Office, warned that fake shirts are not regulated.

"Counterfeit strips are not subject to health and safety regulations, British Standards [or] EU standards," she told BBC Sport.

"We've got evidence that counterfeit goods are made in appalling conditions, so you just don't know what's in them, how they're being made and there's no way of providing any safety reassurances.

"Counterfeiting is criminal, these are manufactured and supplied by criminal networks, criminal gangs and they're very adept at targeting markets where there's demand. So the World Cup, the forthcoming Premier League season - they're going to flood the market with fake shirts.

"The price of a fake shirt, you don't know what you're funding with that money."

title: Grizzlies-Magic Preseason Game Will Bring Desmond Bane Back to Memphis
description: Desmond Bane will return to FedExForum for the first time since his 2025 trade when the Magic visit the rebuilding Grizzlies in the preseason.
fullText:
Desmond Bane is set to return to Memphis since being traded in 2025 when the Orlando Magic visit the Memphis Grizzlies at FedExForum in the preseason.

The Magic finalized their preseason games on Monday and will play two preseason road games. Orlando Magic beat reporter Jason Beede reported that Bane will make his first return to FedExForum since he was traded in June 2025.

"Desmond Bane will return to FedExForum for the first time since last summer's blockbuster trade when the #Magic open the preseason Oct. 7 at Memphis," Beed tweeted on July 20.

New: Desmond Bane will return to FedExForum for the first time since last summer's blockbuster trade when the #Magic open the preseason Oct. 7 at Memphis.

Orlando, which finalized its preseason slate Monday, will play two preseason road games in 2026: https://t.co/5Up5EIf3lQ

— Jason Beede (@therealBeede) July 20, 2026

Bane was drafted in the first round of the 2020 NBA Draft to the Boston Celtics, who traded his rights to the Grizzlies. He spent five seasons in Memphis, where the team made the playoffs four times, including a conference semi-finals run in 2022.

The 2020 draft played a pivotal role for the Grizzlies, as a 3-and-D player who developed offensive prowess with the team. During his time with the franchise, he averaged 17.8 points, 4.6 rebounds, and 3.8 assists per game over 313 games played.

Although Bane was traded to Orlando in 2025 after Memphis lost to the Oklahoma City Thunder 4-0, he wasn't able to play against his former team. The Magic and Grizzlies did play in 2026; it was in Europe because both of their regular-season matchups were overseas.

NBA HISTORY IN BERLIN: The Memphis Grizzlies meet the Orlando Magic in the NBA Berlin Game 2026 presented by Tissot today at 2pm/et on Prime, marking the league’s first regular-season game in Germany. Magic leading scorer Franz Wagner is set to return from a 16-game absence and… pic.twitter.com/ERKLOEnNFp

Even though Bane will go down in Memphis history, the franchise is rebuilding after parting ways with all its past cornerstones.

During the NBA Draft, Memphis also drafted Karim Lopez at No. 21 and Richie Saunders at No. 32. On June 24, the organization acquired Isaiah Stewart from the Detroit Pistons.

Memphis traded Morant after Jaren Jackson Jr. was traded in early February 2026.  While the team acquired Stewart, who was traded due to the Detroit Pistons wanting to send him to a team that would provide playing time, the Grizzlies also signed Carson Cooper to a two-way contract and recently landed AJ Johnson in a trade.

The Grizzlies will be a young team, but the front office will decide how to carve the direction of the roster.

With Morant in Portland, Boozer will be expected to help lead this franchise in its newest era. Some have labeled him as the most pro-ready player from the draft. Boozer had a consistent Summer in both the Salt Lake City and Las Vegas Summer Leagues.

title: Soccer legend moving on from broadcasting following World Cup: ‘Farewell to the studio’
description: Former Swedish soccer star Zlatan Ibrahimovic is moving on from broadcasting after making his debut with FOX Sports for the World Cup.
fullText:
Swedish soccer legend Zlatan Ibrahimovic, who joined FOX Sports as an analyst for the World Cup, is moving on from broadcasting.

Ibrahimovic announced following Sunday’s final between Spain and Argentina that the 2026 World Cup will be his one and only event as a broadcaster.

“I’ve been talking a lot the last one and a half months, so these will be my last words,” Ibrahimovic said on air. “It has been an absolutely pleasure to share this studio with you guys. You made it very easy for me. I want to thank also FOX studio, I want to thank America, I want to thank also all the other people you don’t see. … You made it easy for me.

“I hope America, you enjoyed it as much as I did. And this is also my farewell to the studio. This was the first and last time for me, so thank you everybody.”

Ibrahimovic drew widespread praise throughout the World Cup for his work as an analyst.

Andrew Marchand, who covers sports media for The Athletic, described him as “a World Cup TV star.”

Still, Sweden’s all-time leading goal scorer says he is moving on from broadcasting.

In addition to playing for the Swedish national team, Ibrahimovic also played for AC Milan, FC Barcelona, Inter Milan, Juventus, Manchester United, Paris Saint-Germain and other professional teams during his career.

He scored 62 goals with the Swedish national team and is considered to be one of the best goal scorers in soccer history.

title: LeBron James replies to Tyrese Haliburton’s Pacers pitch
description: Several NBA stars are reportedly doing their best to lure LeBron James to join them, including Tyrese Haliburton. The Indiana Pacers guard revealed that he pitched to him via a text message, and the free agent forward replied to him.…
fullText:
Several NBA stars are reportedly doing their best to lure LeBron James to join them, including Tyrese Haliburton. The Indiana Pacers guard revealed that he pitched to him via a text message, and the free agent forward replied to him.

However, it’s not what Haliburton hoped for. James didn’t use words; instead, he sent six emojis to sum up how he feels about taking his talents to Indianapolis

MORE: LeBron James NBA sweepstakes has familiar team emerge as heavy favorite

“LeBron being my teammate isn’t looking good,” Haliburton said. “I texted him, ‘Hey, you should come be a Pacer.’ He responded with six laughing crying faces.”

The Miami Heat, Cleveland Cavaliers, Philadelphia 76ers, and Golden State Warriors reportedly lead the race to secure the 22-time All-Star’s signature. Reports also suggested that money does not influence James’ decision the most, but where he will have the best chance to win his fifth championship.

James was initially rumored to make his free agency decision early this month but the NBA world continues to await his announcement. His camp has remained mum on providing an exact time and date.

Many believe the Heat are the favorites to land him; however, joining Joel Embiid and Jaylen Brown in Philadelphia is gaining traction. Golden State also has a fighting chance as James shares a strong friendship with Draymond Green and Stephen Curry. It remains to be seen which team he will play for next season, but what’s certain is that his decision will change the league’s landscape.

title: Kings’ Acuff Expected To Score More Than 2021-22 Brunson
description: Analysts predict high-volume scoring for Sacramento’s seventh overall pick, but inconsistent efficiency remains a concern as the rookie studies Jalen Brunson’s film to master elite NBA footwork.
fullText:
Sactown Sports radio host Carmichael Dave has set Jalen Brunson's fourth year in the NBA as the standard for Darius Acuff to surpass in his rookie season with the Sacramento Kings.

At the time, Brunson averaged 16.3 points for the Dallas Mavericks in the 2021-22 season. Dave expects Acuff to average more than Brunson did, but would be "falling out of my chair" shocked if he accomplishes his goal efficiently.

"I fully expect Darius Acuff to average in the 17-18 point range," Dave told his co-host, Jason Russ, on "The Carmichael Dave Show" on Monday. "But I would also be shocked if Darius Acuff shot anything above 42%, probably high 30s."

Sacramento's seventh overall pick wasn't an effective scorer in Summer League. He failed to shoot 50% or better from the field in every game, except in the final appearance against the Brooklyn Nets.

Acuff put together some abysmal shooting performances: 9-for-29 in the California Classic opener, 7-for-19 against the Milwaukee Bucks, 6-for-20 in the Las Vegas Summer League opener and 4-for-14 against the Washington Wizards. That totals out to 31.8% overall.

The 6-foot-3 guard showed promise getting to his favorite spots on the floor, setting up his defenders with nifty dribble moves and blowing past them. He just couldn't convert, especially from three-point range.

As a small guard who isn't the most athletic player on the court, he has to rely on his craftiness to score. Size definitely played a role in Acuff's poor efficiency.

There is room for improvement for the former Arkansas Razorback, though. Receiving comparisons to Brunson influenced him to break down the New York Knicks' star's film from the NBA Finals.

“I’ve been focusing on both teams, of course, but really been seeing how Jalen Brunson is navigating through the defense and stuff like that," Acuff Jr. told Draymond Green on "The Draymond Green Show" on June 7. "Obviously, as a PG myself, I’ve been paying attention to that.”

Brunson's footwork is part of what makes him such an efficient scorer. When he picks up his dribble in a crowded paint, he twists and turns to see which way he wants to score. If a defender bites on the pump fake, he steps in for a floater; if he creates enough separation with a shoulder bump, he'll shoot a turnaround jumper.

Acuff can learn from his comparison's grounded scoring style. It allows him to absorb contact without being pushed off his spot, and continue surveying the defense for scoring or passing opportunities.

The former Razorback could meet Dave's expectations, especially since he led the SEC in scoring this past season at 23.5 points. His efficiency will take some refining, but he'll adjust to the NBA's pace sooner rather than later.

title: How David Malukas went from the hospital to third in Music City Grand Prix
description: How Team Penske's David Malukas went from the last spot to third, nearly winning the Borchetta Bourbon Music City Grand Prix on July 20.
fullText:
LEBANON — David Malukas’ weekend looked over before the green flag waved.

The Team Penske driver crashed four laps into the July 18 opening practice for the Borchetta Bourbon Music City Grand Prix at Nashville Superspeedway, injuring his right knee when it struck the steering wheel. He was sent to a local hospital for evaluation, forcing him to miss qualifying and left him starting the race dead last.

Malukas turned it into one of the best drives of his IndyCar career.

He went from 25th to third in the July 20 race after leading 59 laps, his best finish at the Nashville oval and putting him second in the IndyCar standings behind race winner Alex Palou. Hendersonville native Josef Newgarden finished second.

“That was the best race that I think I’ve ever performed,” Malukas said. “I was pushing so hard with everything going on. This is definitely going to be one that I’m not going to forget for a very long time.”

Malukas started strong after Team Penske vice president of competition Travis Law planned to keep the No. 12 Chevrolet on the track during the race’s opening caution on Lap 19, while all but four drivers pitted. The strategy put Malukas in the lead, where he stayed through multiple green-flag pit cycles despite driving on the worn primary tires.

“He’s just an absolute genius. He’s always seeing what’s going to happen. He put us on the right strategy, and we were able to jump the field,” Malukas said.

He appeared to have the race under control until Lap 124.

Malukas made his third and final pit stop from the lead on Lap 121. Three laps later, drivers Alexnder Rossi and Scott Dixon crashed, bringing out a caution before the rest of the leaders made their final pit stops. The yellow caution seemingly erased Malukas' advantage and saw him playing catch-up instead of defending the lead.

He made one final push in the remaining 25 laps, but ultimately fell short.

Malukas earned 36 points, leaving him 83 points behind Palou in the NTT IndyCar Series Championship standings, with five races remaining before the Aug. 9 race at Portland International Raceway. He said the extra day between Saturday’s crash and Monday’s race changed the outlook of the performance.

“Yesterday was survival, today was game on,” Malukas said. “Getting that extra night of sleep, I'm not gonna lie, I heard that rain on the bus, and I was like, 'Oh, that's nice.' "

This article originally appeared on Nashville Tennessean: How David Malukas went from the hospital to third in Music City Grand Prix

title: Minnesota Twins' Joe Ryan Endures Worst Start of Season vs. Guardians
description: Joe Ryan struggled against the Guardians on Monday.
fullText:
The Minnesota Twins have one of the best pitchers in MLB in Joe Ryan. He earned the second All-Star selection of his career this year. Ryan entered the ball game on Monday with a 2.85 ERA. One would have imagined he would have had a big start against one of the worst offenses in the sport, but that was not the case during the Twins' contest against the Cleveland Guardians on Monday.

Ryan surrendered eight runs across four innings while giving up a career-high six home runs. It was one of the worst starts of his big league career, and his worst start of the season. Ryan's previous career-high for most earned runs allowed this season was five. He had not allowed more than four earned runs in a game since early April.

The Guardians featured one of the worst offenses in the game. They are near the bottom of the league in categories such as runs scored, batting average and OPS. While the Guardians are in second place in the American League Central standings behind the Chicago White Sox, Cleveland's pitching has led the way.

Yet, on Monday, the Guards' lineup looked like one of the best offenses in baseball. Rhys Hoskins and Petey Halpin both connected for two home runs in the contest.

Anything can happen in any given baseball game. Some days, the best teams in baseball struggle. Other times, the worst teams have huge performances. On Monday, one of the worst offenses in the sport gave an All-Star pitcher no shortage of trouble.

Ryan is still a star pitcher. He is more than capable of getting back on track sooner rather than later. He had not pitched in a while given the All-Star break (he did pitch one inning during the All-Star game), so perhaps his schedule not being normal played a role in his underperformance.

Either way, the Twins are obviously hopeful that Ryan will get back on track in his next outing. Given Ryan's track record, he will likely figure things out and get back on track soon.

Minnesota entered the ball game in third place in the American League Central standings. The Twins are four games behind the White Sox for first place in the division.

For now, the ball club is focused on trying to come back in Monday's game as they trail the Guardians at the moment.

title: Ex-Eagles Star Gets Necessary Trade Push From NFL Analyst
description: All NFL offseason long, Josh Sweat’s name has been tossed in trade rumors. The former Philadelphia Eagles defensive end has been described as frustrated with the Arizona Cardinals. When OTAs were starting up across the league, many NFL rumors hinted that Sweat could see a change. The Eagles were often linked as trade candidates for […] The post Ex-Eagles Star Gets Necessary Trade Push From NFL Analyst appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
All NFL offseason long, Josh Sweat’s name has been tossed in trade rumors. The former Philadelphia Eagles defensive end has been described as frustrated with the Arizona Cardinals.

When OTAs were starting up across the league, many NFL rumors hinted that Sweat could see a change. The Eagles were often linked as trade candidates for their former draft pick.

But with training camp quickly approaching, the Sweat rumor mill has gone silent. Bleacher Report’s NFL writer Moe Moton is pushing the Cardinals to kick it back up, by listing a Sweat trade as something that needs to get done before training camp.

GLENDALE, ARIZONA – NOVEMBER 23: Josh Sweat #10 of the Arizona Cardinals looks on from the line in the game against the Jacksonville Jaguars at State Farm Stadium on November 23, 2025 in Glendale, Arizona. (Photo by Norm Hall/Getty Images)

“Josh Sweat needs a change of scenery,” Moton wrote.

“He’s played most of his career with the Philadelphia Eagles, a perennial playoff contender. This offseason, the Arizona Cardinals fired lead skipper Jonathan Gannon, who coached the veteran edge-rusher during their time together in Philadelphia.”

Sweat is reportedly unhappy in Arizona, as Moton noted. He is a win-now player, coming from a competitive NFC team. Sweat was recruited by his ex-Eagles coordinator. Now that Gannon’s out of the picture, the situation seems to be difficult for Sweat to overcome.

GLENDALE, ARIZONA – OCTOBER 05: Josh Sweat #10 of the Arizona Cardinals during the NFL game at State Farm Stadium on October 05, 2025 in Glendale, Arizona. The Titans defeated the Cardinals 22-21. (Photo by Christian Petersen/Getty Images)

In the same rundown of moves that need to happen, Moton suggested the Eagles should pick up the veteran defensive end, Von Miller. It would be easier, as Miller is a free agent and would require just a one-year commitment.

Sweat, on the other hand, is going into year two of a four-year, $76.4 million contract. He’s owed a lot and would need to be acquired via trade. Of course, the Cardinals aren’t going to accept pennies for the 12-sack pass rusher.

PHILADELPHIA, PA – DECEMBER 22: Josh Sweat #94 of the Philadelphia Eagles celebrates his sack on Dak Prescott #4 of the Dallas Cowboys during the fourth quarter at Lincoln Financial Field on December 22, 2019 in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. The Philadelphia Eagles defeated the Dallas Cowboys 17-9. (Photo by Corey Perrine/Getty Images)

Making the call is worth it, to at least figure out a price. If it’s within reason, the Eagles shouldn’t hesitate to pounce.

In 17 games with the Cardinals last season, Sweat racked up 30 total tackles, with 13 going for a loss. His 12.0 sacks marked a career high.

The last time the Eagles had Sweat in action, he picked up 41 tackles and 8.0 sacks. During the Super Bowl run, Sweat had 10 tackles, three for a loss, and 2.5 sacks.

The post Ex-Eagles Star Gets Necessary Trade Push From NFL Analyst appeared first on HEAVY.

title: Boston Red Sox Quietly Announce Release Of 30-Year-Old From Organization
description: On Monday night, the Boston Red Sox will open up a series with the Baltimore Orioles at Fenway Park.They most recently beat the Tampa Bay Rays by a score of 6-1 (on Sunday).The Red Sox are currently the hottest team in the MLB, as they have won 13 games in a row.Boston Red Sox Quietly […] The post Boston Red Sox Quietly Announce Release Of 30-Year-Old From Organization appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
On Monday night, the Boston Red Sox will open up a series with the Baltimore Orioles at Fenway Park.

They most recently beat the Tampa Bay Rays by a score of 6-1 (on Sunday).

The Red Sox are currently the hottest team in the MLB, as they have won 13 games in a row.

Chief Baseball Officer Craig Breslow of the Boston Red Sox looks on during a game between the Sacramento Kings and the Boston Celtics at TD Garden on January 10, 2025 in Boston, Massachusetts.

Ahead of Monday’s series, the Red Sox announced the news that they had released Matt Lloyd from their organization.

MLB.com wrote (on July 19): “Worcester Red Sox released 3B Matt Lloyd.”

He did not play in a game for their MLB team.

The 30-year-old had been playing for their Triple-A affiliate.

This season, he is batting .206 with 20 hits, five home runs, 15 RBIs and 14 runs in 30 games.

Lloyd was picked in the 15th round of the 2019 MLB Draft by the Cincinnati Reds out of Indiana.

In addition to the Reds and Red Sox, Lloyd has also spent time with the St. Louis Cardinals‘ organization.

That said, he has yet to make his MLB debut.

title: DeAndre Hopkins Speaks Out About New Ravens’ Wide Receiver
description: Former Baltimore Ravens wide receiver DeAndre Hopkins has a lot to say about one of the team's newest wideouts. The post DeAndre Hopkins Speaks Out About New Ravens’ Wide Receiver appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
He barely made much of a dent at a perennial problem position in 2025, but the Baltimore Ravens still took a chance by not bringing decorated wide receiver DeAndre Hopkins back this year. A risk compounded by the decision to replace the five-time Pro Bowler with a pair of mid-round picks from the 2026 NFL draft, and Hopkins has plenty to say about one of those rookie wideouts.

Responding to a video on ‘X’of this year’s third-rounder Ja’Kobi Lane running routes, posted by JacksonMuse, but originally released on Lane’s Instagram, Hopkins commented, “He a playmaker! Ball in the air it’s his. Looking forward to see him w Lamar.”

This is a bold endorsement from one of the best receivers in NFL history. Especially since Lane has yet to play a competitive down in the pros.

Fortunately, Hopkins’ exciting prediction for Lane is the just the latest example of hype building for the former UCLA stud to quickly become a favorite target for franchise quarterback Lamar Jackson. The Ravens and Jackson need Lane to be an instant impact player at a position still in a state of flux and lacking the big-name star power Hopkins was supposed to provide.

There’s obvious pressure on Lane to live up to Hopkins’ praise. He needs to become the size and speed mismatch and contested-catch merchant missing from the Ravens’ receiver corps.

Neither of those roles will be filled by leading target Zay Flowers. He’s posted back-to-back 1,000-yard seasons, but Flowers is a diminutive speedster who works best from the slot and needs to be moved around more to join the ranks of the game’s elite.

Lane has a different role to play. One where the 6-foot-4, 200-pounder gets to unleash his frame in clutch, sub-package situations, but the Ravens can expand how they use Lane.

First-year offensive coordinator Declan Doyle can design more plays for Lane to be a threat outside the numbers. It’s the same area where Hopkins used to dominate coverage for fun.

Unfortunately, the Ravens weren’t able to squeeze the last bit of greatness from the 34-year-old last season, leaving the team still waiting for a true star receiver to emerge.

Hopkins wasn’t always happy about the way he was deployed by Doyle’s predecessor Todd Monken. His struggles to get Hopkins more involved mirrored similar problems the Ravens had encountered with another marquee name at receiver, Odell Beckham Jr.

Not being able to coax enough production from established stars is likely why Ravens general manager Eric DeCosta believes the draft offered the best chance to end a long-standing problem.

It’s a major gamble on the potential of both Lane and fourth-round pick Elijah Sarratt. He’s another big-bodied receiver already expected to be an immediate playmaker for Jackson.

That’s a lot of faith being placed in two relative unknowns. Particularly for a team expected to be a contender in the AFC and looking to keep its era-defining QB1 happy ahead of his inevitable next bumper payday.

Putting so much emphasis on unproven talent is a risky dynamic DeCosta and the Ravens can still fix by adding a versatile All-Pro during the remainder of free agency.

title: Former top-five pick in 2015 eyes NBA comeback amid free agency
description: A former top-five pick in 2015, Mario Hezonja is looking to make an NBA return. Hezonja last played in the NBA during the 2019-20 season.
fullText:
A former No. 5 overall pick by the Orlando Magic in the 2015 NBA Draft, Mario Hezonja seems intent on making an NBA comeback. According to author and insider Chema de Lucas, Hezonja informed Real Madrid that he will be leaving the club for the NBA.

However, no NBA team has officially signed the Croatian forward. In five NBA seasons between the Magic, Knicks and Trail Blazers, Hezonja averaged 6.9 points, 3.1 rebounds, 1.3 assists and 0.7 steals in 330 games, never living up to the hype of a top-five selection. During the 2025-26 season with Real Madrid, Hezonja averaged 17.5 points, 4.9 rebounds and 1.9 assists in 28 games. Hezonja is still just 31 years old.

This article originally appeared on Rookie Wire: Former top-five pick in 2015 eyes NBA comeback amid free agency

title: Rams’ Davante Adams Gets Strong Words After Former Team Called Out
description: The Rams star has been elite for a long time. The post Rams’ Davante Adams Gets Strong Words After Former Team Called Out appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
Davante Adams is heading into a potentially pivotal second season with the Los Angeles Rams in 2026, and he got some strong comments from a receiver on a different team.

Training camp is set to begin, and there was a point in time this offseason where it seemed that Adams could be lining up in a different uniform this coming season. Instead, he is back to reprise his role as one of reigning MVP Matthew Stafford’s top targets.

His continued excellence is a fitting bit of comeuppance for one of his former teams.

Steelers rookie Germie Bernard is a big fan of Rams star Davante Adams.

Adams’ first season with the Rams is a reminder that he still has what made him a six-time Pro Bowler. He recently drew telling comments from Pittsburgh Steelers rookie Germie Bernard.

“I ain’t going to lie, they had some weak numbers available. So, I’m like, ‘Man, I don’t know. Obviously, I was 5 in college, but JJ [Jalen] Ramsey, he got 5. So, I’m like, ‘Yeah, we not touching that.’ But they have 17 open, and one of my favorite receivers is Davante Adams,” Bernard told NFL defensive tackle Johnathan Hankins on “Big Time Talks” on July 18.

“I was like, ‘I’m going just go with 17, rock out with that.’”

Hankins and Adams were teammates on the Las Vegas Raiders to begin the 2022 season, before the former was traded to the Dallas Cowboys before the deadline. Adams forced a trade to the New York Jets two years later.

Hankins asked Bernard, a Las Vegas native, if he was a fan before Adams’ stint with his home-town team.

Adams began his career with the Green Bay Packers.

The rookie said, “for sure,” and explained that he has been studying Adams’ film “for a long time,” adding, “I don’t go on YouTube and watch his highlights. I go in the film room and watch his highlights. Watch watch games, really, really diving deep into his game.”

Davante Adams is Green Bay Packers general manager Brian Gutekunst’s “biggest mistake.”

Adams’ trade from the Packers to the Jets was the end of an era that started to crumble when Green Bay traded Aaron Rodgers, now with the Steelers, to New York one year earlier.

There remains some consternation about the decision on the wideout two years after his exit.

“Probably the biggest mistake of Gutey’s [general manager Brian Gutekunst] career, I think it’s safe to say, that you let a Hall of Famer walk out the door. I don’t know if they were betting that he was going to go into a decline, the way that Jordy [Nelson] did when he walked out. But Davante was younger,” ESPN Milwaukee’s Gabe Neitzel said on July 17. “Davante, still, when he left the Packers, went off, and has had highly productive season after season.

“He’s been over a 1000 yards every year [since] he left the Packers, except for last year, and he didn’t even play 17 games. Still led the league in touchdowns. Still, a highly productive wide receiver. The dude’s gonna be in Canton someday.”

The one thing Adams is missing from his resume is a Super Bowl berth.

That makes what could be Adams’ final year with the Rams something worth monitoring. The team is the prohibitive Super Bowl betting  favorite, per ESPN’s Doug Greenberg in June.

title: Yankees’ Expected To Sign Big Venezuela Catching Prospect Sebastian Perez
description: The New York Yankees are reportedly “set to sign Sebastian Perez for a $1.7 million signing bonus in the 2027 international drafting class,” states MLB International Reporter Wilber Sanchez, but Francys Romero was the first to report this news back in February. It was also mentioned by Sanchez in February that Perez originally had a […] The post Yankees’ Expected To Sign Big Venezuela Catching Prospect Sebastian Perez appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
The New York Yankees are reportedly “set to sign Sebastian Perez for a $1.7 million signing bonus in the 2027 international drafting class,” states MLB International Reporter Wilber Sanchez, but Francys Romero was the first to report this news back in February. It was also mentioned by Sanchez in February that Perez originally had a deal in place with the Pittsburgh Pirates, but that the deal fell through, also adding that some scouts believe “he’s one of the best catchers in his draft class” and has lots of power and speed that could translate well to the big leagues one day, reported Sanchez.

MLB International Reporter Francys Romero was the first to report that the Yankees were the “expected team to sign Perez” to this deal, with Perez being a catcher out of Venezuela. Romero further added that at this time, back in February, the signing period doesn’t open until January 15th, 2027, and that this is considered to be “the first major agreement since the Yankees found a new international director of scouting”, stated Romero. Romero also added how Perez’s “original deal with the Pirates was for $1.3 million, but Perez’s representatives went back out on the market for a larger bonus deal”, reported Romero.

Romero has also described Perez’s scouting report as similar to Sanchez’s report today, noting that he hits for a lot of power (high exit velocity) and is an “excellent” defender behind the plate. Reporter Wilber Sanchez recently interviewed Perez to gather his thoughts. When discussing what it means to join the Yankees organization, Perez stated, “I feel good, I’m so excited to belong to the Yankees.” Perez even went on to add how he loved this organization growing up, and his goal is to make the Major Leagues within the next couple of years, Perez said.

The Yankees have changed their approach to international scouting and signing in recent months. The Yankees organization had “parted ways” with former director of scouting Danny Rowland, who had been the Yankees’ head director of international scouting for 15 seasons. Rowland signed international players such as Jasson Dominguez, Miguel Andujar, Luis Severino, and Roderick Arias, but there wasn’t a good track record of success in this department.

The Yankees felt it was best to go in a different direction, leading them to promote Mario Garza as the new director of international scouting this past offseason, a move that was officially announced on June 28th via a report from Yankees beat writer Bryan Hoch of MLB.com. Garza has been with the Yankees for up to 16 years and has reportedly worked in a variety of player development and coaching positions with the Yankees in their farm system, reported Hoch. The Yankees have taken a liking to Garza and his strategy as it relates to evaluating talent, as Hoch also reported Yankees General Manager Brian Cashman, when speaking about Garza, stated: “He knows our systems. He already knows all the personnel we want”, Cashman told Hoch.

The Yankees International Scouting Department has gotten to work recently. Not just with this reported deal expected in January, but also with the recent signing of Taiwanese pitcher Chien Fan Lai, who is fresh out of high school, has already reported to the Yankees Dominican Summer League Academy, and is currently in a throwing program. The Yankees’ overhaul in their international scouting department is a change they hope pays dividends 10-15 years down the line as new talent enters the Bronx in the foreseeable future.

title: Spartans move up in latest preseason poll from prominent national analyst
description: See where Michigan State basketball landed in the latest preseason poll from Jon Rothstein of CBS Sports
fullText:
Michigan State men's basketball has moved up in the latest preseason rankings from a prominent national analyst.

The Spartans moved up one spot into the top five nationally in the latest preseason rankings from Jon Rothstein of CBS Sports. Michigan State moved up to No. 5 in Rothstein's latest poll -- which is one spot higher from when I last checked in with Rothstein's preseason poll nearly two weeks ago.

Michigan State moved up thanks to rival Michigan sliding a few spots down. The Spartans are now only behind the following teams in Rothstein's updated rankings: Florida (No. 1), Duke (No. 2), Illinois (No. 3) and UConn (No. 4).

The Spartans return a number of key pieces from last year's team that reached the Sweet 16, including All-American point guard Jeremy Fears Jr. His return has spearheaded a large amount of preseason expectations for the Spartans heading into next season.

title: Seattle Seahawks’ Undrafted Veteran Receives ESPN Recognition
description: The Seattle Seahawks built a championship roster with enough depth to make even their backup fullback worthy of national attention.ESPN’s Bill Barnwell selected Brady Russell as the fullback and tight end on his 2026 All-Backup Team, an exercise that created a complete roster using players projected to begin the season behind starters.Barnwell described Russell as […] The post Seattle Seahawks’ Undrafted Veteran Receives ESPN Recognition appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
The Seattle Seahawks built a championship roster with enough depth to make even their backup fullback worthy of national attention.

ESPN’s Bill Barnwell selected Brady Russell as the fullback and tight end on his 2026 All-Backup Team, an exercise that created a complete roster using players projected to begin the season behind starters.

Barnwell described Russell as an H-back who would sit behind Robbie Ouzts and contribute heavily on special teams while receiving a small offensive role.

He also added a notable qualifier, writing that the selection applies “if he’s on the 2026 roster in Seattle.”

That caveat gives the recognition a useful training camp angle.

Russell already proved his value during the Seahawks’ run to Super Bowl LX, yet his position remains one where teams rarely devote multiple roster spots.

Seattle’s official depth chart lists Ouzts first and Russell second, leaving the veteran’s path tied to his versatility and special teams impact.

SANTA CLARA, CALIFORNIA – JANUARY 03: Brady Russell #38 of the Seattle Seahawks walks out of the tunnel before the game against the San Francisco 49ers at Levi’s Stadium on January 03, 2026 in Santa Clara, California. (Photo by Lachlan Cunningham/Getty Images)

Seattle re-signed Russell on March 12, keeping a player the team described as one of its top special teams contributors and leaders.

The former undrafted free agent led the Seahawks with 14 special teams tackles last season.

Pro Football Reference credited him with 396 special teams snaps, compared with 58 on offense, showing where his primary value came from during the title run.

His production also earned trust well beyond a normal reserve role.

Russell carried the 12 flag while leading the team onto the field during the season and was selected as one of Seattle’s game captains for its Super Bowl victory over the New England Patriots.

Seahawks coach Mike Macdonald said Russell’s energy, toughness and unselfish approach helped establish the standard for the special teams units. Macdonald added that Russell could be considered “one of the best core four combo guys in the league.”

Seattle also needed Russell to handle a larger offensive responsibility when Ouzts missed time.

After Ouzts suffered an ankle injury in Week 3 and landed on injured reserve, the Seahawks said they would turn to Russell at fullback.

Ouzts later was inactive for Super Bowl LX because of a neck injury, again making Russell the available option in the backfield.

Those moments explain why Barnwell viewed him as an ideal reserve.

Russell has experience at tight end but can also line up as an H-back. He has already stepped into the fullback job when injuries changed the depth chart.

SEATTLE, WASHINGTON – JANUARY 25: Brady Russell #38 of the Seattle Seahawks reacts after the end of the second quarter of a game against the Los Angeles Rams in the NFC Championship game at Lumen Field on January 25, 2026 in Seattle, Washington. (Photo by Jane Gershovich/Getty Images)

Ouzts remains the favorite to lead the position after Seattle used the No. 175 overall pick on him in the 2025 NFL Draft.

The former Alabama tight end played 203 offensive snaps as a rookie and gives the Seahawks a 6-foot-3, 274-pound blocker for their backfield packages.

Russell’s offensive workload was far smaller, and he didn’t record a reception on his lone regular-season target.

The roster decision will likely come down to whether Seattle believes Russell’s special teams influence and position flexibility justify keeping two fullbacks again.

His leadership status and performance during the championship season all strengthen that case.

Barnwell’s roster caveat reflects the numbers game and reinforces how narrow Russell’s margin remains.

The ESPN selection also highlights an unusual advantage for the Seahawks.

Few teams have a reserve fullback who played nearly 400 special teams snaps, led the team in tackles in that phase and was trusted as a Super Bowl captain.

So even though Russell enters camp behind Ouzts on offense, his importance to Seattle has already stretched far beyond the second line of the depth chart.

The post Seattle Seahawks’ Undrafted Veteran Receives ESPN Recognition appeared first on HEAVY.

title: $3 Wilt Chamberlain Lakers jacket from 1972 NBA Finals found in thrift store sells for $89K at auction
description: A warmup jacket worn by Wilt Chamberlain during the 1972 NBA Finals that was bought by Quinn Brown was sold for $89,600 at auction on Monday.
fullText:
A warmup jacket worn by Wilt Chamberlain during the 1972 NBA Finals that was bought by a teenager for $3.07 at an Oregon thrift store sold for $89,600 at auction on Monday.

Quinn Brown had been eyeing a massive bin of clothes at a Goodwill store outside Portland in January when he saw someone toss a Lakers jacket with Chamberlain’s name on it back on the pile.

Brown, who resells used clothes online, quickly grabbed it.

The jacket was auctioned by Sotheby’s, which had estimated its value between $150,000 and $250,000 before bidding closed Monday.

It received a total of 48 bids, according to the auction house.

Described on Sotheby’s website as an “extraordinary piece of memorabilia,” the short-sleeved yellow warmup jacket was worn by Chamberlain during the 1972 NBA finals, in which the Los Angeles Lakers beat the New York Knicks.

“I didn’t even think that finding something like this was possible,” Brown said.

Chamberlain, an NBA Hall of Famer viewed as one of the all-time greats, played in the league from 1959 to 1973.

He won two championship rings — one with the Philadelphia 76ers in 1967, and another with the Los Angeles Lakers in 1972.

He holds the record for most points scored by a player in a single game, netting 100 points in a 1962 match.

For the past three years, Brown has been reselling clothes online after finding them in thrift stores.

The most expensive item he had sold previously was a T-shirt of Sub Pop Records, the indie label that signed Nirvana in 1989 and released the band’s first album. It went for $250.

After buying the Chamberlain jacket back in January, Brown searched online to see if it was worth something.

He found a picture on Google of Chamberlain wearing such a jacket and compared it to the one he had.

In examining the Lakers patch on the front, he noticed certain small stitching details that matched the photo.

He also looked up the measurements of a different jersey worn by Chamberlain, a towering figure at 7 feet, 1 inch (2.2 meters) tall, and discovered that they nearly aligned.

“I really just wanted to make sure, because it was so hard to believe at the time,” he said, adding that he had only ever read about such serendipitous thrift findings.

When he posted a photo of the jacket on social media, Sotheby’s contacted him about consigning the jacket to the auction house. Brown agreed, and an armored van came to pick it up.

Sotheby’s then did its own photo verification and determined that the jacket was worn by Chamberlain during the 1972 Finals, in which he was named the Finals MVP.

“Photomatching a piece of game-worn clothing to a specific Finals series, for a player of this era, is genuinely exceptional,” Sotheby’s website says.

“As photomatched items worn by Chamberlain become exceedingly rare, this jacket is a piece of sports history.”

It is not the first Chamberlain item from the 1972 finals to fetch a hefty sum under the hammer: A game jersey he wore during the series sold for $4.9 million at Sotheby’s in 2023, the fourth most expensive NBA jersey ever sold by the auction house.

As for his thrifted find, Brown said: “That was as good as it gets.”

title: ‘It’s About Showing Respect’ — Ex-Ravens DB Sends Stern Message To Jaxson Dart Amid Rising Concerns
description: Last year, when rookie Jaxson Dart made his New York Giants debut, he breathed life into a fanbase in desperate need of a pick-me-up. The 25th overall pick in the first round of the 2025 NFL Draft flashed true playmaking ability.
fullText:
Last year, when rookie Jaxson Dart made his New York Giants debut, he breathed life into a fanbase in desperate need of a pick-me-up. The 25th overall pick in the first round of the 2025 NFL Draft flashed true playmaking ability.

Yet, at times, a reckless play style led to turmoil. For Dart, it meant dealing with concussion symptoms that knocked him out of one game and kept him out of the next two. Now one former NFL defensive back has a message for the Giants’ 23-year-old franchise quarterback.

Over the weekend, Giants running back Cam Skattebo drew criticism at the Fanatics Fest for a failed backflip attempt. The frustrations didn’t stem from the fact that he didn’t complete the acrobatic move successfully; rather, it was because he’s pulling risky stunts away from the NFL field.

That sparked a conversation on ESPN’s “Get Up” program, to which former NFLPA president and current ESPN NFL analyst Domonique Foxworth had some advice for Dart. He says the young QB needs to make better “business decisions” out of respect for his teammates.

But that doesn’t mean eliminating Dart’s trademark aggressive nature on the field that fans have come to love him for.

“I’d be much more concerned about it from a quarterback standpoint than from the running back’s standpoint,” Foxworth said.

“You have to get to a point where Dart understands that it’s not only putting yourself at risk. It’s about showing respect to everybody else on this team.”

BUILD YOUR OWN TOP 100: PFN’s FREE NFL Top 100 Builder

So, how does Dart do that? Foxworth suggests Dart would be better off practicing the mantra of living to see another down. Take what the defense gives you, and learn to trust your teammates.

But Foxworth adds that Dart doesn’t have to change who he is as a player; he can still make plays with his feet, he just needs to be smarter.

BE THE GM OF YOUR FAVORITE TEAM: PFN’s FREE NFL Mock Draft Simulator

“You don’t have to win every play,” Foxworth stressed. “You don’t have to win every single time you touch the ball. There are other people who can make plays, and nobody eats if you’re hurt.

“That’s the responsibility part. The aggressiveness, the linebacker nature of his mindset? That’s not the problem. You gotta be able to make a smart business decision. That’s the challenge, and I think he’ll get there.”

Foxworth is a former defensive back who played seven NFL seasons, including with the Baltimore Ravens from 2009 to 2011 until knee problems ended his career. Now, the former Harvard grad is offering his wisdom to Dart, and he believes the Giants QB will figure it out.

After all, Dart was just a rookie last season. He was still getting used to the size, strength, and speed of the NFL.

It usually takes young players a few seasons to get fully adjusted to making the jump from college to the pros, and Dart had his fair share of learning moments last season. Whether he learns from them and shows “respect” to his teammates by making smarter decisions remains to be seen.

title: Chicago Superstar Expected to Push Recovery Timeline in Latest Injury
description: Connor Bedard injured a shoulder for the second time in about eight months. The first was to his right shoulder in December. He missed nearly a month of the season, and he wasn't quite the same player when he returned. The post Chicago Superstar Expected to Push Recovery Timeline in Latest Injury appeared first on HEAVY.
fullText:
The latest Connor Bedard injury certainly adds some concern for fans, hoping to avoid yet another dismal season in the Windy City. Now, the Chicago Blackhawks’ newly extended franchise center is expected to push hard to beat a four-month recovery timeline for his latest shoulder injury. That update is according to Bedard’s agent, per reporting from Ben Pope of the Chicago-Sun Times.

Days ago, Bedard signed a new five-year contract worth $15 million annually that buys out one year of unrestricted free agency. Bedard spoke on a myriad of topics Monday afternoon, including reaffirming his commitment to Chicago. That commitment extends to his desire to get back on the ice as quickly as possible.

Connor Bedard injured a shoulder for the second time in about eight months. The first was to his right shoulder in December. He missed nearly a month of the season, and he wasn’t quite the same player when he returned. Bedard injured his left shoulder during off-season training, which required surgery.

Bedard’s agent, Don Meehan, reportedly told Chicago General Manager Kyle Davidson to expect Bedard to want to suit up faster than his currently estimated four-month recovery timeline.

‘‘As I said to Kyle: ‘You better be prepared. After 3½ months, he’ll be in your office, and he’s not going to be a very positive guy if you don’t give him clearance to play. He’ll be miserable to live with,’ ’’ Meehan said. ‘‘[Kyle] laughed and said: ‘I understand that all too well. I remember the jaw [injury in early 2024].’

‘‘[Connor is] absolutely determined. He’s been talking about a very aggressive form of rehab. There’s no reservations about him being totally committed to…being back as soon as possible.’’

Chicago isn’t likely to be a popular pick to make the playoffs. Even with the additions of Bowen Byram and Ian Cole on the blue line, Chicago has a heavy hill to climb. The Blackhawks remain in the business of projecting how young players will improve year-over-year. The Hawks organization is counting on improvement from Bedard, Frank Nazar, Oliver Moore and many other young players. That only becomes harder with Bedard sidelined for months.

Simultaneously, the Hawks have a big bet on former Kontinental Hockey League standout Roman Kantserov. Fans hope the smallish winger will live up to names like Artemi Panarin, Kirill Marchenko and Kirill Kaprizov. Between his youth and small stature, Kantserov isn’t a lock to be any type of immediate boost.

Kantserov becoming a major contributor will be harder with Connor Bedard’s four month recovery timeline. That will cost him some conditioning, training camp, pre-season and part of the regular season. Building chemistry between Kantserov and Bedard is critical this season, and now it will have to wait.

Bedard didn’t look like the same player after suffering a shoulder injury in December. Upon returning in January, Bedard still put up points, finishing with 31 in 38 games. But the Hawks went 11-20-7 in that stretch. Bedard was deeply underwater in basically every five-on-five metric, including Corsi (42.3 percent), Fenwick (41.5 percent), shot differential (40.7 percent) and high-danger chances (31.9 percent). Critically, Bedard was minus-16 in five-on-five goals after returning from injury and just a 36.6 percent rating for Expected Goals, per Natural Stat Trick.

The numbers can make your eyes glaze over, but they paint a simple truth. Bedard was across-the-board better by every statistic before getting hurt. He scored 44 points in his first 31 games, was plus-four at five-on-five and the Hawks were much closer to respectable at 13-12-6.

Simply put, the latest Connor Bedard injury needs to be fully resolved before Chicago should feel comfortable letting him back on the ice.

The Blackhawks need a healthy Bedard to even sniff competitiveness this season. Certainly, there will be questions about how quickly he can get up to speed once he does return.

Chicago received good news as well. Doctors do not believe that the two shoulder injuries Bedard has now suffered in just eight months are linked, Pope reported. In other words, both injuries were unpleasant and disappointing. Yet, neither of them should be a long-term concern.

Bedard has received a surprising amount of criticism early in his career. It’s likely in part because Chicago has been so uncompetitive in his three seasons there. The Hawks consecutive streak of generating the league-worst in expected goals, per Natural Stat Trick, has been part of their struggle. For Bedard’s part, he’s scored 75 goals and 128 assists for 203 points in his first 219 games.

The counting stats are there, and Bedard has shown flashes of being more than just a scorer. Whether he lives up to his potential — and stays healthy enough to do it — could come down to some of Kyle Davidson’s team-building hunches.
